results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (identified by a unique number) against the respondent State lodged with the former human rights commission (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Human Rights Convention (“the Convention”) by four nationals of the respondent State, individuals A, B, C and D (“the applicants”), on 29 November 1996.

The applicants were represented by a legal professional practising in the capital city. The State's representatives (“the Government”) did not designate an Agent for the purposes of the proceedings before the Convention institutions.

The applicants alleged that their case, which commenced in 1989 and terminated in 1996, was not heard within a reasonable time as required by the Convention.

The application was transmitted to the international human rights tribunal on 1 November 1998, when Protocol No. 11 to the Convention came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

The application was allocated to the Second Section of the tribunal (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of the tribunal). Within that Section, the Chamber that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

On 1 November 2001 the tribunal changed the composition of its Sections (Rule 25 § 1). This case was assigned to the newly composed First Section (Rule 52 § 1).

By a decision of 15 May 2003, the tribunal declared the application admissible.

The applicants and the State's representatives each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1).

THE FACTS

In September 1980 the applicants were taken into police custody and were subsequently placed in detention on remand on suspicion of membership of an illegal organisation. A military public prosecutor filed a bill of indictment with a regional martial law court (its designation in the local language) charging the applicants under Article 146 of the Criminal Code with membership of an illegal organisation whose object was to undermine the constitutional order.

On various dates between 1982 and 1984 the applicants were released pending trial.

On 13 September 1988 a regional martial law court acquitted the applicants of the charges against them.

On various dates in March, April and June 1989 the applicants brought individual actions before a regional assize court against the national treasury, pursuant to Law no. 466. They requested compensation for their unjustified detention on remand.

On 20 October 1989 the court decided to join the applicants’ cases.

On 15 December 1993 a regional assize court awarded non‑pecuniary compensation to the applicants. The applicants appealed, arguing that the amount of compensation awarded was not sufficient.

On 31 January 1995 the national supreme court of appeal quashed the judgment of the first instance court.

On 6 June 1995 a regional assize court increased the amount of non‑pecuniary compensation.

On 15 June 1995 the applicants again appealed.

On 30 May 1996 the national supreme court of appeal dismissed the applicants’ appeal and upheld the judgment of the regional assize court.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in two applications (referred to as Application A and Application B) against a Contracting State lodged with the former international human rights adjudication body (hereinafter “the initial body”) under a former Article of the foundational human rights treaty (hereinafter “the Treaty”) by two nationals of the respondent State: the first applicant (whose application was introduced on an early date in that year) and the second applicant (whose application was introduced on a later date in that year).

The respondent Government (hereinafter “the authorities”) were represented by their Agent, a representative from a national government department.

The applicants alleged that the proceedings in which they were sued in respect of property in another jurisdiction were not dealt with efficiently or without undue delay.

The applications were subsequently transmitted to the international judicial body (hereinafter “the Court”) later that year, when a relevant protocol to the Treaty came into force (Article 5 § 2 of a relevant Protocol).

The applications were allocated to a specific section of the Court (Rule 52 § 1 of its internal rules). Within that section, the Chamber that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Treaty) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

On a later date, the Court changed the composition of its sections (Rule 25 § 1). This case was assigned to a newly composed section (Rule 52 § 1).

By a decision on a subsequent date, the Court declared the applications partly admissible, having in the interim joined them some months prior.

The applicants and the authorities each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1).

THE FACTS

THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicants were born in the late 1920s and early 1920s respectively, a few years apart. The first applicant (in Application A) had a professional background; the second applicant (in Application B) worked in an administrative capacity. They resided in a location in the country.

The applicants, together with the first applicant’s spouse, were sued by a third party (hereinafter “the claimant”), who had previously been under the first applicant's professional care, in respect of property in another jurisdiction. Legal proceedings were initiated in the mid-1980s. Before the action came to trial, the claimant was adjudicated bankrupt some years later and the trustee in bankruptcy continued the action.

The trial was scheduled to commence in a national court in mid-year, but was postponed until the end of the following month because the presiding official was not available for the full duration required for the hearing.

On a subsequent day of trial, while the claimant was being cross-examined, an adjournment was granted because the claimant reported feeling unwell. In the event, it emerged that no medical justification could be given for the claimant's continued non-attendance, but nonetheless the claimant remained absent for the rest of the trial. Because of the delay caused by the claimant’s non-attendance, the trial could not be concluded in late that year and had to be adjourned until early the following year. Judgment was given some months later.

The presiding official accepted the argument of the applicants and the first applicant’s spouse that it was necessary to disregard the claimant’s evidence because of the claimant's failure to remain for cross-examination. However, on the basis of the documentary evidence and that adduced by the applicants and the first applicant’s spouse, the presiding official found that the transfer of the overseas property should be set aside on the ground that the defendants had not been able to rebut the presumption of undue influence created by the discrepancy in the price they paid the claimant for the property and its value at the time of the transfer, together with the previous professional relationship between the first applicant and the claimant.

The formal record of the presiding official’s order was not prepared by the parties until late that year. The applicants and the first applicant’s spouse applied to an appellate court for leave to appeal shortly thereafter. The appeal bundles were lodged almost a year later. Early the next year the appeal was listed for hearing, and it was heard in spring of that year, on which last date the appellate court gave judgment dismissing the appeal.

In connection with the moneys paid by the first applicant for the property, the appellate court confirmed the decision of the initial trial judge that the sum should be repaid, but that no interest should be paid on it. The initial trial judge had based the decision on the fact that the first applicant “had had possession of the properties and receipt of the rents since the completion of the sale, subject only to the agreement to allow the claimant to use one of the units in the early months”. A senior judge regarded this as a “rough and ready result”, but one which would “reduce the opportunities for further dispute between the parties”.

A few days later, the applicants and the first applicant’s spouse were refused leave by the appellate court to appeal to the highest national court of appeal. Approximately a year later, the applicants and the first applicant’s spouse were refused leave to appeal by the highest national court of appeal.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. [APPLICATION_NUMBER]) against the Republic of Turkey lodged with a regional human rights commission under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (hereinafter referred to as "the Convention") by Turkish nationals, the first applicant and the second applicant, on a date in late May.

The applicants, who had been granted legal aid, were represented by their legal professional, a legal professional practising in Izmir. The Turkish Government (hereinafter referred to as "the respondent state's representatives") did not designate an Agent for the purpose of the proceedings before the regional judicial body.

On a date in late March of a subsequent year, the regional judicial body decided to communicate the application to the respondent state's representatives. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicants were born in their early twenties and mid-twenties respectively at the time of the initial events.

In mid-August of that year, the applicants were taken into custody by police officers from a specialized police unit focusing on terrorism, operating under the regional security directorate in Aydın, on suspicion of their membership to an illegal organisation, namely the DHKP/C.

In their police statements the applicants accepted the accusations against them.

A few days later in August of the same year, the applicants were brought before the judicial official overseeing the investigation at a local judicial body in Aydın, where they repeated the statements they had made to the police. The judicial official overseeing the investigation ordered that they be placed in detention on remand.

In an indictment dated in early September of that year, the prosecuting authority at a specialized court in Izmir initiated criminal proceedings against the applicants. He charged the first applicant with being member of an armed gang under Article 168 § 2 of the Criminal Code and the second applicant with aiding and abetting an armed gang under Article 169 of the Criminal Code.

During the hearings before the specialized court in Izmir, the applicants denied the statements they had made to the police and the judicial official overseeing the investigation.

On a date in late March, a specialized court in Izmir, composed of two non-military judicial officials and a military judicial official, convicted the applicants as charged and sentenced the first applicant to several years' imprisonment and the second applicant to a significant period of over a decade's imprisonment. Neither the applicants nor their legal counsel were present in this last hearing.

The applicants appealed. In mid-November of that year, the Court of Cassation dismissed the applicants’ appeal, upholding the specialized court in Izmir's reasoning and assessment of evidence. The decision, which was pronounced in the absence of the applicants and their legal counsel, was deposited with the registry of the specialized court in Izmir later that month.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a specific case number) against a certain Member State lodged with a judicial body under Article 34 of a particular international human rights instrument (“the said instrument”) by a national of that state, the individual concerned, in late 2010.

The individual concerned, who had been granted legal aid, was represented by a legal representative practising in a city in that region. The national authorities (“the authorities”) were represented by their Agent, a representative, of a relevant government department.

The individual concerned alleged, in particular, a breach of Article 3 of the said instrument on account of the imposition on them of an enhanced security classification regime.

In mid-2014 the application was communicated to the authorities.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The individual concerned was born in the late 1980s and is detained in a detention facility in that region.

A. Criminal proceedings against the individual concerned

In late 2005 the individual concerned was arrested and remanded in custody. The following year they were convicted of several serious offenses involving violence and theft and received prison sentences ranging from varying periods. In late November of the following year a regional judicial body imposed a cumulative sentence for a number of criminal convictions.

B. Imposition of an enhanced security classification regime

In early November of a later year a penitentiary commission at a detention facility imposed on the individual concerned an enhanced security classification regime. The commission held that the individual concerned had played a significant role in a concerted act of defiance among the incarcerated population at that facility (pursuant to relevant legal regulations), as the incarcerated individuals refused to accept sustenance provided by the custodial staff. The staff learned that the incarcerated individuals were planning another concerted act of defiance shortly thereafter. The commission also considered that the individual concerned was particularly intractable.

In early 2011 the same penitentiary commission rejected an appeal lodged by the individual concerned and dismissed their request for leave to examine the appeal outside the statutory time-limit.

Subsequently, the individual concerned was transferred to another detention facility in the region.

In early February of that year the penitentiary commission at the new facility reviewed and upheld the decision to apply the regime to the individual concerned, considering that they posed a serious threat to the security of the detention centre. The individual concerned appealed.

In early April of that year the regional judicial body dismissed the appeal. The judicial body referred to the reasons given in the decision of early November of the previous year, holding that the decision had been lawful and justified.

In early March of that year the individual concerned lodged a complaint with a regional oversight body for correctional facilities. They complained that they were experiencing challenges in participating in institutional programs at the detention facility. The authorities dismissed the complaint, holding that rehabilitative programs were not typically offered to prisoners under that classification.

On an unspecified date in that year the individual concerned notified law enforcement of their allegedly inappropriate application of an enhanced security classification made in early November of the previous year. They claimed that the staff at the detention facility mentioned earlier had exceeded their powers in imposing the regime on them. In late April of that year a district prosecuting authority refused to open an investigation in the case, holding that no offence had been committed.

In late April of that year the penitentiary commission at the new facility revoked its decision to classify the individual concerned under the enhanced security classification. It considered that the individual concerned’s behaviour had improved and that they no longer posed a threat to the security of the detention facility.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a particular application number) against the respondent State lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a national of the respondent State, the applicant, early in a particular year.

The applicant, who had been granted legal aid, was represented by his legal representative, a lawyer practising in a city in the region. The respondent Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, a representative of the relevant government department, based in the capital city.

The applicant alleged that his criminal proceedings exceeded a reasonable time contrary to Article 6 § 1 of the Convention.

The application was allocated to a particular section of the Court (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of Court). Within that section, the chamber that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

Mid-year in a subsequent period, the Court rejected part of the application as inadmissible.

Later that year, the Court changed the composition of its sections (Rule 25 § 1). This case was assigned to the newly composed specific section.

By a final decision early in a later year, the Court declared the application partly admissible.

The applicant and the Government each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1). The chamber decided, after consulting the parties, that no hearing on the merits was required (Rule 59 § 3 in fine).

THE FACTS

The applicant was born in the mid-1960s and is currently serving a sentence of imprisonment in a detention facility in a specific region.

The applicant was arrested on a charge of attempted murder arising out of a high speed car chase which occurred in the early hours of late October of a specific year. The following day, he was arrested again, interviewed and cautioned in respect of a separate incident of rape and assault which also occurred during the early morning of late October of that year.

Two days after the initial incident, the applicant appeared in court in respect of the various road traffic offences and the attempted murder of a police officer. A week later, he was fully committed and remanded in custody on those charges (of which he was later acquitted).

Later that month, the applicant appeared before the Sheriff and was committed in respect of the rape charge.

Mid-year in the subsequent period, the trial began in the higher court. A few days later, the applicant was convicted, by a majority verdict, of all charges and sentenced to a substantial period of imprisonment.

Shortly after the conviction, the applicant lodged an intention to appeal with the higher court sitting as an appeal court. A month later, a Note of Appeal was lodged.

In late September of that year, a single judge refused the appeal. The applicant appealed.

Late October that year, the Note of Appeal was allowed by three judges in restricted terms relating to the direction given by the trial judge on the special defence of alibi.

Late in that year, the appeal was set down to be heard. According to the Government, it did not take place as the applicant refused to leave prison. The applicant stated that contrary to statutory authority the prison governor refused to allow him to leave the prison wearing civilian clothes instead of a prison uniform for the hearing. A further hearing was fixed for mid-year in the next period. On that date, the Crown requested, and obtained, an adjournment as the Advocate Depute was engaged abroad.

Late in the same year, the appeal was set down to be heard. It was discontinued due to lack of court time and pressure of other business. It was noted that the appeal was complex and would last a significant period.

In mid-January of a subsequent year, there was another appeal hearing. As the applicant's solicitor had become aware that the prosecution had never cited a witness to appear at the trial, he had lodged and argued an additional ground of appeal on this point. Following argument, he sought to lodge further additional grounds of appeal. The appeal was continued to a month later to enable him to do so.

On that date, the higher court held that the failure to hear the evidence of the witness resulted in unfairness and that further evidence should be heard. The designated judge was designated as the judge to hear the evidence. Several months later, he fixed a date to take the evidence of the witness. Later that summer, the hearing was adjourned as the applicant's counsel withdrew from acting on his behalf.

In mid-October of that year, the witness gave evidence. In his note a month later, the designated judge gave his view of the credibility and reliability of the witness. The appeal was relisted for hearing early in the next year but the applicant's solicitors wrote to the court requesting that another date, possibly in the following week, be fixed due to the unavailability of counsel.

Mid-year in a subsequent period, the appeal was heard before the higher court.

A month later, the judgment of the court was given by the Lord Justice General, the presiding judge. It dismissed the applicant's appeal.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in a specific application number against the Republic of Turkey lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Cypriot national, the applicant, in mid-1997.

The applicant was represented by the applicant's legal representative, a lawyer practising in a city in the region. The Turkish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, the Agent for the respondent Government.

The applicant alleged that the Turkish occupation of the northern part of Cyprus had prevented him from having access to his home and properties.

The application was transmitted to the Court in late 1998, when Protocol No. 11 to the Convention came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

By a decision of mid-1999 the Court declared the application admissible.

The applicant and the Government each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1). In addition, third-party comments were received from the Government of Cyprus, which had exercised its right to intervene (Article 36 § 1 of the Convention and Rule 44 § 1 (b)).

THE FACTS

The applicant was born in the mid-1950s and lives in a city in the region.

The applicant claimed that until mid-1974 he used to live with his family in a house belonging to his parents in a village in the northern region of the island, located within a district in the northern region of the island. In mid-1974, as the Turkish army was advancing, the applicant and his family had been forced to leave the village. Ever since, they have not been able to return to the applicant’s parents’ house.

In early 1990 the applicant’s parents donated half of their house to the applicant. They also donated to him half of three other houses, two shops and a number of plots of land and water installations in the aforementioned village. The remaining half of the properties mentioned above was transferred to the applicant’s sister. The transfers at issue were recorded in the Land Registry of the Republic of Cyprus.

The applicant produced the relevant affirmations of ownership issued by the Republic of Cyprus. According to these documents, the applicant’s properties could be described as follows: (a) a plot of land described by cadastral details, consisting of an olive grove, area 2,282 sq. m; (b) two plots of land described by cadastral details, area: 3,862 sq. m and 3,405 sq. m respectively; (c) several plots of land described by cadastral details, area: 1,053 sq. m, 1,024 sq. m, 1,769 sq. m and 1,405 sq. m respectively; (d) two plots of land described by cadastral details, consisting of lemon plantations, area: 5,219 sq. m and 1,469 sq. m respectively; (e) a plot of land described by cadastral details, consisting of a lemon plantation with bore hole, area: 4,877 sq. m; (f) several plots of land described by cadastral details, consisting of a lemon plantation with two wells and one ground storey residence, area: 7,032 sq. m; (g) a plot of land described by cadastral details, consisting of a lemon plantation, area: 1,018 sq. m; (h) a plot of land described by cadastral details, consisting of a tank of an area of 37 sq. m with a freshwater spring; (i) several plots of land described by cadastral details, consisting of a lemon plantation (area: 2,358 sq. m) with a bore hole and one tank (area: 84 sq. m); on this property had been constructed: a ground storey residence of an area of 152.25 sq. m; two shops of an area of 56 sq. m; two semidetached ground storey residences (areas: 152.25 sq. m and 63 sq. m respectively); one residence of an area of 219.41 sq. m; (j) several plots of land described by cadastral details, referring to a right to use a freshwater spring.

The applicant alleged that the Turkish occupation of northern Cyprus had prevented him from using and/or enjoying his properties.

Specific names like 'Lars Wassdahl' and 'M. Falk' have been removed and are not inferable from the rewritten text.

judge_leaks.rationale: The original date was '22 July 1996'. Similar to the preliminary consideration, the relative expression 'in the summer of that year', when anchored by 'mid-2000', strongly implies 'summer 1999'.

judge_leaks.rationale: The rewritten text explicitly states 'Early 2002', which is a direct, specific temporal leak matching the original year '2002'.

judge_leaks.attribute: Date administrative court of appeal upheld judgment
judge_leaks.rationale: The original date was '15 April 2002'. The rewritten text states 'By judgment in spring of that year' referring to the year of 'Early 2002', which strongly implies 'spring 2002'.

judge_leaks.attribute: Date applicant appealed to supreme administrative court

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (Case No. XXXX/YY) against the respondent State lodged with the European human rights court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the human rights convention”) by a national of that country, the applicant, in late 2003.

The Government of the respondent State (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, a representative from the Ministry for Foreign Affairs.

About two years later, the court declared the application partly inadmissible and decided to communicate the complaint concerning the length of the administrative proceedings to the Government. Applying Article 29 § 3 of the convention, it decided to rule on the admissibility and merits of the application at the same time.

THE FACTS

THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the mid-1940s and lives in a locality in the country.

In late spring of that year, the taxation authority (the national taxation body) of a regional administrative division sent a preliminary consideration (övervägande) to the applicant informing him that it was considering disallowing the deduction he had made for the interest he had paid on debts in the amount of a substantial sum in local currency, as well as his declared capital gains for the sale of stocks in the amount of a significant amount, that he had made in his tax return for a prior tax period. Further, it was considering imposing tax surcharges (skattetillägg) amounting to a further substantial sum (approximately an equivalent sum in foreign currency), i.e. a significant percentage of the increased tax liability on the sum of a related sum.

The preliminary consideration was based on an investigation of a business entity which had done business with a substantial number of private persons. As concerned the applicant, it appeared that he had borrowed a very large sum in local currency from the entity and commissioned it to buy stocks for the same amount on his behalf. The applicant then sold the stocks back to the entity at a fixed date, upon which he had made a capital gain. At the same time, he repaid the loan together with interest to the entity. As the capital gain corresponded to the interest, they were set off against each other. In the taxation authority’s view, the transactions had in reality never taken place and the applicant had simply received two statements of account and a promissory note in order to use them to obtain tax advantages.

The applicant was requested to submit any comments he might have to the taxation authority in the summer of that year, which he did. He claimed that the transactions had taken place and that his tax return should be approved.

In the autumn of that year, the taxation authority decided to follow its preliminary consideration. With regard to the imposition of tax surcharges, it found that the applicant had submitted incorrect information about the purported transactions.

Early the following year, the applicant appealed against the decision, disputing the taxation authority’s findings. He maintained that there were no grounds for disallowing the deductions or imposing tax surcharges on him.

Some months later, the taxation authority made its obligatory re-assessment of its decision from the autumn of the preceding year but upheld it. It then forwarded the appeal to the regional administrative court (the regional court) of another regional administrative division.

In mid-2000, the regional administrative court rejected the applicant’s appeal after having held an oral hearing. It agreed with the taxation authority’s conclusion that no transactions had in reality taken place between the applicant and the business entity, for which reason they could not be included in his tax return. Moreover, the court found that the applicant had submitted incorrect information to the taxation authority and that there were no grounds on which to remit the tax surcharges.

Shortly after, the applicant appealed to the administrative court of appeal (the appellate court) in a provincial city, maintaining his claims. Early 2002 he made further submissions to the court, stating inter alia that his rights under Article 6 of the convention had been violated as he had not had access to the same material as the taxation authority, and since the tax surcharges had been incorrectly imposed on him.

By judgment in spring of that year, the administrative court of appeal upheld the lower court’s judgment in full, without giving any new reasons of its own.

A month later, the applicant appealed to the supreme administrative court (the highest administrative court), relying on the same grounds as before the lower courts and expanding them further.

In late summer of the following year, the supreme administrative court refused leave to appeal.

Later that year, the applicant sued the respondent State, through its legal representative, before the district court (the local court) in the capital city. He sought a large sum in local currency (approximately an equivalent sum in foreign currency) in damages on the grounds that the taxation authority and the administrative courts had not afforded him a fair procedure, having withheld certain information from him, and that the proceedings had been of excessive length. He invoked national legislation as well as the convention and its case-law.

The respondent State contested the suit, claiming that it had not caused the applicant any injury, either by fault or neglect, for which he could claim damages.

In a judgment towards the end of the next year, the district court rejected the applicant’s claims. As concerned the length of the proceedings, the court considered that the administrative courts’ examination of his tax case had not taken so long as to incur the respondent State's liability for damages.

A few days later, the applicant appealed to the court of appeal (the appellate court), maintaining his claims.

The following year, the court of appeal upheld the lower court’s judgment in full. With regard to the complaint about the length of the proceedings, the court, without giving any reasons, found that the tax proceedings could not be considered to have been of excessive duration.

Towards the end of that year, the applicant appealed to the supreme court (the highest court) where the case is still pending, awaiting a decision on leave to appeal.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a specific application number) against the United Kingdom lodged with the adjudicating body under Article 34 of the relevant international human rights treaty ("the treaty") by a national of that country, the claimant, on a date in early March of a certain year.

The claimant, who had been granted legal aid, was represented by their legal representative, legal counsel practising in the capital city. The Government of the respondent State ("the Government") were represented by their agent from a specific governmental department in the capital city.

The claimant, serving a sentence of mandatory life imprisonment, alleged that the Parole Board did not provide the guarantees and procedures required by Article 5 § 4 when examining whether they should be released and that they had no enforceable right to compensation for any breach of their rights as required by Article 5 § 5.

The application was allocated to a particular section of the adjudicating body (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of the adjudicating body). Within that section, the specific panel that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the treaty) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

By a decision issued in late September of the following year, the adjudicating body declared the remainder of application admissible.

The claimant filed observations on just satisfaction and the Government replied (Rule 59 § 1).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The claimant was born in the late 1950s and is serving a sentence of life imprisonment in a correctional facility in a northern region.

The claimant was convicted in or around the early 1980s for the murder of an acquaintance. They were sentenced to mandatory life imprisonment, and their tariff (the minimum period of imprisonment required to satisfy the requirements of retribution and deterrence) was set at a period of just over a decade.

At the time of the claimant's latest Parole Board review in the early 2000s, they were detained in a different correctional facility as a “Category C” prisoner (prisoners being given a security category classification ranging from Category A (highest risk) to Category D (suitable for open conditions)). The Parole Board recommended that the claimant should be transferred to open conditions, and that their detention should be reviewed after a two-year span. By letter dated early July of that same year the Secretary of State informed the prison administration body that he did not accept the Parole Board's recommendation for transfer to open conditions, and that the next review should begin after a twelve-month period. In the letter to the claimant which accompanied the decision it was stated that: “... [t]he Secretary of State attaches particular weight to the psychologist's report, it has highlighted in some detail, the need for further exploration of your insight into, and responsibility for, the index offence and the apparent lack of empathy towards the victim.”

Around mid-2001 the claimant was transferred to another correctional facility in a different part of the country. They claimed that they did not receive notification of the parole decision until sometime after mid-September of that year. The Government stated that a copy of the decision letter was sent to the claimant on early July of that year and a copy to the legal counsel then acting for the claimant on early August of that same year. When the claimant changed their legal counsel in September of that year, the authorities sent a copy of the decision to the new legal counsel.

The claimant stated that they wanted to seek judicial review of the decision, but were unable to find legal counsel who was prepared to act on their behalf. Their then legal counsel gave advice that judicial review stood little prospect of success as the reports recommending a move to open conditions had been slightly ambiguous, there were a number of very unfavourable reports and the Secretary of State had given extensive reasons for rejecting the recommended move.

The claimant's first Parole Board oral hearing under new arrangements was scheduled for late in a subsequent year. At the claimant's request, it was deferred until in mid-year of the following year to allow the claimant time to complete certain courses identified in the reports for the Parole Board review.
results.final_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in a specific application number against a particular European state lodged with the international judicial body under Article 34 of the relevant human rights convention (“the Convention”) by a national of that state, the applicant, on a specific date in the early part of the relevant period of proceedings.

The applicant, who had been granted legal aid, was represented by a legal representative, a solicitor practising in a major city. The national authorities (“the authorities”) were represented by their Agent, a representative of the national authorities of the national foreign affairs office, in a major city.

The applicant, serving a sentence of mandatory life imprisonment, alleged that the review board did not provide the guarantees and procedures required by Article 5 § 4 when examining whether he should be released and that he had no enforceable right to compensation for any breach of his rights as required by Article 5 § 5.

The application was allocated to a specific section of the judicial body (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of Court). Within that Section, the division of the judicial body that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

By a decision made in late autumn of a subsequent year, the international judicial body declared the remainder of application admissible.

The applicant filed observations on just satisfaction and the national authorities replied (Rule 59 § 1).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the late 1950s and is serving a sentence of life imprisonment in a specific correctional facility.

The applicant was convicted in or around the early 1980s for the murder of an acquaintance. He was sentenced to mandatory life imprisonment, and his tariff (the minimum period of imprisonment required to satisfy the requirements of retribution and deterrence) was set at a period exceeding ten years.

At the time of the applicant's latest review board review around the turn of the millennium, he was detained in a specific correctional facility as a “Category C” prisoner (prisoners being given a security category classification ranging from Category A (highest risk) to Category D (suitable for open conditions)). The review board recommended that the applicant should be transferred to open conditions, and that his detention should be reviewed after a period of several years. By letter dated in mid-summer of that year the responsible minister informed the correctional authorities that he did not accept the review board's recommendation for transfer to open conditions, and that the next review should begin after a period of one year. In the letter to the applicant which accompanied the decision it was stated that: “... [t]he responsible minister attaches particular weight to the psychologist's report, it has highlighted in some detail, the need for further exploration of your insight into, and responsibility for, the index offence and the apparent lack of empathy towards the victim.”

In early summer of that year the applicant was transferred to a specific correctional facility. He claimed that he did not receive notification of the parole decision until sometime after early autumn of that same year. The national authorities stated that a copy of the decision letter was sent to the applicant in mid-summer of that year and a copy to the solicitors then acting for the applicant in mid-summer of that same year. When the applicant changed his solicitors in early autumn of that same year, the authorities sent a copy of the decision to the new solicitors.

The applicant stated that he wanted to seek judicial review of the decision, but was unable to find a solicitor who was prepared to act on his behalf. His then solicitors gave advice that judicial review stood little prospect of success as the reports recommending a move to open conditions had been slightly ambiguous, there were a number of very unfavourable reports and the responsible minister had given extensive reasons for rejecting the recommended move.

The applicant's first review board oral hearing under new arrangements was scheduled for late autumn of a subsequent year. At the applicant's request, it was deferred until late spring of the year following to allow the applicant time to complete certain courses identified in the reports for the review board review.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a particular case number) against a particular country lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a national of that country, the claimant, on a particular date.

The claimant was until a certain date represented by one legal representative and thereafter by another legal representative, both legal professionals practising in a major city. The national government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, the agent for the government, from a governmental department responsible for external relations.

The claimant alleged, in particular, that his rights under Article 6 of the Convention had been violated on account of the lack of an oral hearing before an administrative appellate court in proceedings concerning social security benefits.

The application was transmitted to the international court at a specific time later on, when Protocol No. 11 to the Convention came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

The application was allocated to a specific section of the international court (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of the international court). Within that Section, a specific section that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

By a decision of a later date the international court declared the application partly admissible.

At another point in time the international court changed the composition of its Sections (Rule 25 § 1). This case was assigned to a newly composed section.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

On an early date the claimant, who was born in the late 1910s, applied for social security benefits due to health circumstances under the Social Insurance Act (from a specific legislative period, hereinafter “the relevant social insurance legislation”). He claimed that he was in need of assistance and had additional costs due to a particular health condition that arose after a medical procedure undertaken in the late 1970s.

By a decision issued on a subsequent date the local social insurance agency (“the agency”) of a regional administrative area rejected the application, finding that the claimant’s needs or costs were not such as to make him eligible for social security benefits due to health circumstances.

Some years later the claimant requested the agency to review its decision under chapter 20, section 10 a of the relevant social insurance legislation. He claimed that he had failed to fully state his need of assistance and his additional costs in the original application. A few months after that he asked the agency, alternatively, to consider his request as a new application and to grant him benefits as from late in an earlier year.

On a specific date the agency found that there was no basis for a review of its earlier decision. Further, in regard to the claimant’s alternative claim, the agency noted that the claimant had reached retirement age in the mid-1980s and that information relating to the period that followed could not be taken into account under the relevant rules. Consequently, the claimant’s alternative claim was rejected.

The claimant appealed to a regional administrative court in a regional administrative area. On a particular date the court gave judgment in his favour. It considered that the agency’s first decision had been based on insufficient information and that the agency should therefore have reviewed that decision at the claimant’s request. It noted that, according to two medical certificates in the case, the claimant had considerable additional costs due to his health-related limitations. Consequently, he was entitled to social security benefits due to health circumstances as long as his need of assistance and his additional costs, taken together, attained the level required under the relevant social insurance legislation. Accepting the information submitted by the claimant in this regard, the court found that that level had been attained and granted him a health allowance as from mid-1986. The court did not hold an oral hearing nor, apparently, did the claimant request one.

The national social insurance authority (“the authority”) appealed against the regional administrative court’s judgment to an administrative appellate court in a major city, requesting that the agency’s decision of a specific date be confirmed. The authority claimed that the information in the case showed that, before retirement age, the claimant had not suffered significant functional limitations to such an extent that his need of assistance and extra expenses on account thereof had made him eligible for a health allowance. Thus, the agency’s first decision could not be considered to have been incorrect due to it being based on incorrect or incomplete material. Consequently, there were no grounds for a review under the relevant social insurance legislation. The authority further requested the appellate court to stay the enforcement of the appealed judgment. The latter request was granted by a decision issued late in that year.

The claimant made submissions in response to the authority’s appeal. Early the following year he requested the administrative appellate court to hold an oral hearing in the case during which the person in charge of the case at the agency should give evidence relating to the meaning of its decision and the information on which it had been based. The claimant also asked the court to obtain the opinion of a medical professional.

Later that year the administrative appellate court rejected both requests. In regard to the request for an oral hearing, the court, after having restated section 9 of the Administrative Court Procedure Act (from a specific legislative period; see further paragraph 23 below), gave the following reasons: “Having regard to the subject-matter at issue and the information that has come to hand in the case, [the court] finds that an oral hearing is unnecessary and rejects the request to that effect. [The claimant] is invited to state the further circumstances he wishes to invoke and submit his final written observations in the case within a short period after having been notified of this decision. The case can be determined notwithstanding a failure to submit such written observations.”

The claimant and the authority made further submissions. The claimant maintained that the authority had failed to adequately explain the reasons for its appeal and posed several questions to the authority. He requested that the court order the authority to clarify its position in the case. The court did not do so, however. The claimant’s submissions were, however, forwarded to the authority which replied that it maintained its position in the case. On account of the court’s refusal to order the authority to clarify its position and to hold a hearing, the claimant called into question the impartiality of the proceedings. On two separate occasions in the mid-1990s he reiterated his request for an oral hearing, stating that a hearing would give him the opportunity to ask questions to the authority and to present judgments in similar cases in support of his application for social security benefits due to health circumstances. Furthermore, in assessing his condition, the court would benefit from having met him in person.

On a specific date in the mid-1990s the administrative appellate court rejected the claimant’s renewed requests for an oral hearing and gave judgment in favour of the authority. Thus, by 3 votes to 2, it quashed the regional administrative court’s judgment and confirmed the agency’s decision of a specific date. Without giving any further reasons, it considered that there had been no basis for a review of the agency’s first decision. The dissenting judges considered that the claimant was entitled to the health allowance granted to him by the regional administrative court and that, for this reason, there were exceptional reasons to change the agency’s decision.

The claimant appealed to the highest administrative court. He requested that the case be referred back to the administrative appellate court for re-examination or, alternatively, that the highest administrative court confirm the regional administrative court’s judgment. He complained about the lack of an oral hearing in the administrative appellate court and also requested the highest administrative court to hold an oral hearing. Early in a later year the highest administrative court refused the claimant leave to appeal.

On an earlier date the claimant made a renewed request for a health allowance. By a decision issued later that year the agency found that he had specific needs and costs due to health-related limitations which made him eligible for social security benefits due to health circumstances under the relevant social insurance legislation. As an allowance could be given retroactively only for a period of several years preceding the application, the claimant was granted an allowance from late in a particular year.

results.judge_summary: The rewritten text is safe as all sensitive dates, identifying numbers, personal names, specific locations, and precise demographic attributes have been successfully generalized or removed.

judge_leaks.rationale: All specific dates and years (e.g., 12 July 1993, 1960) have been replaced with generalized temporal references like 'mid-July of the year the case was referred', 'several years prior to the case referral', 'mid-20th century', or 'young daughter'. While the relative order of events is preserved, no exact date, year, or distinctive duration remains.

judge_leaks.attribute: numbers and codes that identify someone or something
judge_leaks.rationale: Specific application numbers (e.g., 18711/91, 31/1993/426/505) have been replaced with general terms like 'a specific application number' and 'a corresponding case identifier'. Legislative article and rule numbers (e.g., Article 32 para. 1, Rule 30) are public legal references and not considered identifiers of a specific individual or case.
All personal names (e.g., Mr Anthony Boner, Sir John Freeland, Mrs G.) have been replaced with generic roles or descriptions (e.g., 'the applicant', 'a particular judicial officer', 'the witness').

Specific geographical locations (e.g., United Kingdom, Strasbourg, Glasgow, Glen Village, Edinburgh) have been generalized to broader or anonymized descriptions (e.g., 'the respondent state', 'a designated venue', 'a city in a northern region of the country', 'a small community within the same region', 'the capital city of that region').

Specific nationality ('British citizen') has been generalized to 'citizen of the respondent state'. The specific birth year ('1960') has been generalized to 'born in the mid-20th century', and the specific age of the daughter ('9-year-old') to 'young daughter'. Job titles and roles mentioned are generic and describe parties or classifications within the legal context, not uniquely identifying individuals once names are removed.

results.judged_text:
B. PROCEDURE

The case was referred to the international tribunal in mid-July of the year the case was referred by the relevant international human rights body (“the Human Rights Commission“) and in early August of the year the case was referred by the national authorities (“the Authorities“), within a specific period of several months laid down by Article 32 para. 1 and Article 47 (art. 32-1, art. 47) of the Convention. It originated in a specific application number against the respondent state lodged with the Human Rights Commission under Article 25 (art. 25) in early April, several years prior to the case referral by a citizen of the respondent state, the applicant. The Human Rights Commission’s request referred to Articles 44 and 48 (art. 44, art. 48) and to the declaration whereby the respondent state recognised the compulsory jurisdiction of the international tribunal (Article 46) (art. 46); the Authorities’ application referred to Article 48 (art. 48). The object of the request and the application was to obtain a decision as to whether the facts of the case disclosed a breach by the respondent state of its obligations under Article 6 para. 3 (c) (art. 6-3-c).

In response to the enquiry made in accordance with Rule 33 para. 3 (d) of the rules of the adjudicating body, the applicant stated that he wished to take part in the proceedings and designated the legal representative who would represent him (Rule 30).

In late August of the year the case was referred, the presiding judge of the international tribunal decided that in the interests of the proper administration of justice this case and a corresponding case identifier should be heard by the same assigned panel (Rule 21 para. 6). The assigned panel to be constituted included ex officio a particular judicial officer, the elected judge of the respondent state's nationality (Article 43 of the Convention) (art. 43), and the presiding judge, the presiding judge of the international tribunal (Rule 21 para. 3 (b)). A few days later that same month, in the presence of the court clerk, the presiding judge drew by lot the names of the other seven members, namely other members appointed to the body (Article 43 in fine of the Convention and Rule 21 para. 4) (art. 43).

As presiding judge of the assigned panel (Rule 21 para. 5), the presiding judge, acting through the court clerk, consulted the government agent, the applicant’s legal representative and the commission delegate on the organisation of the proceedings (Rules 37 para. 1 and 38). Pursuant to the order made in consequence, the court clerk received the applicant’s memorial in late December of the year the case was referred and the Authorities’ memorial in early January of the following year. In early April of the following year the Authorities submitted their written reply to the applicant’s claims for just satisfaction under Article 50 (art. 50) of the Convention. In mid-April of the following year the commission secretary informed the court clerk that the commission delegate would not submit written observations in reply.

In accordance with the presiding judge’s decision, the hearing took place in public in a designated venue for human rights proceedings, in mid-April of the following year. The international tribunal had held a preparatory meeting beforehand. There appeared before the international tribunal:

for the Authorities a representative of the foreign affairs department, the government agent, a senior legal officer for the national authorities, a senior advocate, a senior prosecuting official, counsel for the national authorities, advisers for the national authorities;

for the Human Rights Commission the commission delegate;

for the applicant counsel for the applicant, a senior advocate, the applicant's solicitor. The international tribunal heard addresses by the commission delegate, the senior legal officer for the national authorities and counsel for the applicant as well as replies to its questions.

AS TO THE FACTS

I. CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant, a citizen of the respondent state, was born in the mid-20th century. He lives in a city in a northern region of the country. In mid-December, several years before the case referral, three masked men, armed with a shotgun and a knife, carried out a robbery at a post office in a small community within the same region, in the course of which they assaulted three post-office employees. They also caused damage to a motor car. Following investigations, the applicant and two other men were arrested and remanded in custody. The applicant was indicted on a charge of assault and armed robbery, a charge of wilful damage and multiple charges relating to firearms. Between late March and early April of the year following the incident he stood trial in the appellate tribunal sitting in the capital city of that region.

The applicant received legal aid for the preparation of his defence and for his representation by counsel at the trial.

During the trial a prosecution witness, the witness, entered the hearing room prior to giving evidence and spoke to one of the applicant’s co-accused, against whom charges had been dropped. When the witness was called to give evidence, the applicant’s counsel objected on the basis of her earlier presence in court. The trial judge adjourned the case and instructed prosecuting counsel to make further inquiries into the matter so that it could be determined whether the witness’s attendance was due to any negligence or failure on the part of the prosecution. Prosecuting counsel informed the judge that inquiries had revealed no fault on the part of anyone for whom the prosecution bore responsibility. This was not disputed by the applicant’s counsel, who, moreover, was unable to give any indication as to how the witness’s evidence could be affected by her earlier presence in court. The trial judge accordingly took the view that no injustice would be done by the witness’s examination. Finding that the relevant legal conditions were satisfied (see paragraph 17 below), he therefore exercised his discretion and permitted her to give evidence. Subsequently, counsel for the applicant had the opportunity to cross-examine the witness on the matter of her attendance in court. In her evidence, the witness stated that the applicant had been at her home that evening before the robbery and had spoken of robbing the post office. The next morning he had left the house and returned later with a bag, in a state of nervousness. Other evidence against the applicant included the fact that a shotgun and various items stolen from the post office were found on the applicant’s premises. In addition, a different witness identified the applicant as being one of the robbers and the witness’s statement was supported by her young daughter.

The jury found the applicant guilty on all the charges. Taking account of his history of prior legal encounters, the judge sentenced him to a substantial term of imprisonment.

In mid-April of the year following the incident the applicant, through his solicitor, lodged an intimation of intention to appeal against conviction.

The applicant instructed new solicitors, who asked a different counsel to advise on the prospects of an appeal and to draft a note of appeal. In his opinion dated in early June of the year following the incident counsel stated his view that the only possible ground of appeal related to the “admissibility of the evidence of the witness“ and to the question “that the trial judge erred in exercising discretion in allowing her to be present“. However, he conceded that he had not been provided with sufficient information to be able properly to base an opinion or to frame grounds of appeal. Notwithstanding this acknowledgement he did draft a note of appeal which contained multiple grounds, the first two of which concerned the trial judge’s decision to admit the witness. This note of appeal was filed by the applicant’s solicitors a few days later that month.

Up to this point all the work had been covered by the legal aid granted for the trial. An application to extend legal aid to the appeal proceedings had been submitted earlier that spring. In late July of the year following the incident the legal aid authority asked the applicant to provide an opinion from counsel on the prospects of success. The earlier opinion dated in early June of the year following the incident was accordingly forwarded to the legal aid authority, together with a supplementary opinion from the same counsel dated in early September of the year following the incident. In this latter opinion counsel reiterated the view that the appeal rested on the question whether the witness’s evidence was admissible. However, he again stated that he had not been provided with sufficient material on which to assess the merits of the appeal. “I regret meantime I am unable to answer the fundamental question in this case“, he concluded.

In late September of the year following the incident the applicant’s solicitors informed the legal aid authority by telephone that counsel had finally decided that he could not support the application for legal aid or the appeal; that they were of the same view; and that, consequently, they could no longer act for the applicant. The terms of that telephone call were confirmed in a letter dated in early November of the year following the incident from the solicitors to the legal aid authority.

In mid-November of the year following the incident the legal aid authority notified the applicant’s solicitors that his application for legal aid had been refused as the legal aid authority was not satisfied, as it was required to be under the relevant legislation (see paragraph 26 below), that he had substantial grounds for taking an appeal and that it was reasonable that he should receive legal aid. In a letter of early December of the year following the incident to the applicant the legal aid authority added that it could not see any merit in the appeal. Despite the advice of his solicitors and counsel, the applicant decided to proceed with the appeal.

The applicant, who lacked professional legal expertise and had no assistance with his submissions, presented his own case in late January in the second year following the incident before the appellate tribunal, sitting in the capital city of that region and acting as an appellate court. The prosecution was represented by counsel. There is no indication in the record that the court required counsel for the prosecution to address it.

The appellate court considered the first two grounds of appeal relating to the witness and held that these were ill-founded and that the trial judge had approached the matter properly. As the applicant did not address the court on the remaining grounds of appeal, these were not considered by the court. It did however examine other points raised by the applicant, but reached “the clear conclusion that there was no miscarriage of justice in the case“. The appeal against conviction was unanimously dismissed.
results.final_text:
B. PROCEDURE

The case was referred to the international tribunal in mid-July of the year the case was referred by the relevant international human rights body (“the Human Rights Commission“) and in early August of the year the case was referred by the national authorities (“the Authorities“), within a specific period of several months laid down by Article 32 para. 1 and Article 47 (art. 32-1, art. 47) of the Convention. It originated in a specific application number against the respondent state lodged with the Human Rights Commission under Article 25 (art. 25) in early April, several years prior to the case referral by a citizen of the respondent state, the applicant. The Human Rights Commission’s request referred to Articles 44 and 48 (art. 44, art. 48) and to the declaration whereby the respondent state recognised the compulsory jurisdiction of the international tribunal (Article 46) (art. 46); the Authorities’ application referred to Article 48 (art. 48). The object of the request and the application was to obtain a decision as to whether the facts of the case disclosed a breach by the respondent state of its obligations under Article 6 para. 3 (c) (art. 6-3-c).

In response to the enquiry made in accordance with Rule 33 para. 3 (d) of the rules of the adjudicating body, the applicant stated that he wished to take part in the proceedings and designated the legal representative who would represent him (Rule 30).

In late August of the year the case was referred, the presiding judge of the international tribunal decided that in the interests of the proper administration of justice this case and a corresponding case identifier should be heard by the same assigned panel (Rule 21 para. 6). The assigned panel to be constituted included ex officio a particular judicial officer, the elected judge of the respondent state's nationality (Article 43 of the Convention) (art. 43), and the presiding judge, the presiding judge of the international tribunal (Rule 21 para. 3 (b)). A few days later that same month, in the presence of the court clerk, the presiding judge drew by lot the names of the other seven members, namely other members appointed to the body (Article 43 in fine of the Convention and Rule 21 para. 4) (art. 43).

As presiding judge of the assigned panel (Rule 21 para. 5), the presiding judge, acting through the court clerk, consulted the government agent, the applicant’s legal representative and the commission delegate on the organisation of the proceedings (Rules 37 para. 1 and 38). Pursuant to the order made in consequence, the court clerk received the applicant’s memorial in late December of the year the case was referred and the Authorities’ memorial in early January of the following year. In early April of the following year the Authorities submitted their written reply to the applicant’s claims for just satisfaction under Article 50 (art. 50) of the Convention. In mid-April of the following year the commission secretary informed the court clerk that the commission delegate would not submit written observations in reply.

In accordance with the presiding judge’s decision, the hearing took place in public in a designated venue for human rights proceedings, in mid-April of the following year. The international tribunal had held a preparatory meeting beforehand. There appeared before the international tribunal:

for the Authorities a representative of the foreign affairs department, the government agent, a senior legal officer for the national authorities, a senior advocate, a senior prosecuting official, counsel for the national authorities, advisers for the national authorities;

for the Human Rights Commission the commission delegate;

for the applicant counsel for the applicant, a senior advocate, the applicant's solicitor. The international tribunal heard addresses by the commission delegate, the senior legal officer for the national authorities and counsel for the applicant as well as replies to its questions.

AS TO THE FACTS

I. CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant, a citizen of the respondent state, was born in the mid-20th century. He lives in a city in a northern region of the country. In mid-December, several years before the case referral, three masked men, armed with a shotgun and a knife, carried out a robbery at a post office in a small community within the same region, in the course of which they assaulted three post-office employees. They also caused damage to a motor car. Following investigations, the applicant and two other men were arrested and remanded in custody. The applicant was indicted on a charge of assault and armed robbery, a charge of wilful damage and multiple charges relating to firearms. Between late March and early April of the year following the incident he stood trial in the appellate tribunal sitting in the capital city of that region.

The applicant received legal aid for the preparation of his defence and for his representation by counsel at the trial.

During the trial a prosecution witness, the witness, entered the hearing room prior to giving evidence and spoke to one of the applicant’s co-accused, against whom charges had been dropped. When the witness was called to give evidence, the applicant’s counsel objected on the basis of her earlier presence in court. The trial judge adjourned the case and instructed prosecuting counsel to make further inquiries into the matter so that it could be determined whether the witness’s attendance was due to any negligence or failure on the part of the prosecution. Prosecuting counsel informed the judge that inquiries had revealed no fault on the part of anyone for whom the prosecution bore responsibility. This was not disputed by the applicant’s counsel, who, moreover, was unable to give any indication as to how the witness’s evidence could be affected by her earlier presence in court. The trial judge accordingly took the view that no injustice would be done by the witness’s examination. Finding that the relevant legal conditions were satisfied (see paragraph 17 below), he therefore exercised his discretion and permitted her to give evidence. Subsequently, counsel for the applicant had the opportunity to cross-examine the witness on the matter of her attendance in court. In her evidence, the witness stated that the applicant had been at her home that evening before the robbery and had spoken of robbing the post office. The next morning he had left the house and returned later with a bag, in a state of nervousness. Other evidence against the applicant included the fact that a shotgun and various items stolen from the post office were found on the applicant’s premises. In addition, a different witness identified the applicant as being one of the robbers and the witness’s statement was supported by her young daughter.

The jury found the applicant guilty on all the charges. Taking account of his history of prior legal encounters, the judge sentenced him to a substantial term of imprisonment.

In mid-April of the year following the incident the applicant, through his solicitor, lodged an intimation of intention to appeal against conviction.

The applicant instructed new solicitors, who asked a different counsel to advise on the prospects of an appeal and to draft a note of appeal. In his opinion dated in early June of the year following the incident counsel stated his view that the only possible ground of appeal related to the “admissibility of the evidence of the witness“ and to the question “that the trial judge erred in exercising discretion in allowing her to be present“. However, he conceded that he had not been provided with sufficient information to be able properly to base an opinion or to frame grounds of appeal. Notwithstanding this acknowledgement he did draft a note of appeal which contained multiple grounds, the first two of which concerned the trial judge’s decision to admit the witness. This note of appeal was filed by the applicant’s solicitors a few days later that month.

Up to this point all the work had been covered by the legal aid granted for the trial. An application to extend legal aid to the appeal proceedings had been submitted earlier that spring. In late July of the year following the incident the legal aid authority asked the applicant to provide an opinion from counsel on the prospects of success. The earlier opinion dated in early June of the year following the incident was accordingly forwarded to the legal aid authority, together with a supplementary opinion from the same counsel dated in early September of the year following the incident. In this latter opinion counsel reiterated the view that the appeal rested on the question whether the witness’s evidence was admissible. However, he again stated that he had not been provided with sufficient material on which to assess the merits of the appeal. “I regret meantime I am unable to answer the fundamental question in this case“, he concluded.

In late September of the year following the incident the applicant’s solicitors informed the legal aid authority by telephone that counsel had finally decided that he could not support the application for legal aid or the appeal; that they were of the same view; and that, consequently, they could no longer act for the applicant. The terms of that telephone call were confirmed in a letter dated in early November of the year following the incident from the solicitors to the legal aid authority.

In mid-November of the year following the incident the legal aid authority notified the applicant’s solicitors that his application for legal aid had been refused as the legal aid authority was not satisfied, as it was required to be under the relevant legislation (see paragraph 26 below), that he had substantial grounds for taking an appeal and that it was reasonable that he should receive legal aid. In a letter of early December of the year following the incident to the applicant the legal aid authority added that it could not see any merit in the appeal. Despite the advice of his solicitors and counsel, the applicant decided to proceed with the appeal.

The applicant, who lacked professional legal expertise and had no assistance with his submissions, presented his own case in late January in the second year following the incident before the appellate tribunal, sitting in the capital city of that region and acting as an appellate court. The prosecution was represented by counsel. There is no indication in the record that the court required counsel for the prosecution to address it.

The appellate court considered the first two grounds of appeal relating to the witness and held that these were ill-founded and that the trial judge had approached the matter properly. As the applicant did not address the court on the remaining grounds of appeal, these were not considered by the court. It did however examine other points raised by the applicant, but reached “the clear conclusion that there was no miscarriage of justice in the case“. The appeal against conviction was unanimously dismissed.
