results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. [APPLICATION NUMBER]) against the respondent State of Turkey lodged with the European Court of Human Rights under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by an individual, the applicant, in mid-2002.

The applicant was represented by their legal representative, a lawyer practising in Mardin. The Government of Turkey (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent.

The applicant alleged that, as a result of unfair decisions given by the domestic courts, he had been deprived of his land which was the main source of income for his family. He relied on Articles 6, 13, 14 and 18 of the Convention and Article 1 of Protocol No. 1.

Early in 2008 the President of the Second Section decided to give notice of the application to the Government. It was also decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility (Article 29 § 3).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the early 1950s and lives in a certain regional town.

On 22 November 1984 Law no. 3083 came into force. Article 5 of this law provided the possibility of leasing public land to farmers who were in need and who fulfilled certain eligibility criteria (“right holders”). In particular, Article 24 § 1 of Law no. 3083 provided that those who had been convicted of certain offences were not entitled to benefit from this law (see paragraph 16 below).

Following the promulgation of Law no. 3083, the applicant applied to a special committee which determined the eligibility of farmers to be right holders and requested to be provided with land in Ceylanpınar in the south-east of the country.

Early in 1999 the applicant was identified as a right holder and leased land from the Directorate General for Agricultural Reform (“the Directorate General”) for an indefinite period on payment of a rent. The applicant earned his living by cultivating this land.

In May of a subsequent year (2000), after conducting a “security investigation”, the Directorate General annulled the right holder status of the applicant.

Later that same month, the applicant brought an action in the Gaziantep Administrative Court requesting the stay of execution and reversal of the Directorate General’s decision.

Early in June of that year (2000) the Gaziantep Administrative Court decided to request the defendant administration to indicate the reasons which grounded its decision to annul the right holder status of the applicant. The court invited the defendant administration to submit their response and relevant documents within a specified period.

Early in August of that year (2000) the Gaziantep Administrative Court dismissed the applicant’s request for a stay of execution of the decision, holding that necessary conditions were not met.

On an unspecified date, the Directorate General refused to submit the information or documents requested by the Gaziantep Administrative Court. In this respect, the Directorate General relied on Article 20 § 3 of the Law on Administrative Procedure which provides that the Prime Minister or other Government Minister may refrain from submitting information or documents to an administrative court if the information or documents in question pertain to the security or vital interests of the State.

In December of that year (2000) the Gaziantep Administrative Court dismissed the applicant’s case. The court held that the impugned decision of May 2000 and the refusal of the Directorate General to submit the relevant information and documents had been in accordance with the domestic legislation. The applicant appealed.

In April of a later year (2002) the Supreme Administrative Court dismissed the applicant’s appeal. This decision was served on the applicant in early July of the same year.
results.final_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. [Case_Number]) against the respondent State lodged with a judicial body under Article 34 of an international treaty for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the treaty”) by an individual, the claimant, at a certain point in the early 2000s.

The claimant was represented by their legal representative, practising in a particular locality. The State authorities were represented by their Agent.

The claimant alleged that, as a result of unfair decisions given by the domestic tribunals, they had been deprived of property that was central to their livelihood. They relied on Articles 6, 13, 14 and 18 of the treaty and Article 1 of Protocol No. 1.

After a period had passed, the presiding official of the relevant section decided to give notice of the application to the State authorities. It was also decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility (Article 29 § 3).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The claimant was born in the mid-20th century and lives in a regional town.

In the mid-1980s, a particular legislative act came into force. Article 5 of this act provided the possibility of leasing land of the country to individuals engaged in cultivation who met certain eligibility criteria (“right holders”). In particular, Article 24 § 1 of this legislative act provided that those who had been convicted of certain offences were not entitled to benefit from its provisions (see paragraph 16 below).

Following the promulgation of this legislative act, the claimant applied to a Commission which determined the eligibility of individuals to be right holders and requested to be provided with land in an agricultural area in a particular geographical region.

Towards the close of the 20th century, the claimant was identified as a right holder and leased land from the central agency responsible for agricultural land distribution (“the central agency”) for an indefinite period on payment of a rent. The claimant sustained themselves through the use of this land.

At the start of the new millennium, after conducting a “security investigation”, the central agency annulled the right holder status of the claimant.

Shortly after this decision, the claimant brought an action in a regional administrative tribunal requesting the stay of execution and reversal of the central agency’s decision.

In the period immediately following, the regional administrative tribunal decided to request the defendant administration to indicate the reasons which grounded its decision to annul the right holder status of the claimant. The tribunal invited the defendant administration to submit their response and relevant documents within a specified timeframe.

A few months later, the regional administrative tribunal dismissed the claimant’s request for a stay of execution of the decision, holding that necessary conditions were not met.

On a subsequent occasion, the central agency refused to submit the information or documents requested by the regional administrative tribunal. In this respect, the central agency relied on Article 20 § 3 of the Law on Administrative Procedure which provides that senior executive officials may refrain from submitting information or documents to an administrative tribunal if the information or documents in question pertain to the security or vital interests of the country.

At a later point, the regional administrative tribunal dismissed the claimant’s case. The tribunal held that the impugned decision made at the start of the new millennium and the refusal of the central agency to submit the relevant information and documents had been in accordance with the domestic legislation. The claimant appealed.

Some time later, a higher administrative appeals tribunal dismissed the claimant’s appeal. This decision was served on the claimant during that period.

results.judged_text:
PROCE DURE

The case originated in an application (a specific application number) against the respondent state lodged with a regional human rights body (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of a relevant international human rights convention (“the Convention”) by a dual national, the individual (“the applicant”), on a date early in that year.

The applicant was represented by a legal representative, a lawyer practising in a major city in the country. The national authorities (“the authorities”) were represented by their Agent, an appointed agent, an Attorney, from a specific department within the national legal administration.

The applicant alleged that certain criminal proceedings against him in relation to fraud charges had exceeded a reasonable time, in violation of Article 6 § 1 of the Convention.

The application was transmitted to the Tribunal on a date late in that period, when Protocol No. 11 to the Convention came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

The application was allocated to a specific section of the Tribunal (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of the Tribunal). Within that Section, the designated panel that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1 of the Rules of the Tribunal.

By a decision of a date late in a subsequent year the designated panel declared the application admissible.

The applicant and the authorities each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1). The designated panel having decided, after consulting the parties, that no hearing on the merits was required (Rule 59 § 2 in fine), decided the case on the basis of the case-file as it stood.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

In a period in the mid-1980s, the applicant, together with three associates, started a business involving lactic cultures, purportedly for use in the cosmetic industry. It consisted of two limited liability companies, founded respectively in the middle and end of that same year and named Company A and Company B. According to the local judicial bodies’ findings in the proceedings referred to below, the business was a fraudulent pyramid scheme by which Company A purchased from a producer in a neighboring country freeze-dried concentrates of lactic acid bacteria at a certain amount per bag (containing a small quantity), resold them to Company B at a higher amount per bag, which again sold them at the price of a significantly higher amount per bag to cultivators whom it had recruited, and after several weeks bought the cultivated products back at the price of another higher amount. The number of producers increased from a few at the end of that year to numerous by early a later year. Payments were made to a company in a financial center abroad. No goods containing the cultivated substances were ever marketed or produced. As of late in a preceding year, the companies stopped bookkeeping.

On a date early in the year, a consumer protection agency filed a complaint with the chief public prosecutor's office against Company B and Company A for defrauding numerous persons who had invested in products from Company B. The companies went into bankruptcy early in that year. Between early and mid-year, the police received many complaints from cultivators in various parts of the country. Further complaints were made early in two subsequent years respectively by a major bank and the tax authorities.

On a date early in the year, the police interviewed the applicant as a witness, because of his role in the companies. On another date shortly after, the police carried out searches of premises and confiscations. Such measures, including interviews of the persons involved, continued that same month. On a date in that month the applicant was arrested but then released on certain conditions. He then moved to another European country and, by a letter on a date late in that year, his lawyer informed the police of his location details in that country and his preparedness to go to the major city to meet police investigators if necessary. Throughout a certain period, investigations were conducted in several European countries concerning the companies’ business connections and investments. Later, in a spring period, police held meetings with police authorities from other European countries. During a period in the early 1990s, police interviewed a number of associates. On a date early in a subsequent year, the probate authority received the final report from the administrators of the estates of Company A and Company B. On a date early in a further year, the police received the tax authorities’ report on the tax and value-added tax (“VAT”) offences.

After the initial interviews mentioned above, one of the two leading figures in the criminal activity concerned, a co-defendant, disappeared. In a late part of that same period, a search for him with an arrest warrant was made via an international law enforcement agency. On a date late in that period he was arrested by police from another European country. The national authorities requested his extradition on a date later that month. On a date mid-year in the subsequent year he was released from custody in that country, subject to a duty to report to the police. Early in a later year he was extradited to the country.

Following the issuing of indictments in mid-year in a prior period, the case was submitted to the local court on a date in the latter part of that period.

On a date early in the following year, the local judicial body fixed a date in the autumn of that year for opening the trial hearing, rather than the earlier date in the spring of that year, to which objection had been made by defence counsel, whilst accepted by the prosecution.

In the final indictments issued on a date in the early part of that year, the applicant was charged with the offences of serious fraud in relation to amounts totalling approximately a substantial sum (Articles 270 and 271 of the Penal Code), unlawfully drawing up cheques – in amounts totalling a significant amount – on behalf of an insolvent company (Articles 285 and 288 of the Penal Code), and having disregarded statutory obligations relating to bookkeeping and the provision of information to the authorities relevant to VAT payments, with the resultant evasion of another significant amount (Article 286 of the Penal Code and section 72 of the VAT Act a relevant year in the previous century – merverdiavgiftsloven). After holding a hearing over a period of several weeks in the autumn of that year, the local judicial body, by a judgment of a date late in that autumn, convicted the applicant of all but the VAT charges, and sentenced him to a specific number of years’ imprisonment. As regards sentencing, the local judicial body noted from the outset that the offences for which the applicant was convicted were punishable by up to a higher number of years’ imprisonment. It further stated that general considerations of crime prevention suggested that economic offences of the kind at issue warranted a severe penalty. As it appears from its reasoning, the local judicial body found the defendants’ conduct particularly reprehensible and emphasised that the suffering which they had caused to innocent cultivators must be viewed as an aggravating factor in sentencing. The local judicial body then went on to consider the question of mitigation, having regard to the following three factors: “With the exception of the co-defendant, the accused were of a relatively early stage of life at the time of the commission of the proscribed conduct. ... There were thus important periods of time for settling down which elapsed between the criminal acts and judgment in the case. Furthermore, the Tribunal attaches some weight, albeit not a great deal, to the fact that the offences were committed at a time when banks were somewhat uncritical about giving loans. ... In the judicial body’s view, had greater caution been displayed, the pyramid scheme would have been revealed earlier. Finally the judicial body attaches weight, to a not insignificant degree, to the fact that a considerable period has elapsed since the acts were committed. The prosecution transmitted the case to the local judicial body late in a prior year and final investigations concerning one aspect of the case as important as the value of the cultivators and their possible use went on as late as the early part of the following year. Although it is correct that the co-defendant stayed abroad over long periods, the judicial body could not see how this could justify the delays in the investigations. It is understood that the delays in the investigation were caused by a change of investigators in the case. Notwithstanding that weight must be given to the considerable period that has elapsed since the acts were committed, the judicial body finds that the sentencing must be severe.” Two other co-defendants, including the co-defendant, who were considered to have played a more prominent role in the proscribed activity, were sentenced to another specific number of years’ imprisonment.

On a date late in that year the individual sought to appeal to the highest court, alleging a procedural error in the first instance proceedings and requesting a new trial. On a date in the spring of a subsequent year the appellate selection committee of the highest court admitted the appeal in as far as it concerned the procedural matter. In the proceedings before the highest court the individual in addition asked for a reduction in sentence. After taking evidence on a date late in that year and holding a hearing on a date in the autumn of a further year, the highest court rejected the appeal on a date shortly after. As regards sentencing, it recalled that the local judicial body had taken into consideration as a mitigating circumstance the considerable period which had elapsed since the incriminated conduct. The highest court held that, although an extended period had elapsed since the local judicial body’s judgment, it saw no reason for reducing the sentence.
results.final_text:
PROCE DURE

The case originated in an application (a specific application number) against the respondent state lodged with a regional human rights body (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of a relevant international human rights convention (“the Convention”) by a dual national, the individual (“the applicant”), on a date early in that year.

The applicant was represented by a legal representative, a lawyer practising in a major city in the country. The national authorities (“the authorities”) were represented by their Agent, an appointed agent, an Attorney, from a specific department within the national legal administration.

The applicant alleged that certain criminal proceedings against him in relation to fraud charges had exceeded a reasonable time, in violation of Article 6 § 1 of the Convention.

The application was transmitted to the Tribunal on a date late in that period, when Protocol No. 11 to the Convention came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

The application was allocated to a specific section of the Tribunal (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of the Tribunal). Within that Section, the designated panel that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1 of the Rules of the Tribunal.

By a decision of a date late in a subsequent year the designated panel declared the application admissible.

The applicant and the authorities each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1). The designated panel having decided, after consulting the parties, that no hearing on the merits was required (Rule 59 § 2 in fine), decided the case on the basis of the case-file as it stood.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

In a period in the mid-1980s, the applicant, together with three associates, started a business involving lactic cultures, purportedly for use in the cosmetic industry. It consisted of two limited liability companies, founded respectively in the middle and end of that same year and named Company A and Company B. According to the local judicial bodies’ findings in the proceedings referred to below, the business was a fraudulent pyramid scheme by which Company A purchased from a producer in a neighboring country freeze-dried concentrates of lactic acid bacteria at a certain amount per bag (containing a small quantity), resold them to Company B at a higher amount per bag, which again sold them at the price of a significantly higher amount per bag to cultivators whom it had recruited, and after several weeks bought the cultivated products back at the price of another higher amount. The number of producers increased from a few at the end of that year to numerous by early a later year. Payments were made to a company in a financial center abroad. No goods containing the cultivated substances were ever marketed or produced. As of late in a preceding year, the companies stopped bookkeeping.

On a date early in the year, a consumer protection agency filed a complaint with the chief public prosecutor's office against Company B and Company A for defrauding numerous persons who had invested in products from Company B. The companies went into bankruptcy early in that year. Between early and mid-year, the police received many complaints from cultivators in various parts of the country. Further complaints were made early in two subsequent years respectively by a major bank and the tax authorities.

On a date early in the year, the police interviewed the applicant as a witness, because of his role in the companies. On another date shortly after, the police carried out searches of premises and confiscations. Such measures, including interviews of the persons involved, continued that same month. On a date in that month the applicant was arrested but then released on certain conditions. He then moved to another European country and, by a letter on a date late in that year, his lawyer informed the police of his location details in that country and his preparedness to go to the major city to meet police investigators if necessary. Throughout a certain period, investigations were conducted in several European countries concerning the companies’ business connections and investments. Later, in a spring period, police held meetings with police authorities from other European countries. During a period in the early 1990s, police interviewed a number of associates. On a date early in a subsequent year, the probate authority received the final report from the administrators of the estates of Company A and Company B. On a date early in a further year, the police received the tax authorities’ report on the tax and value-added tax (“VAT”) offences.

After the initial interviews mentioned above, one of the two leading figures in the criminal activity concerned, a co-defendant, disappeared. In a late part of that same period, a search for him with an arrest warrant was made via an international law enforcement agency. On a date late in that period he was arrested by police from another European country. The national authorities requested his extradition on a date later that month. On a date mid-year in the subsequent year he was released from custody in that country, subject to a duty to report to the police. Early in a later year he was extradited to the country.

Following the issuing of indictments in mid-year in a prior period, the case was submitted to the local court on a date in the latter part of that period.

On a date early in the following year, the local judicial body fixed a date in the autumn of that year for opening the trial hearing, rather than the earlier date in the spring of that year, to which objection had been made by defence counsel, whilst accepted by the prosecution.

In the final indictments issued on a date in the early part of that year, the applicant was charged with the offences of serious fraud in relation to amounts totalling approximately a substantial sum (Articles 270 and 271 of the Penal Code), unlawfully drawing up cheques – in amounts totalling a significant amount – on behalf of an insolvent company (Articles 285 and 288 of the Penal Code), and having disregarded statutory obligations relating to bookkeeping and the provision of information to the authorities relevant to VAT payments, with the resultant evasion of another significant amount (Article 286 of the Penal Code and section 72 of the VAT Act a relevant year in the previous century – merverdiavgiftsloven). After holding a hearing over a period of several weeks in the autumn of that year, the local judicial body, by a judgment of a date late in that autumn, convicted the applicant of all but the VAT charges, and sentenced him to a specific number of years’ imprisonment. As regards sentencing, the local judicial body noted from the outset that the offences for which the applicant was convicted were punishable by up to a higher number of years’ imprisonment. It further stated that general considerations of crime prevention suggested that economic offences of the kind at issue warranted a severe penalty. As it appears from its reasoning, the local judicial body found the defendants’ conduct particularly reprehensible and emphasised that the suffering which they had caused to innocent cultivators must be viewed as an aggravating factor in sentencing. The local judicial body then went on to consider the question of mitigation, having regard to the following three factors: “With the exception of the co-defendant, the accused were of a relatively early stage of life at the time of the commission of the proscribed conduct. ... There were thus important periods of time for settling down which elapsed between the criminal acts and judgment in the case. Furthermore, the Tribunal attaches some weight, albeit not a great deal, to the fact that the offences were committed at a time when banks were somewhat uncritical about giving loans. ... In the judicial body’s view, had greater caution been displayed, the pyramid scheme would have been revealed earlier. Finally the judicial body attaches weight, to a not insignificant degree, to the fact that a considerable period has elapsed since the acts were committed. The prosecution transmitted the case to the local judicial body late in a prior year and final investigations concerning one aspect of the case as important as the value of the cultivators and their possible use went on as late as the early part of the following year. Although it is correct that the co-defendant stayed abroad over long periods, the judicial body could not see how this could justify the delays in the investigations. It is understood that the delays in the investigation were caused by a change of investigators in the case. Notwithstanding that weight must be given to the considerable period that has elapsed since the acts were committed, the judicial body finds that the sentencing must be severe.” Two other co-defendants, including the co-defendant, who were considered to have played a more prominent role in the proscribed activity, were sentenced to another specific number of years’ imprisonment.

On a date late in that year the individual sought to appeal to the highest court, alleging a procedural error in the first instance proceedings and requesting a new trial. On a date in the spring of a subsequent year the appellate selection committee of the highest court admitted the appeal in as far as it concerned the procedural matter. In the proceedings before the highest court the individual in addition asked for a reduction in sentence. After taking evidence on a date late in that year and holding a hearing on a date in the autumn of a further year, the highest court rejected the appeal on a date shortly after. As regards sentencing, it recalled that the local judicial body had taken into consideration as a mitigating circumstance the considerable period which had elapsed since the incriminated conduct. The highest court held that, although an extended period had elapsed since the local judicial body’s judgment, it saw no reason for reducing the sentence.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (identified by a particular number) against the Republic of Turkey lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Turkish national (referred to herein as “the applicant”), on a specific date in late 2001.

The applicant was represented by his lawyer, who practiced in Izmir. The Turkish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent.

On a specific date in early 2007, the Court declared the application partly inadmissible and decided to communicate to the Government the complaints concerning the lack of legal assistance to the applicant during his police custody and the non-communication of the written opinion of the relevant public prosecutor at the Court of Cassation. It also decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility (Article 29 § 3).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the late 1960s and lives in Izmir.

On a specific date in April 2000, the applicant was taken into custody by police officers from the Anti-Terrorist Branch of the Izmir Security Directorate on suspicion of his involvement in the activities of an illegal armed organisation.

On the same day, the applicant was interrogated at the Anti-Terrorist branch in the absence of a lawyer. According to a form explaining arrested persons’ rights which the applicant had signed, he had been reminded of the charges against him and of his right to remain silent. In his statement, the applicant admitted to his involvement in the illegal armed organisation.

Three days later, the applicant was brought before the public prosecutor and subsequently the investigating judge at the Izmir State Security Court. Before the public prosecutor and the judge, again in the absence of a lawyer, the applicant admitted that he had participated in certain activities of the illegal armed organisation.

On the same day, the applicant’s lawyer requested to visit him; however this request was rejected by the Public Prosecutor.

Several days later, in early May of that year, the Public Prosecutor at the Izmir State Security Court filed a bill of indictment with that court, accusing the applicant of aiding and abetting an illegal organisation. He requested that the applicant be sentenced and convicted under Article 169 of the Criminal Code and Section 5 of the Prevention of Terrorism Act (Law no. 3713).

Several months later, in late 2000, the Izmir State Security Court convicted the applicant as charged and sentenced him to several years’ imprisonment. In its decision, the court attached particular importance to the applicant’s statements to the investigating judge.

Later that same month, the applicant appealed. On an unspecified date, the relevant public prosecutor at the Court of Cassation submitted his written opinion. This opinion was not served on the applicant or his representative. Several months later, in mid-2001, the Court of Cassation, upholding the reasoning and assessment of the Izmir State Security Court, dismissed the applicant’s appeal.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. [CASE_NUMBER]) against a Western European Member State lodged with the international human rights tribunal under Article 34 of the fundamental human rights convention (“the Convention”) by a national from a non-EU country, the applicant, around the middle of November of the primary procedural year.

The applicant was represented by [LAW_FIRM_NAME] Solicitors, a firm of lawyers practising in a major metropolitan area in the respondent State. The respondent authorities were represented by their Agent, [LEGAL_REPRESENTATIVE_NAME] of the relevant governmental department for external affairs.

The applicant alleged that his deportation to his country of origin would breach Articles 3 and 8 of the Convention.

On a few days later in that same month, a presiding judicial officer of a designated chamber of the international tribunal decided to apply Rule 39 of the Rules of Court, indicating to the respondent authorities that it was in the interests of the parties and the proper conduct of the proceedings that the applicant should not be expelled to his country of origin pending the judicial body’s decision. In the spring of the following year, a senior judicial officer of the designated chamber decided to give notice of the application to the respondent authorities. It was also decided to rule on the admissibility and merits of the application at the same time (Article 29 § 1).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The facts of the case, as submitted by the applicant, may be summarised as follows.

The applicant, who was born in the mid-1980s, claims to have arrived in the respondent State in early childhood. However, there is no official record of his presence until the mid-1990s, when he was in his pre-teen years. He first came to the notice of the governmental minister for the national immigration department late in the mid-1990s, when a domestic welfare court sought clarification of the applicant’s immigration status, while considering an application for a residence order in respect of the applicant by a relative.

The applicant’s relative made an application for indefinite leave to remain in the respondent State, on the basis of her long stay, and with the applicant listed as her dependant, early in a prior decade. While this application was under consideration, a further application for indefinite leave was made on behalf of the applicant by local social services. This application stated that the applicant had been thrown out by his relative in a year earlier than the relative's application and placed in foster care. It also mentioned that the applicant claimed to have been the victim of beatings by his relative and her partner since a very young age. Indefinite leave to remain was granted to the applicant, outside the immigration rules, late in that same prior decade. The applicant lived in foster care from the early part of that prior decade until he reached adulthood, when he began to live alone in public housing.

The applicant was convicted early in an earlier year of that prior decade, in early adulthood, of multiple charges of possession of controlled substances of a serious nature with intent to supply. He pleaded guilty on the basis that he had been coerced into letting his premises be used for the preparation and sale of drugs by a group of people whom he feared because of a previous attack some years before the conviction, in which the applicant had been shot. He was sentenced to a significant period of imprisonment, and late in the year of conviction was notified of the governmental minister’s intention to deport him. The governmental minister found that there was no evidence that the applicant had been present in the respondent State since a very young age. His relative had stated that he had been left with her by his parent in early childhood, and the first official record of his presence was when he was in his pre-teen years. Even allowing for his long stay in the respondent State, only a limited period had been with valid leave. It was believed that he was in contact with his parent, who remained in his country of origin, and that as he had lived alone since attaining the age of majority, the applicant was evidently independent and capable of adapting to new circumstances. It was not accepted that he had family life in the respondent State.

The applicant appealed against the decision to deport him and his appeal was dismissed by the national immigration appeals body early in the year following the conviction. The reviewing body noted the applicant’s previous criminal record: he had been convicted of possession of controlled substances of a serious nature and other controlled substances early in the decade prior to the conviction; handling stolen goods mid-year in the decade prior to the conviction; and possession of controlled substances in mid-year, a few years before the conviction.

The reviewing body also noted his claim to be in a relationship of some years’ duration, but observed that he had never mentioned his girlfriend in previous applications to the national immigration department, and that he and his girlfriend had given mutually inconsistent evidence at the hearing. It was not therefore accepted that he was in a serious or permanent relationship. He had no contact with the relative with whom he had previously lived, but had another relative in the respondent State whom he claimed was a surrogate parent to him. However, the reviewing body found that he could not be as close to this relative as he claimed, given that she had not taken him in when he had been thrown out, and that it was clear from her evidence at the hearing that she had been unaware of his criminal conviction. The applicant’s relationship with a sibling in the respondent State was found to have been similarly exaggerated. The reviewing body concluded that the applicant did not have any protected family life in the respondent State. With regard to his private life, while it was accepted that he had been in the country since a young age and had been educated there, as well as gaining some work experience, it was not considered that these ties were sufficiently strong to render his deportation an interference with his private life. It appeared that his parent still lived in his country of origin and, even if contact had been lost, as claimed by the applicant, there was no reason why it could not be re-established. Whilst the applicant would have practical difficulties in relocating to his country of origin, he could re-establish his private life there. The reviewing body took into account the case of [ANOTHER_CASE_NAME] v. [ANOTHER_STATE] [GC], a specific case number, from the relevant human rights jurisprudence from mid-decade E, in finding that, on balance, and having due regard to the public interest, the applicant’s deportation was proportionate.

A deportation order against the applicant was signed late in the year of appeal dismissal. The applicant made an application to have the order revoked on human rights grounds, which was rejected by the governmental minister mid-year of the primary procedural year on the basis that all matters raised by the applicant had previously been considered by the national immigration appeals body. An application for judicial review of this decision was refused late in the autumn of the primary procedural year. A superior national court, in refusing the application, stated that the applicant had no family life in the respondent State, and that the interference with his private life was proportionate. The applicant did not renew his application for judicial review.

The applicant was taken into state detention early November of the primary procedural year and directions for his deportation to his country of origin were set a couple of days later for a week after detention. Shortly after detention, the applicant sought interim measures from this judicial body under Rule 39 of the Rules of Court to prevent his deportation. He submitted with his application a report from a mental health professional dated early autumn of the primary procedural year, which stated that the applicant had attempted suicide in the late summer of the primary procedural year, after being notified of the refusal of his human rights application by the governmental minister. He had then been held as an in-patient until early September of the primary procedural year. The report also stated that the applicant had continued to express feelings of despair throughout his in-patient treatment. He was described as suffering from a significant mental health condition.

On a few days after the interim measures were sought, a presiding judicial officer of a designated chamber of the international tribunal decided to apply Rule 39 of the Rules of Court, indicating to the respondent authorities that it was in the interests of the parties and the proper conduct of the proceedings that the applicant should not be expelled to his country of origin pending the judicial body’s decision. Rule 39 was initially applied by the presiding judicial officer for a short period, in order to give the respondent authorities the opportunity to comment on the applicant’s mental health history and to state whether any special measures had been put in place to alleviate the risk of suicide prior to and during his proposed removal.

By letter dated early in December of the primary procedural year, the respondent authorities informed the judicial body that they had not previously been aware of the applicant’s attempted suicide, but had now considered the psychiatric report from early autumn of the primary procedural year. As regards the logistics of the applicant’s removal, the respondent authorities stated that all appropriate measures to protect the applicant from risk were already in place at the detention facility at which he was detained. Trained members of staff were aware of the applicant’s situation and the applicant was under constant supervision. As to the risk during removal, the contractor effecting removal would be informed and a suitable escort would be provided, including a medical escort if deemed necessary. The applicant would be escorted up until the point of arrival in his country of origin. The respondent authorities also considered that there were sufficient mental health facilities in his country of origin, which would be available to the applicant if needed. The respondent authorities therefore invited the judicial body to lift the interim measure which had been indicated in respect of the applicant. However, a few days later in that same month, the presiding judicial officer decided to prolong until further notice the interim measure under Rule 39.

The applicant notified the authorities in the state detention facility that he had taken an overdose of paracetamol late in December of the primary procedural year. He was assessed by the medical team and found only to have taken a few tablets. No further concerns as to his well-being appear to have been raised and the applicant was released from detention early in the subsequent year. He does not claim to have made any further attempts at suicide.
results.final_text:
The case originated in an application (a specific case number) against a particular Member State lodged with the present judicial body under a specific article of a key human rights convention (”the convention”) by a national from a West African country, the applicant, on a particular date in late autumn of that year.

The applicant was represented by a firm of lawyers practising in a major city. The respondent authorities were represented by their agent, a representative from a government department.

The applicant alleged that their deportation to that West African country would breach specific articles of the convention.

Shortly after that date, the presiding official of a specific section decided to apply a specific procedural rule of the Court, indicating to the respondent authorities that it was in the interests of the parties and the proper conduct of the proceedings that the applicant should not be expelled to that West African country pending the judicial body’s decision. Several months later, another senior judicial official of that section decided to give notice of the application to the respondent authorities. It was also decided to rule on the admissibility and merits of the application at the same time (a specific procedural article).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The facts of the case, as submitted by the applicant, may be summarised as follows.

The applicant, who was born in the mid-1980s, claims to have arrived in this country at a very young age. However, there is no official record of their presence until the mid-1990s, when they were a young age. They first came to the notice of the relevant government minister for immigration on a date in late 1994, when a family judicial body sought clarification of the applicant’s immigration status, while considering an application for a residence order in respect of the applicant by a family member.

The applicant’s family member made an application for indefinite leave to remain in this country, on the basis of their long stay, and with the applicant listed as their dependant, on a date in early 2003. While this application was under consideration, a further application for indefinite leave was made on behalf of the applicant by a local social services department. This application stated that the applicant had been thrown out by that family member on a date in early 2002 and placed in foster care. It also mentioned that the applicant claimed to have been the victim of beatings by that family member and a partner since a very young age. Indefinite leave to remain was granted to the applicant, outside the immigration rules, on a date in late 2003. The applicant lived in foster care from the early 2000s until adulthood, when they began to live alone in council accommodation.

The applicant was convicted on a date in early 2007, at their early twenties, of multiple serious drug-related offenses. They pleaded guilty on the basis that they had been coerced into letting their premises be used for the preparation and sale of drugs by a group of people whom they feared because of a prior incident in a prior year, in which the applicant had been physically assaulted. They were sentenced to a significant period of imprisonment, and on a date in late 2007 was notified of the relevant government minister’s intention to deport them. The relevant government minister found that there was no evidence that the applicant had been present in this country since a very young age. Their family member had stated that they had been left with her by a parent at a young age, and the first official record of their presence was when they were a young age. Even allowing for their long stay in this country, only a number of years had been with valid leave. It was believed that they were in contact with their parent, who remained in that West African country, and that as they had lived alone since attaining the age of majority, the applicant was evidently independent and capable of adapting to new circumstances. It was not accepted that they had family life in this country.

The applicant appealed against the decision to deport them and their appeal was dismissed by a national immigration appeal tribunal on a date in early 2008. The national appeal body noted the applicant’s previous criminal record: they had been convicted of drug-related offenses involving different categories of substances in early 2004; offenses related to stolen property in some months later in 2004; and drug-related offenses involving a less serious category of substances in a year later in mid-2005.

The national appeal body also noted their claim to be in a relationship of some years’ duration, but observed that they had never mentioned a partner in previous applications to the relevant government department, and that the applicant and their partner had given mutually inconsistent evidence at the hearing. It was not therefore accepted that they were in a serious or permanent relationship. They had no contact with the family member with whom they had previously lived, but had another family member in this country whom they claimed was a surrogate parent to them. However, the national appeal body found that they could not be as close to this family member as they claimed, given that she had not taken them in when they had been thrown out, and that it was clear from her evidence at the hearing that she had been unaware of their criminal conviction. The applicant’s relationship with another family member in this country was found to have been similarly exaggerated. The national appeal body concluded that the applicant did not have any protected family life in this country. With regard to their private life, while it was accepted that they had been in the country since a young age and had been educated there, as well as gaining some work experience, it was not considered that these ties were sufficiently strong to render their deportation an interference with their private life. It appeared that their parent still lived in that West African country and, even if contact had been lost, as claimed by the applicant, there was no reason why it could not be re-established. Whilst the applicant would have practical difficulties in relocating to that West African country, they could re-establish their private life there. The national appeal body took into account a previous case concerning similar issues, in finding that, on balance, and having due regard to the public interest, the applicant’s deportation was proportionate.

A deportation order against the applicant was signed on a date in late 2008. The applicant made an application to have the order revoked on human rights grounds, which was rejected by the relevant government minister on a date in mid-2009 on the basis that all matters raised by the applicant had previously been considered by the national immigration appeal tribunal. An application for judicial review of this decision was refused on a date in late 2009. A superior national court, in refusing the application, stated that the applicant had no family life in this country, and that the interference with their private life was proportionate. The applicant did not renew their application for judicial review.

The applicant was taken into a detention facility on a date in mid-November of that year and directions for their deportation to that West African country were set two days later for a week later. Around the same time, the applicant sought interim measures from this judicial body under a specific procedural rule of the Court to prevent their deportation. They submitted with their application a report from a mental health professional dated a date in mid-September of that year, which stated that the applicant had engaged in self-harm on a month prior, after being notified of the refusal of their human rights application by the relevant government minister. They had then been held as an in-patient until a date in early September of that year. The report also stated that the applicant had continued to express feelings of despair throughout their in-patient treatment. They were described as experiencing significant emotional distress.

On a date in mid-November of that year, the presiding official of that section decided to apply a specific procedural rule of the Court, indicating to the respondent authorities that it was in the interests of the parties and the proper conduct of the proceedings that the applicant should not be expelled to that West African country pending the judicial body’s decision. The specific procedural rule was initially applied by the presiding official for a short period, in order to give the respondent authorities the opportunity to comment on the applicant’s mental health history and to state whether any special measures had been put in place to alleviate the risk of self-harm prior to and during their proposed removal.

By letter dated a date in early December of that year, the respondent authorities informed the judicial body that they had not previously been aware of the applicant’s self-harm, but had now considered the mental health professional report of a date in mid-September of that year. As regards the logistics of the applicant’s removal, the respondent authorities stated that all appropriate measures to protect the applicant from risk were already in place at the facility at which they were detained. Trained members of staff were aware of the applicant’s situation and the applicant was under constant supervision. As to the risk during removal, the contractor effecting removal would be informed and a suitable escort would be provided, including a medical escort if deemed necessary. The applicant would be escorted up until the point of arrival in that West African country. The respondent authorities also considered that there were sufficient mental health facilities in that West African country, which would be available to the applicant if needed. The respondent authorities therefore invited the judicial body to lift the interim measure which had been indicated in respect of the applicant. However, a few days later, the presiding official decided to prolong until further notice the interim measure under a specific procedural rule.

The applicant notified the authorities in the detention facility that they had taken an overdose of medication on a date in late December of that year. They were assessed by the medical team and found only to have taken a small number of tablets. No further concerns as to their well-being appear to have been raised and the applicant was released from detention on a date in early January of the following year. They do not claim to have made any further attempts at self-harm.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a particular application number) against the United Kingdom lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a national of a Member State, the applicant, on a date in late autumn of that year.

The applicant, who had been granted legal aid, was represented by a legal practice. The United Kingdom Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent from a relevant government department.

The applicant alleged in particular that his whole life sentence gave rise to a violation of Article 3 of the Convention.

The application was allocated to a Section of the Court (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of Court). On a date in mid-2013 the complaint concerning Article 3 was communicated to the Government and the remainder of the application was declared inadmissible pursuant to Rule 54 § 3 of the Rules of Court. In its judgment issued in early 2015, a Chamber of that Section composed of a panel of Judges and also of a Deputy Section Registrar, declared by a majority the applicant’s Article 3 complaint admissible. The Chamber also held, by six votes to one, that there had been no violation of Article 3. A dissenting opinion of one of the Judges was appended to the judgment.

On a date in early summer of that year, pursuant to a request by the applicant dated earlier that year, a Panel of the Grand Chamber decided to refer the case to the Grand Chamber in accordance with Article 43 of the Convention.

The composition of the Grand Chamber was determined according to the provisions of Article 26 §§ 4 and 5 of the Convention and Rule 24 of the Rules of Court.

The applicant and the Government each filed written observations (Rule 59 § 1 of the Rules of Court) on the merits. In addition, third-party comments were received from a relevant non-governmental organisation, which had been granted leave to intervene in the written procedure (Article 36 § 2 of the Convention and Rule 44 § 3 of the Rules of Court). The Government replied to these comments in the course of their oral submissions at the hearing (Rule 44 § 6 of the Rules of Court).

A hearing took place in public at the Court's designated venue, on a date in late 2015 (Rule 59 § 3 of the Rules of Court). There appeared before the Court: (a) for the Government their Agent, the Attorney-General, other counsel, and advisers; (b) for the applicant the applicant's counsel, another counsel, and an adviser. The Court heard addresses by the Government’s representative and the applicant’s representative, and their answers to questions put by the Court.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the early 1940s and is detained in a correctional facility.

In late 1983, the applicant broke into a family home, where he stabbed to death a man, his wife and their adult son. He then repeatedly raped their young adult daughter, having first dragged her past her father’s body. He was arrested several weeks later and charged with these offences. At trial he pleaded not guilty, denying the killings and claiming that the sexual intercourse had been consensual. In late 1984, he was convicted of three counts of murder, rape, and aggravated burglary.

The trial judge sentenced the applicant to a term of life imprisonment and, in accordance with the rules on sentencing then in force, recommended a minimum period (tariff) of 18 years to the Secretary of State for the Home Office. When asked to give his opinion again in early 1988, the judge wrote that “for the requirements of retribution and general deterrence this is genuinely a life case”. A few days later in early 1988 the Lord Chief Justice recommended that the period should be set at a whole life term stating that “I do not think that this man should ever be released, quite apart from the risk which would be involved”. In late 1994, the Secretary of State informed the applicant that he had decided to impose a whole life term.

Following the entry into force of the Criminal Justice Act 2003, the applicant applied to the High Court for a review of his sentence, arguing that he should receive the 18-year tariff mentioned at his trial. In mid-2008, the High Court gave its judgment. It found that there was no reason to depart from the Secretary of State’s decision. The seriousness of the offences alone was such that the starting point was a whole life order. A number of very serious aggravating factors were present, and no mitigating factors. In late 2008, the Court of Appeal dismissed the applicant’s appeal.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in a specific application number against the respondent State, lodged with the European Court of Human Rights under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a national of the respondent State, the applicant, in late April of a particular year. The applicant was represented by a legal representative practising in a major city.

Several years later, in November of a subsequent year, the Court declared the application partly inadmissible and decided to communicate the complaint concerning the length of the criminal proceedings to the respondent Government. It also decided to rule on the admissibility and merits of the application at the same time (Article 29 § 3).

THE FACTS

THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the mid-1960s and lives in a major city in the country.

In late 1990 the applicant, who served as a first lieutenant in the national armed forces at the material time, was arrested on suspicion of, inter alia, professional misconduct and bribery.

In early 1991 a military prosecutor at a specific infantry division commander's office filed an indictment charging the applicant with numerous different offences, including professional misconduct, bribery and assault and battery of his inferiors.

In mid-1993 a military court acquitted the applicant in relation to certain charges and found him guilty of the remaining offences.

Early the following year the military court of appeal upheld the judgment of the first-instance court in relation to certain offences and quashed the remainder of the judgment on grounds of, inter alia, insufficient investigation.

Late in that year a military court acquitted the applicant in relation to certain charges and found him guilty as charged for the remaining offences.

Early the next year the military court of appeal partially upheld the first-instance court's judgment.

A few weeks later a military court once again acquitted the applicant in relation to certain charges and found him guilty of the remaining offences.

In mid-1997 the applicant was expelled from the armed forces.

The following year in June, the military court of appeal quashed the judgment of the first-instance court on procedural grounds in relation to certain charges and for being time-barred (zamanaşımı) in relation to the remainder.

In mid-1999 a local military court found the applicant guilty as charged in relation to those offences which were not time-barred and of which he had not already been acquitted.

Early in 2001 the military court of appeal quashed the judgment of the first-instance court once again in the light of a recent amendment to the relevant jurisdictional rules, and held that the case should be heard by ordinary criminal courts.

Late in 2001 a local military court issued a decision of lack of jurisdiction and referred the case to a local assize court.

Late in 2003 a local assize court decided to discontinue the proceedings against the applicant as the prosecution had become time-barred.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in a specific application number against the respondent State, lodged with the European Court of Human Rights under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a national of the respondent State, the applicant, in late April of a particular year. The applicant was represented by a legal representative practising in a major city.

Several years later, in November of a subsequent year, the Court declared the application partly inadmissible and decided to communicate the complaint concerning the length of the criminal proceedings to the respondent Government. It also decided to rule on the admissibility and merits of the application at the same time (Article 29 § 3).

THE FACTS

THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the mid-1960s and lives in a major city in the country.

In late 1990 the applicant, who served as a first lieutenant in the national armed forces at the material time, was arrested on suspicion of, inter alia, professional misconduct and bribery.

In early 1991 a military prosecutor at a specific infantry division commander's office filed an indictment charging the applicant with numerous different offences, including professional misconduct, bribery and assault and battery of his inferiors.

In mid-1993 a military court acquitted the applicant in relation to certain charges and found him guilty of the remaining offences.

Early the following year the military court of appeal upheld the judgment of the first-instance court in relation to certain offences and quashed the remainder of the judgment on grounds of, inter alia, insufficient investigation.

Late in that year a military court acquitted the applicant in relation to certain charges and found him guilty as charged for the remaining offences.

Early the next year the military court of appeal partially upheld the first-instance court's judgment.

A few weeks later a military court once again acquitted the applicant in relation to certain charges and found him guilty of the remaining offences.

In mid-1997 the applicant was expelled from the armed forces.

The following year in June, the military court of appeal quashed the judgment of the first-instance court on procedural grounds in relation to certain charges and for being time-barred (zamanaşımı) in relation to the remainder.

In mid-1999 a local military court found the applicant guilty as charged in relation to those offences which were not time-barred and of which he had not already been acquitted.

Early in 2001 the military court of appeal quashed the judgment of the first-instance court once again in the light of a recent amendment to the relevant jurisdictional rules, and held that the case should be heard by ordinary criminal courts.

Late in 2001 a local military court issued a decision of lack of jurisdiction and referred the case to a local assize court.

Late in 2003 a local assize court decided to discontinue the proceedings against the applicant as the prosecution had become time-barred.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a particular application number) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, the applicant, in mid-1998.

The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, an agent for the Government, of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

The application was transmitted to the Court several months later in that year, when Protocol No. 11 to the Convention came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

Some years later, in late 2001, the Fourth Section decided to communicate the application to the Government. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

The applicant was born in the late 1920s and lives in Warsaw.

In early 1992 the applicant filed with the Warsaw Regional Court (sąd wojewódzki) a petition in which he requested the annulment of a judgment delivered in 1947 in criminal proceedings against him, submitting that they had been of a political nature. He also claimed compensation.

In early 1993 the court annulled that judgment. However, its decision did not relate to the compensation claim.

A few days later that month the applicant filed another compensation claim.

Subsequently, he lodged several complaints concerning the inactivity of the court, but to no avail.

In her letters of late 1993 and mid-1994, in reply to the applicant's complaints, a judge of the Regional Court informed the applicant that the delay in the examination of his compensation claim was caused by a large number of similar requests lodged with that court and the dearth of judges.

In early 1995 the court held a hearing. A public prosecutor requested that the proceedings be stayed, submitting that a part of the Regional Court's decision of early 1993 was invalid and required supplementation. Subsequently, the court stayed the proceedings.

The hearing scheduled for a month later was adjourned at the applicant's request.

A couple of weeks after that, the Regional Court asked the Office for the State Protection (Urząd Ochrony Państwa) to submit certain documents. The documents were submitted to the court almost a year later.

A few months after the documents were submitted, the proceedings were resumed.

The court adjourned the hearing scheduled for early 1997, as the proceedings concerning the annulment of the criminal judgment against the applicant given in 1947 had not been completed.

Some years later, in mid-2000, the Warsaw Regional Court gave judgment. It awarded the applicant a certain sum of money in compensation. He did not lodge an appeal against that judgment.

results.judged_text:
The case originated in an application (a specific case number) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, the applicant, on a date in mid-August of a particular year.

The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, a representative of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

On a date in April of a subsequent year the President of the Fourth Section decided to communicate the complaint concerning the monitoring of the applicant’s correspondence to the Government. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it was decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the late 1940s and lives in a city in the country, Poland.

A. Criminal proceedings against the applicant

In mid-July of a particular year the applicant was arrested on suspicion of robbery. The following day the local District Court ordered that the applicant be remanded in custody until mid-October of that year. It found that there were reasonable grounds – in particular, evidence from witnesses – for suspecting him of the offence charged. The court further noted that the applicant was liable to a statutory maximum sentence of at least a decade's imprisonment. It also found that there was a risk that the applicant might tamper with evidence.

In early October of that same year the local District Court prolonged the applicant’s detention until mid-January of the next year. It considered that the reasons for keeping him in detention were still valid and the need to secure the proper conduct of the investigation justified holding him in custody. It referred to the likelihood of a severe sentence of imprisonment being imposed on the applicant. It also found that there was a risk that the applicant might tamper with evidence, given the nature of the charges against him and the fact that he had acted with other co-accused.

The applicant’s detention was subsequently prolonged by the District Court on unspecified dates. The courts’ decisions were based on Article 258 § 1 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, which lists grounds for pre-trial detention, such as, the risk that an accused will abscond or go into hiding or a justified fear that an accused will attempt to induce witnesses or co-defendants to give false testimony or to obstruct the proper course of proceedings by any other unlawful means.

In late October of a later year the local District Court convicted the applicant of two counts of robbery and sentenced him to several years’ imprisonment. The applicant appealed.

In late September of a subsequent year the Regional Court upheld the impugned judgment.

The applicant did not lodge a cassation appeal.

B. Censorship of the applicant’s correspondence

The applicant’s letter to the Court dated in late November of the first year mentioned bears a stamp marked “censored”, “the prosecutor” followed by an illegible signature. It appears that the envelope in which that letter was sent had been cut open and subsequently resealed with adhesive tape. According to the postmark the letter was posted in early December of that same year.

The application form submitted by the applicant and dated in mid-January of the next year bears a stamp marked “censored”, “the judge” followed by an illegible signature. The envelope bears a stamp confirming that the applicant’s letter was received for dispatch by the prison administration a few days later. However, according to the postmark the letter was not posted until in late January of that same year. It also appears that the envelope was cut open and subsequently resealed with adhesive tape.

results.judged_text:
The cases originated in applications (specific application numbers) against the respondent State lodged with the international tribunal under Article 34 of the international human rights Convention (“the Convention”) by two nationals of the respondent State, the first applicant and the second applicant (“the applicants”), in mid-2006.

The applicants were represented by their legal counsel, a practitioner in a particular region. The national authorities (“the national authorities”) were represented by their Agent, a representative from a governmental department.

Approximately a year later, the President of a specific section of the international tribunal decided to give notice of the applications to the national authorities. It was also decided to rule on the admissibility and merits of the applications at the same time (Article 29 § 3).

THE FACTS

I THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicants were born in the late 1940s and the mid-1950s, respectively, and live in a specific city in the respondent State.

A. Civil proceedings for payment

In the late 1990s, the applicants filed a claim with the local first-instance court against a residential association concerning the level of maintenance fees to be fixed in respect of their apartment building.

A year later, the proceedings were stayed pending the termination of parallel proceedings relating to the alleged nullity of an amendment made to the residential association’s statute. The local first-instance court resumed the proceedings several years thereafter.

A couple of months later, a hearing was held. The court requested the regional appellate court to provide the case-file of the proceedings relating to the alleged nullity of an amendment made to the residential association’s statute.

Shortly after that, the local first-instance court decided to stay the proceedings at the applicants’ request. They were resumed, upon the applicants’ request, a year and a half later.

Throughout the subsequent period, multiple hearings were conducted.

Early in the following period, the local first-instance court decided to admit an expert opinion. During the middle months of that same period, the expert requested prolongation of the time-limit for submission of the opinion.

Later that autumn in the same period, the court summoned the expert to submit the opinion within a specific short timeframe.

In a further subsequent period, the lower court held multiple hearings.

In the autumn of that further subsequent period, the local first-instance court gave a judgment in the case.

The judgment was quashed early in an even later period by the regional appellate court and the case remitted for re-examination.

The proceedings are currently pending before the trial court.

B. Proceedings under the relevant legal act from an earlier period

Late in a particular period, the applicants lodged a complaint under the Law enacted in a prior period on complaints about a breach of the right to a trial within a reasonable time (Ustawa o skardze na naruszenie prawa strony do rozpoznania sprawy w postępowaniu sądowym bez nieuzasadnionej zwłoki) (“the relevant legal act from an earlier period”). Early in the following period, the regional appellate court dismissed the applicants’ complaint, finding that the trial court could not be held responsible for any delays and that the proceedings had been prolonged as it had been necessary to stay them.

results.judge_summary: The rewrite successfully anonymized direct names and most specific dates/durations but leaks highly specific contextual information about the type of state, international and supranational organizations, the individual's political background, and the specific outcome of the lustration proceedings, which could lead to re-identification.

judge_leaks.rationale: The explicit name 'Stanisław Jałowiecki' has been successfully removed and replaced with 'the party concerned'.
The birth year '1946' has been generalized to 'mid-1940s', which is considered an acceptable generalization.
The specific year '1973' for collaboration meetings has been generalized to 'early 1970s', which is acceptable as per guidelines (not an exact date and generalized).
The duration '1985 and 1994' has been generalized to 'mid-1980s and mid-1990s', which is acceptable as per guidelines (not exact years and generalized).

The year the lustration law entered into force ('1997') has been anonymized to 'late summer of a particular year'. The 'early 1990s' mentioned refers to the historical period of collaboration, not the law's enactment or the application's earliest year.

judge_leaks.inferred_value: A former communist state in Central or Eastern Europe, now a member of the Council of Europe.
judge_leaks.rationale: The presence of 'lustration proceedings', 'state security services during a specific historical period (from the mid-20th century to the early 1990s)', and the application to an 'international human rights tribunal under Article 34' strongly implies a former communist state in Central or Eastern Europe that is now a member of the Council of Europe (where the ECHR applies).
The specific foreign city 'Munich' has been replaced with 'a foreign city', which is sufficiently anonymized.
The phrase 'lodged with the international human rights tribunal under Article 34 of the relevant international instrument' is a direct and strong identifier for the European Court of Human Rights.
The specific media organization 'Radio Free Europe' has been replaced with 'a media organization broadcasting internationally to the region from a foreign city', which is sufficiently anonymized.

The description 'legislative body at a supranational level' in the context of European human rights law and a former communist state strongly implies the European Parliament, making it highly inferable.
