results.judged_text:
The case originated in an application with a specific case number against a Member State, lodged with the international human rights tribunal under Article 34 of the relevant international instrument by a national of the respondent State, an individual referred to as "the party concerned," in late summer of a particular year.

The party concerned was represented by legal counsel practising in a city in the respondent State. The national authorities of the respondent State were represented by their appointed agent from a ministry responsible for external relations.

The party concerned alleged, inter alia, that the lustration proceedings in their case had been unfair, in violation of Article 6 of the human rights treaty.

In late winter of a subsequent year, the presiding official of a specific judicial section decided to give notice of the application to the national authorities. It was also decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility (Article 29 § 3).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The party concerned was born in the mid-1940s and lives in a location in the respondent State.

In late summer of a particular year, a national law concerning the disclosure of past collaboration with state security services during a specific historical period (from the mid-20th century to the early 1990s) entered into force.

In the spring of a later year, the party concerned, a candidate for a legislative body at a supranational level, declared that they had not collaborated with the former state security apparatus. They were subsequently elected as a member of that legislative body.

In late autumn of that same year, a regional appellate court in the capital city decided to institute lustration proceedings against the party concerned following a request made by the public prosecutor's office on the grounds that the party concerned had submitted an untrue declaration by denying cooperation with the former security agencies.

In early spring of a subsequent year, the regional appellate court, acting as the first-instance specialized lustration tribunal, found that the party concerned had submitted an untrue lustration declaration since they had been an intentional and secret collaborator with the former state security apparatus. The tribunal established that the party concerned had met on probably two occasions in the early 1970s with agents of the former security agencies and had agreed to help them in an advisory capacity.

The party concerned appealed against the decision. They submitted that they had never collaborated with the former security agencies and the meeting in question had been in connection with the preparation of an article. The party concerned also referred to their subsequent activity in a prominent social and political movement for democratic change, for which they had been persecuted and detained by the former regime's authorities. Subsequently, they were allowed to leave the country and between the mid-1980s and mid-1990s they held a representative role for a media organization broadcasting internationally to the region from a foreign city.

In early summer of that same year, the regional appellate court, acting as the second-instance specialized lustration tribunal, upheld the impugned judgment.

In winter of the following year, the highest court of appeal in the country dismissed the party concerned's cassation appeal. That decision was notified to the party concerned in early spring of the same year.
results.final_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. XXX/YY) against a Member State lodged with an international human rights tribunal under a provision of a major international human rights convention (“the convention”) by a national of that State, the applicant, in late summer of a particular year.

The applicant was represented by a legal representative practicing in a city within the country. The national authorities were represented by their Agent, from a relevant government department.

The applicant alleged, inter alia, that the lustration proceedings in their case had been unfair, in violation of a relevant provision of the convention.

In late winter of a later year, the President of a specific Section of the tribunal decided to give notice of the application to the national authorities. It was also decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility (Article 29 § 3).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the mid-1940s and resides in a specific locality within the country.

In late summer of a prior year, a national law enacted around that time on disclosing work for or service in the former state security apparatus or collaboration with them between the mid-1940s and the early 1990s by persons exercising public functions (with its original title in the national language) (the “national Lustration Act”) entered into force.

In the spring of a subsequent year, the applicant, a candidate for a representative political body, declared that they had not collaborated with the former regime's state security apparatus. They were subsequently elected as an elected representative.

In late autumn of a later year, a national appellate court decided to institute lustration proceedings against the applicant following a request made by a public interest commissioner on the grounds that the applicant had lied in their lustration declaration by denying that they had cooperated with the state intelligence agencies.

In early winter of a subsequent year, the national appellate court, acting as the first‑instance lustration tribunal, found that the applicant had submitted an untrue lustration declaration since they had been an intentional and secret collaborator with the former state security apparatus. The tribunal established that the applicant had met on probably two occasions in the early 1970s with agents of the state intelligence agencies and had agreed to help them as a consultant.

The applicant appealed against the decision. They submitted that they had never collaborated with the state intelligence agencies and the meeting in question had been in connection with the preparation of an article. The applicant also referred to their subsequent activity in a prominent social and political movement of the era, for which they had faced persecution and detention by the former regime's authorities. Subsequently, they were permitted to leave the country and for a period between the mid-1980s and mid-1990s, they held a representative role with an international media organization broadcasting to the region, based in a city abroad.

In early summer of that year, the national appellate court, acting as the second‑instance lustration tribunal, upheld the impugned judgment.

In early spring of a later year, the highest national court dismissed the applicant’s cassation appeal. That decision was notified to the applicant in early spring of the same year.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (the aforementioned case) against a Member State lodged with the international Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a national of that State, the applicant, in late 2003.

The State's Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, a representative from a government ministry.

In early October several years later, the President of a Section of the international Court decided to give notice of the application to the Government. Applying Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it was decided to rule on the admissibility and merits of the application at the same time.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the mid-1940s and lives in a specific locality.

A. Main proceedings

In late 1993 the applicant divorced.

In mid-1994 she lodged with a local trial court a claim for division of matrimonial property consisting of a family house in another specific locality and a right to a flat in a housing-co-operative in a major city.

Between late 1994 and mid-2000, the local trial court scheduled numerous hearings, several of which were adjourned and an initial expert report was obtained.

In late 2000 the court issued an interim order (zarządzenie tymczasowe) securing the applicant’s claim. Shortly thereafter in late 2000 the defendant appealed against the making of the order. In mid-2001 the court amended its order.

At a hearing held in late 2001 the local trial court gave a preliminary decision (postanowienie wstępne) on the parties’ respective shares in the matrimonial property.

The applicant and the defendant appealed in early 2002, respectively.

In early 2003 the regional appellate court amended the preliminary decision and dismissed the defendant’s appeal. His cassation appeal was rejected in mid-2003 on procedural grounds.

In early 2004 the court ordered that an expert report be obtained. The report was submitted in mid-2004.

In late 2005 the court held a hearing.

In early 2006 the court ordered that a further expert report be obtained.

At a hearing held in late 2006 the applicant’s legal representative made a friendly-settlement proposal. The court stayed the proceedings.

In mid-2007 the local court resumed the proceedings and delivered a partial decision (postanowienie częściowe) in the case, concerning the composition of the matrimonial property. The defendant appealed against this decision. In early 2008 the regional appellate court rejected the defendant’s appeal.

The proceedings are still pending.

B. Proceedings under a law enacted in the mid-2000s

In late 2004 the applicant lodged with the regional appellate court a complaint under section 5 of a relevant law from the mid-2000s on complaints about a breach of the right to a trial within a reasonable time (Ustawa o skardze na naruszenie prawa strony do rozpoznania sprawy w postępowaniu sądowym bez nieuzasadnionej zwłoki) (“the relevant Act”).

She sought a ruling declaring that the length of the proceedings before the local trial court had been excessive and an award of just satisfaction in the amount of a significant sum in local currency (equivalent to a lesser sum in a common international currency).

In early 2005 the regional appellate court dismissed her complaint in the part alleging, to the highest court. The court observed that the proceedings had indeed been lengthy but it was the applicant’s former spouse who had been responsible for the delay in examination of the case. The court categorically stated that the local trial court was not responsible for the protracted length of the proceedings.
results.final_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. [APPLICATION NUMBER]) against the respondent State lodged with the international human rights court under Article 34 of the relevant human rights convention (“the said convention”) by a national of the respondent State, the applicant, in late 2003.

The respondent Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, an official from the state administration.

Several years later, the President of a particular Section of the international human rights court decided to give notice of the application to the respondent State's representatives. Applying Article 29 § 3 of the convention, it was decided to rule on the admissibility and merits of the application at the same time.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the mid-1940s and lives in a locality in the region.

A. Main proceedings

In late 1993 the applicant divorced.

Several months later she lodged with a regional court of first instance a claim for division of matrimonial property consisting of a residential property in a different locality and a right to a flat in a housing-co-operative in a major city in the region.

Between late that year and mid-2000, the court of first instance scheduled numerous hearings, nine of which were adjourned and an expert report was obtained.

Late that year the court issued an interim order (zarządzenie tymczasowe) securing the applicant’s claim. Later that month the defendant appealed against the making of the order. Several months into the following year the court amended its order.

At a hearing held late in that year the court of first instance gave a preliminary decision (postanowienie wstępne) on the parties’ respective shares in the matrimonial property.

The applicant and the defendant appealed early the following year and shortly thereafter, respectively.

Early the next year a regional appellate court amended the preliminary decision and dismissed the defendant’s appeal. His cassation appeal was rejected several months later on procedural grounds.

Early in a particular year the court ordered that an expert report be obtained. The report was submitted several months later in the same year.

Late in a subsequent year the court held a hearing.

Early in a later year the court ordered that another expert report be obtained.

At a hearing held late in that same year the applicant’s legal representative made a friendly-settlement proposal. The court stayed the proceedings.

Mid-year in a subsequent period the court of first instance resumed the proceedings and delivered a partial decision (postanowienie częściowe) in the case, concerning the composition of the matrimonial property. The defendant appealed against this decision. Early the following year the regional appellate court rejected the defendant’s appeal.

The proceedings are still pending.

B. Proceedings under a specific Act concerning trial length

Late in the initial year mentioned the applicant lodged with the regional appellate court a complaint under section 5 of the Law on a particular date that year on complaints about a breach of the right to a trial within a reasonable time (Law on complaints about a breach of the right to a trial within a reasonable time) (“the said Act”).

She sought a ruling declaring that the length of the proceedings before the court of first instance had been excessive and an award of just satisfaction in the amount of a significant monetary amount in local currency (a corresponding amount in a major international currency).

Early the following year the regional appellate court dismissed her complaintin the part alleging., to the highest national court. The court observed that the proceedings had indeed been lengthy but it was the applicant’s former spouse who had been responsible for the delay in examination of the case. The court categorically stated that the court of first instance was not responsible for the protracted length of the proceedings.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a specific case number) against the respondent State lodged with the international tribunal under Article 34 of the Convention on human rights (“the Convention”) by a national from the respondent State, identified as the claimant, in late August of a particular year.

The respondent State's representatives were represented by an agent from a governmental department.

In early December of a later year, the international tribunal decided to give notice of the application to the respondent State.

Applying Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it decided to rule on the admissibility and merits of the application at the same time.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The claimant was born in the mid-1930s and resides in the capital city of the respondent State.

In late November of a specific year, the claimant, who is an individual engaged in a public transport profession, was involved in a traffic accident.

In late February of the following year, the claimant was charged with causing the accident. In early September of a subsequent year, the claimant was served with a bill of indictment.

The hearing scheduled for mid-March of yet another year was postponed due to the judicial officer’s illness. In mid-July several years later, the local court in the capital held the first hearing in the case. The hearing scheduled for late September of the same year was adjourned due to the witnesses’ absence.

In early December of that year, the court ordered an expert opinion. In late April of the next year, the expert submitted their opinion to the court. The court ordered a supplementary opinion.

The hearing scheduled for mid-September of that year was postponed. At the hearing held in early November of the same year, the local court in the capital issued a judgment. The claimant appealed.

In early June of the following year, the regional appellate court in the capital overturned the first-instance judgment and sent the case back.

Following the remittal, in late September of a subsequent year, the local court in the capital issued a judgment. The court sentenced the claimant to a specific term of imprisonment, suspended for a probationary period of several years. The claimant appealed.

The claimant filed a complaint with the regional appellate court in the capital under the Act of mid-June of a particular year on complaints about a breach of the right to a trial within a reasonable time (“the Act concerning timely trials”). In mid-November of the same year, the regional appellate court in the capital issued a decision and confirmed that the proceedings had indeed been lengthy. However, the court did not grant any compensation to the claimant.

In late April of the following year, the regional appellate court in the capital held an appeal hearing and issued a judgment. The judgment is final.

In early June of that year, the claimant again filed a complaint with the highest national judicial body under the Act concerning timely trials, alleging that a copy of the final judgment had not yet been served. The highest national judicial body rejected the complaint, on an unspecified later date, as the claimant had never asked to be served with a copy of the final judgment.

In late February of a later year, the claimant for the third time filed a complaint with the regional appellate court in the capital under the Act concerning timely trials. In late September of the same year, the court declined to address the merits of the complaint. The court held that the proceedings had already concluded and therefore the complaint was without merit.
results.final_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a specific case number) against the respondent State lodged with the international tribunal under Article 34 of the Convention on human rights (“the Convention”) by a national from the respondent State, identified as the claimant, in late August of a particular year.

The respondent State's representatives were represented by an agent from a governmental department.

In early December of a later year, the international tribunal decided to give notice of the application to the respondent State.

Applying Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it decided to rule on the admissibility and merits of the application at the same time.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The claimant was born in the mid-1930s and resides in the capital city of the respondent State.

In late November of a specific year, the claimant, who is an individual engaged in a public transport profession, was involved in a traffic accident.

In late February of the following year, the claimant was charged with causing the accident. In early September of a subsequent year, the claimant was served with a bill of indictment.

The hearing scheduled for mid-March of yet another year was postponed due to the judicial officer’s illness. In mid-July several years later, the local court in the capital held the first hearing in the case. The hearing scheduled for late September of the same year was adjourned due to the witnesses’ absence.

In early December of that year, the court ordered an expert opinion. In late April of the next year, the expert submitted their opinion to the court. The court ordered a supplementary opinion.

The hearing scheduled for mid-September of that year was postponed. At the hearing held in early November of the same year, the local court in the capital issued a judgment. The claimant appealed.

In early June of the following year, the regional appellate court in the capital overturned the first-instance judgment and sent the case back.

Following the remittal, in late September of a subsequent year, the local court in the capital issued a judgment. The court sentenced the claimant to a specific term of imprisonment, suspended for a probationary period of several years. The claimant appealed.

The claimant filed a complaint with the regional appellate court in the capital under the Act of mid-June of a particular year on complaints about a breach of the right to a trial within a reasonable time (“the Act concerning timely trials”). In mid-November of the same year, the regional appellate court in the capital issued a decision and confirmed that the proceedings had indeed been lengthy. However, the court did not grant any compensation to the claimant.

In late April of the following year, the regional appellate court in the capital held an appeal hearing and issued a judgment. The judgment is final.

In early June of that year, the claimant again filed a complaint with the highest national judicial body under the Act concerning timely trials, alleging that a copy of the final judgment had not yet been served. The highest national judicial body rejected the complaint, on an unspecified later date, as the claimant had never asked to be served with a copy of the final judgment.

In late February of a later year, the claimant for the third time filed a complaint with the regional appellate court in the capital under the Act concerning timely trials. In late September of the same year, the court declined to address the merits of the complaint. The court held that the proceedings had already concluded and therefore the complaint was without merit.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in a specific application number against the respondent State lodged with the adjudicating body under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a national of that State, the applicant, on an initial filing date.

The representatives of the respondent State were represented by their appointed agent from a relevant governmental department.

The applicant alleged that a delay in releasing him from a correctional facility had given rise to a violation of Article 5.

On a later date, the President of the relevant Section decided to give notice of the application to the national authorities. Under the provisions of Article 29 § a particular paragraph of the Convention, it was decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the mid-1950s and lives in a specific locality.

The applicant was serving a prison sentence in a particular correctional facility following multiple separate convictions. The overall term of his imprisonment was due to come to an end early in an unspecified year.

Pursuant to the provisions of the Criminal Code applicable to re-offenders, the applicant became eligible for conditional release after having served a significant portion of the overall term of his imprisonment, namely early in an earlier unspecified year. After that date, the applicant requested a judicial body on several occasions to grant him conditional release. However, his requests were denied on grounds of insufficient progress in his rehabilitation. On a subsequent date, the facility's director requested the regional court to order the applicant's release (warunkowe przedterminowe zwolnienie) in view of his satisfactory rehabilitation. The regional public prosecutor objected to that request.

A few days later, the regional court gave a decision ordering the applicant's conditional release for a multi-year probationary period. It placed the applicant under a supervising official's supervision and imposed certain other obligations on him.

The regional public prosecutor appealed against that decision. This judicial body did not amend its decision and transmitted the public prosecutor's appeal to the higher regional court.

The hearing before the appellate body was scheduled for a specified morning. On that day, the higher regional court upheld the decision of the initial judicial body. No further appeal lay against the decision of the appellate body. In the applicant's submission, the appellate body's decision was delivered early in the day.

Later on that same day, a certified copy of the appellate body's decision was sent to the regional court, which received it the following day. On the same day, the regional court ordered the director of the correctional facility to release the applicant and served him with a copy of the appellate body's decision. The director received these documents on a subsequent day.

According to the official release certificate, the applicant was released on that later date. The applicant submitted that he had been released on that date in the afternoon.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a specific application number) against a certain nation lodged with the adjudicating body under Article 34 of a relevant international human rights instrument ("the instrument") by a national of that country, the applicant, in early April of a particular year.

The applicant was represented by a legal representative practicing in a city in the region. The national government ("the Government") were represented by their Agent, from a relevant government department in the capital city.

The applicant invoked Articles 8 and 13 of the instrument in relation to the covert recording by law enforcement of conversations in his residence.

The application was allocated to a section of the adjudicating body (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of the adjudicating body). Within that Section, the judicial panel that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the instrument) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

By a decision of mid-July of a later year, the adjudicating body declared the application partly admissible.

Neither party filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1). The judicial panel decided, after consulting the parties, that no hearing on the merits was required (Rule 59 § 3 in fine).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant is a national of the respondent state, who was born in the mid-1940s and is currently in detention in a correctional facility.

The applicant and his wife frequently travelled between various countries. In mid-April of a particular year, a senior law enforcement official from a regional police force authorised the installation of covert recording devices at the applicant's residence and this was carried out a few days later. Authority for a period of several weeks' surveillance was granted in mid-May of that year.

Many hours of tapes were obtained between the period of late April to early June of that year, on which latter date the applicant and his wife were arrested. The transcripts of the taped conversations made up the bulk of the case against the applicant.

At trial, the applicant's defence alleged that the recorded discussions, which occurred while he and the others concerned were in an impaired state, were "ramblings under altered consciousness" and challenged their admissibility. A "voir dire" took place from late May to early June of a subsequent year, during which the defence made submissions concerning the procedure for authorisation of the surveillance and seeking inter alia to exclude irrelevant or prejudicial material under sections 78 of relevant national legislation concerning criminal procedure. By agreement, the transcript evidence was reduced from five to two files and an agreed schedule was adopted by all counsel concerning the occasions on which the conversations occurred concurrently with the consumption of substances. In summing up to the jury at the conclusion of the trial, the presiding judicial officer directed their attention to the fact that the supposedly incriminating statements taped at the residence were or may have been the product of the participants' altered mental state and that it was for them to assess whether despite their impairment the defendants were expressing rational, genuine thoughts, real ideas, plans or arrangements.

In mid-June of a subsequent year, the applicant was convicted of serious criminal offenses, including involvement in the importation of prohibited substances. He was sentenced to a substantial prison term. A confiscation order was imposed on him in late September of that year in the sum of a significant sum of money.

In early November of a later year, a single judicial officer of an appellate court refused an extension of time to appeal against sentence and refused leave to appeal against conviction, noting that no satisfactory or sufficient reason had been given for the period of nearly a year delay in lodging the application. The applicant's renewed application was refused by the appellate court in mid-October of a subsequent year.
results.final_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (with a specific application number) against the United Kingdom lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a national of that country, the applicant, on a specific date in early April of that year.

The applicant was represented by a solicitor practising in a city in the country. The Government were represented by their Agent, a representative of the Government's foreign affairs department, located in the capital city.

The applicant invoked Articles 8 and 13 of the Convention in relation to the covert recording by the police of conversations in his cottage.

The application was allocated to the Fourth Section of the Court (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of Court). Within that Section, the Chamber that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

By a decision made in early July of a later year, the Court declared the application partly admissible.

Neither party filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1). The Chamber decided, after consulting the parties, that no hearing on the merits was required (Rule 59 § 3 in fine).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant is a national of that country, who was born in the mid-1940s and is currently serving a sentence of imprisonment in a correctional facility.

The applicant and his wife frequently travelled between various countries. In mid-April of a particular year, the Chief Constable of a regional police force authorised the installation of covert recording devices at the applicant's cottage and this was carried out later that month. Authority for a period of several weeks' surveillance was granted in mid-May of that year.

Many hours of tapes were obtained between late April to early June of that same year, on which latter date the applicant and his wife were arrested. The transcripts of the taped conversations made up the bulk of the case against the applicant.

At trial, the applicant's defence alleged that the recorded discussions, which occurred while he and the others concerned were under the influence of drugs, were “drug-crazed ramblings” and challenged their admissibility. A “voir dire” took place from late May to early June of the following year, during which the defence made submissions concerning the procedure for authorisation of the surveillance and seeking inter alia to exclude irrelevant or prejudicial material under sections 78 of the Police and Criminal Evidence Act 1984 (PACE). By agreement, the transcript evidence was reduced to a reduced number of files and an agreed schedule was adopted by all counsel concerning the occasions on which the conversations occurred at the same time as drug taking. In summing up to the jury at the conclusion of the trial, the judge directed their attention to the fact that the supposedly incriminating statements taped at the cottage were or may have been the product of the participants' intoxicated state and that it was for them to assess whether despite the drugs the defendants were expressing rational, genuine thoughts, real ideas, plans or arrangements.

In mid-June of that year, the applicant was convicted of charges inter alia of conspiracy to import controlled drugs and possession of controlled drugs in connection with importation of marijuana and cocaine from overseas. He was sentenced to a lengthy prison sentence. A confiscation order was imposed on him in late September of that year in a substantial sum.

In early November of a subsequent year, a single judge of the Court of Appeal refused an extension of time to appeal against sentence and refused leave to appeal against conviction, noting that no satisfactory or sufficient reason had been given for a significant delay in lodging the application. The applicant's renewed application was refused by the Court of Appeal in mid-October of the year after that.

results.judged_text:
PROCEOURE

The case was referred to the Court by the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission“) in early 1993, within a specified period of several months laid down in Article 32 para. 1 and Article 47 (art. 32-1, art. 47) of the Convention. It originated in a specific application number against the respondent state lodged with the Commission under Article 25 (art. 25) by a citizen of the respondent state, the applicant, in mid-1988. The Commission’s request referred to Articles 44 and 48 (art. 44, art. 48) and to the declaration whereby the respondent state recognised the compulsory jurisdiction of the Court (Article 46) (art. 46). The object of the request was to obtain a decision as to whether the facts of the case disclosed a breach by the respondent state of its obligations under Article 6 para. 1 (art. 6-1).

In response to the enquiry made in accordance with Rule 33 para. 3 (d) of the Rules of Court, the applicant stated that they wished to take part in the proceedings. The presiding official granted them leave to present their own case (Rule 30).

The Chamber to be constituted included ex officio an elected judge of the respondent state's nationality (Article 43 of the Convention) (art. 43), and the President of the Court (Rule 21 para. 3 (b)). Later that month the Vice-President drew by lot, in the presence of the court registrar, the names of the other seven members, namely several other judicial officials (Article 43 in fine of the Convention and Rule 21 para. 4) (art. 43).

As President of the Chamber (Rule 21 para. 5), the presiding judge, through the court registrar, consulted the Agent of the government of the respondent state (“the respondent government“), the applicant and the Commission's delegate on the organisation of the proceedings (Rules 37 para. 1 and 38). Pursuant to the order made in consequence, the court registrar received the respondent government’s memorial in late summer of that year and the applicant’s memorial a month later. In mid-September the Commission secretary informed the court registrar that the delegate did not intend to file a memorial in reply. In early and mid-October of that year the Commission produced a number of documents, as requested by the court registrar on the presiding official’s instructions, and the applicant submitted their claims under Article 50 (art. 50).

In accordance with the presiding official’s decision, the hearing took place in public in a designated building in the city where the Court is located, later that month. The Court had held a preparatory meeting beforehand. There appeared before the Court:

for the respondent government a legal adviser from a relevant ministry of the respondent state, Agent, another legal adviser from a relevant ministry of the respondent state, Adviser;

for the Commission the Commission's delegate;

the applicant. The Court heard addresses by a legal adviser for the respondent government, the Commission's delegate and the applicant.

AS TO THE FACTS

I. THE PARTICULAR CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

A. Introduction

The applicant, a university lecturer in law, is a citizen of the respondent state and lives in a city in the respondent state.

From around the early 1980s the applicant held a power of attorney from their adoptive mother, and, in late 1982, they were appointed administrator (god man) for their friend. As both the applicant's adoptive mother and their friend became unable to care for themselves due to their increasing care needs associated with advanced age, they were placed in a care facility by a local authority in the respondent state. The care facility charged them for medical care and the applicant effected the relevant payments. When they subsequently realised that the care facility was a charitable association, they stopped paying the fees on the ground that the institution in question was not entitled to charge them. A dispute arose between the applicant and the care facility and the latter brought a court action.

In early spring of a particular year, while the above-mentioned proceedings were pending, the principal guardian board in a municipality within the respondent state asked the local court in the same town to appoint an administrator for the applicant's adoptive mother. The applicant, on their own and their adoptive mother’s behalf, filed a counter-claim and sought the immediate dismissal of the members of the principal guardian board. Referring to Article 6 para. 1 (art. 6-1) of the Convention, they asked for a public hearing.

On three occasions in the course of the ensuing proceedings the applicant was ordered by the relevant courts, under Chapter 9, Article 5, of the Code of Judicial Procedure (rättegångsbalken; see paragraphs 19-20 below), to pay fines for improper (otillbörliga) statements made in their written observations. The orders were made in the form of decisions (beslut). They appear to have paid the fines.

B. The first fine

In their written observations of in early May of that year to the local court they stated, inter alia, that the principal guardian board could be described as “a basket of municipal rotten eggs of different colours with a common denominator and overriding ideology, namely fascism“. Accordingly, the rights and legitimate interests of individuals within the municipality - if these were taken into account at all, so intoxicated were the authorities with power - were never given proper consideration amounting to an effective examination of the needs of the community. As a result, such needs were defined by so-called democratically elected “peoples’ courts“ - officially named “boards“ and “councils“ of the municipality within the respondent state - the members of which “consist[ed] to a surprising degree of the local public mob“ or “pure rotten eggs“.

The local court, considering the applicant’s statements “improper“ within the meaning of Chapter 9, Article 5, of the Code of Judicial Procedure, ordered them, at a sitting later that month, to pay a fine of a specific amount in local currency. It did not hold a hearing and adjourned its examination of the merits of the case (see paragraph 15 below).

C. The second fine

In early June the applicant appealed from the above decision to the regional appellate court. They asked for an oral hearing and complained of not having had the possibility to defend themselves orally before the local court. In their written observations, the applicant informed the regional appellate court that, should it refuse their demands, they intended to lodge a further appeal (presumably to the highest court of appeal) in order to bring their case to the Commission in a city where another international human rights body is located or to the Human Rights Committee in Geneva. They expressed the opinion that the likelihood of their being granted leave to appeal was very small in view of the “generally lethargic[,]... lax and allergic attitude of the final instance“, which resulted from the “anti-human rights indoctrination received by its members during their extended period’ service in public administration“.

In early November of that year, without holding an oral hearing, the regional appellate court confirmed the local court’s decision and ordered the applicant to pay a further fine of a specific amount in local currency, finding that their written observations of in early June also contained improper remarks prejudicing the good order of court proceedings.

The applicant then applied to the highest court of appeal for leave to appeal, alleging that the lower and appellate courts had not only denied them a fair trial but had also violated their right to freedom of expression. Such leave was refused in early January of the following year.

D. The third fine

In the meantime, in mid-June, the local court, without holding a hearing, accepted the applicant’s and their adoptive mother’s objection against the principal guardian board’s request for the appointment of an administrator, but rejected their demand for the dismissal of the members of the principal guardian board (see paragraph 8 above).

In early July the applicant, on their own and their adoptive mother’s behalf, lodged an appeal with the regional appellate court, seeking to have the case referred back to the local court for reconsideration, as well as an oral hearing before the lower court. On this occasion, they asked that their case be heard by a specially composed local court as they wished to have certain members disqualified from sitting. They alleged that one of its members, whom they described, had a “far-reaching tendentiously fascist way of presiding“ over the court and was “grossly partial in favour of municipal interests, collegiate corruption and abuse of public power through high-handedness, terror and reactionary principles“. When composed of the described judicial official and certain other members, who were also described, the local court had been generally autocratic and had applied the law in a manner which had been heavily in favour of the local authorities. On another occasion in early November of that year, in a separate decision from the one mentioned in paragraph 13 above, the regional appellate court rejected the appeal. In addition, it again ordered the applicant to pay a specific amount in local currency for improper remarks in their appeal. It did not hold a hearing.

The applicant, on their own behalf and on that of their adoptive mother’s estate (the latter had died a few days later), applied to the highest court of appeal for leave to appeal, which was refused on another occasion in early January of the following year (in a different decision from that referred to in paragraph 14 above).
results.final_text:
PROCEDURE

The case was referred to the international judicial body by an international human rights body (“the body“) at an early point in a particular year, within a period of several months laid down in Article 32 para. 1 and Article 47 (art. 32-1, art. 47) of the Convention. It originated in a specific application number against the respondent country lodged with the body under Article 25 (art. 25) by the applicant, on a prior date. The body’s request referred to Articles 44 and 48 (art. 44, art. 48) and to the declaration whereby the respondent country recognised the compulsory jurisdiction of the international judicial body (Article 46) (art. 46). The object of the request was to obtain a decision as to whether the facts of the case disclosed a breach by the respondent entity of its obligations under Article 6 para. 1 (art. 6-1).

In response to the enquiry made in accordance with Rule 33 para. 3 (d) of the Rules of the international judicial body, the applicant stated that he wished to take part in the proceedings. The presiding official granted him leave to present his own case (Rule 30).

The designated panel to be constituted included ex officio a panel member, an elected judicial official from the respondent country (Article 43 of the Convention) (art. 43), and another panel member, the presiding official of the international judicial body (Rule 21 para. 3 (b)). On a subsequent date during that initial period, the Vice-Presiding official drew by lot, in the presence of the registry official, the names of several other members of the panel (Article 43 in fine of the Convention and Rule 21 para. 4) (art. 43).

As Presiding official of the designated panel (Rule 21 para. 5), the presiding official, through the registry official, consulted the representative of the respondent government (“the respondent government“), the applicant and an appointed representative of the human rights body on the organisation of the proceedings (Rules 37 para. 1 and 38). Pursuant to the order made in consequence, the registry official received the respondent government's memorial several months afterwards, in the summer period, and the applicant’s memorial at a later point in the summer. In the early autumn, an administrative officer of the human rights body informed the registry official that the appointed representative did not intend to file a memorial in reply. On several occasions in the mid-autumn, the body produced a number of documents, as requested by the registry official on the presiding official’s instructions, and the applicant submitted his claims under Article 50 (art. 50).

In accordance with the presiding official’s decision, the hearing took place in public in the premises of the international judicial body in an international city, at the end of that autumn period. The international judicial body had held a preparatory meeting beforehand. There appeared before the international judicial body:

for the respondent government a legal counsel, acting as their representative, and another legal counsel, a consultant for the respondent government;

for the body an appointed representative;

the applicant. The international judicial body heard addresses by the legal counsel, the appointed representative, and the applicant.

AS TO THE FACTS

I. THE PARTICULAR CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

A. Introduction

The applicant, a legal academic, resides in a local town.

From early in a previous decade the applicant held a power of attorney from his adoptive mother, and, in a subsequent year, he was appointed a designated guardian for her friend. As both the adoptive mother and her friend became unable to care for themselves due to age-related infirmities, they were placed in a care facility by a local administrative body. The care facility charged them for medical care and the applicant effected the relevant payments. When he subsequently realised that the care facility was a philanthropic organization, he stopped paying the fees on the ground that the institution in question was not entitled to charge them. A dispute arose between the applicant and the care facility and the latter brought a court action.

On a specific date during that period, while the above-mentioned proceedings were pending, the local supervisory board in the same local administrative area asked a local court in the same town to appoint a designated guardian for the adoptive mother. The applicant, on his own and the adoptive mother’s behalf, filed a counter-claim and sought the immediate dismissal of the members of the local supervisory board. Referring to Article 6 para. 1 (art. 6-1) of the Convention, he asked for a public hearing.

On three occasions in the course of the ensuing proceedings the applicant was ordered by the relevant courts, under Chapter 9, Article 5, of the Code of Judicial Procedure (see paragraphs 19-20 below), to pay fines for improper (otillbörliga) statements made in his written observations. The orders were made in the form of decisions. He appears to have paid the fines.

B. The first fine

In his written observations of on a date in early May to the local court he stated, inter alia, that the local supervisory board could be described as “a basket of local rotten eggs of different colours with a common denominator and overriding ideology, namely fascism“. Accordingly, the rights and legitimate interests of individuals within the local administrative area - if these were taken into account at all, so intoxicated were the local governance officials with power - were never given proper consideration amounting to an effective examination of the needs of the community. As a result, such needs were defined by so-called democratically elected “local governance structures“ - officially named “boards“ and “councils“ of the local administrative body - the members of which “consist[ed] to a surprising degree of the local public mob“ or “pure rotten eggs“.

The local court, considering the applicant’s statements “improper“ within the meaning of Chapter 9, Article 5, of the Code of Judicial Procedure, ordered him, at a sitting about a month later, to pay a penalty amount. It did not hold a hearing and adjourned its examination of the merits of the case (see paragraph 15 below).

C. The second fine

About a few weeks later, the applicant appealed from the above decision to an appellate court in the region. He asked for an oral hearing and complained of not having had the possibility to defend himself orally before the local court. In his written observations, the applicant informed the appellate court that, should it refuse his demands, he intended to lodge a further appeal (presumably to the highest court in the country) in order to bring his case to the human rights body in an international human rights venue or to another international human rights forum. He expressed the opinion that the likelihood of his being granted leave to appeal was very small in view of the “generally lethargic[,]... lax and certain sensitivities of the final instance“, which resulted from the “anti-human rights indoctrination received by its members during their extensive service in public sector roles“.

Several months later, without holding an oral hearing, the appellate court confirmed the local court’s decision and ordered the applicant to pay a further penalty amount, finding that his written observations of a few weeks earlier also contained improper remarks prejudicing the good order of court proceedings.

The applicant then applied to the highest court in the country for leave to appeal, alleging that the lower and appellate courts had not only denied him a fair trial but had also violated his right to freedom of expression. Such leave was refused early the following year.

D. The third fine

In the meantime, on a date in the previous month, the local court, without holding a hearing, accepted the applicant’s and the adoptive mother’s objection against the local supervisory board’s request for the appointment of a designated guardian, but rejected their demand for the dismissal of the members of the local supervisory board (see paragraph 8 above).

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. a specific application number) against the respondent State lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention on human rights (“the Convention”) by a national of the respondent State, the applicant, on a particular date in early 1999.

The applicant was represented by a legal representative practicing in a specific foreign jurisdiction. The Government did not designate an Agent for the purpose of the proceedings before the Court.

Some years later, on a specific date in early 2004, the Court declared the application partly inadmissible and decided to communicate the complaints concerning the applicant’s right to a fair trial by an independent and impartial tribunal and the failure to notify the applicant of the public prosecutor’s submissions on his appeal to the Government. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant, who was born in the mid-1960s, resides in a major city.

On a date in late January of a certain year, the applicant made a speech about the liberation movement in Palestine and praised the Muslim leaders who worked for the liberation of Jerusalem during a meeting which was organised by a local municipal body in the capital city.

A few days later in early February of that year, the applicant was taken into police custody. In his statement, he explained that he supported a particular foreign regime and that he longed for a regime that was based on the Koran.

Later that month, in mid-February, the applicant was questioned by the public prosecutor attached to the national security court in the capital. During his questioning by the prosecutor, the applicant repeated his police statement. On the same day the applicant was brought before the investigating judge attached to the national security court in the capital and he was subsequently placed in detention on remand.

In an indictment dated in early March of that year, the national security court in the capital’s public prosecutor initiated criminal proceedings against the applicant.

Before the national security court in the capital, which was composed of three judges including a military judge, the applicant contested the charges against him.

In mid-October of that year, the national security court in the capital convicted the applicant of being a member of an illegal organisation and sentenced him to a lengthy period of imprisonment pursuant to Article 168 of the Criminal Code.

In early January the following year, the applicant appealed to the supreme appellate court. The Chief Public Prosecutor attached to the supreme appellate court submitted his written opinion on the merits of the appeal. The opinion was not notified to the applicant, but was read out during the hearing before the supreme appellate court.

In late September of that year, the supreme appellate court dismissed the applicant’s appeal, upholding the national security court in the capital’s assessment of evidence and its reasons for rejecting the applicant’s defence.

In late December of that year, the Chief Public Prosecutor attached to the supreme appellate court rejected the applicant’s request for rectification.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. XXXX/YY) against the respondent State lodged with the tribunal under Article 34 of the international human rights treaty (“the treaty”) by a Turkish national, the claimant, in mid-2002.

The claimant was represented by a legal representative, counsel practicing in a certain provincial city. The respondent authorities (“the authorities”) were represented by their Agent.

Early in a subsequent year, a presiding official of a section of the tribunal decided to give notice of the application to the authorities. It was also decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility (Article 29 § 3).

THE FACTS

The individual concerned was born in the late 1970s and lives in that same city.

In early October of that year, the individual was taken into police custody in the aforementioned city on suspicion of aiding and abetting a proscribed organization, an identified insurgent group. During police custody, the person signed a form explaining arrested persons' rights, whereby they were reminded of their right to remain silent. The following day the police officers interrogated the individual at the anti-terrorism unit of the local police headquarters, in the absence of legal counsel. The individual admitted that they had aided and abetted the designated group and acted as a courier for the organization. Another suspect was also arrested in the course of the operation. In a police statement, this other individual stated that they were a member of the designated group and that they had stayed at the individual's residence when they had come to that city and maintained that the individual had acted as a designated group courier and helped them in certain illegal activities.

A few days later, the person was questioned by the prosecuting authority and they denied that they had been involved in the designated group. They also claimed that they had signed their police statement without reading it.

On the same day, the person was brought before the examining magistrate, who questioned them and a group of other suspects in relation to their involvement in the designated group. The individual's legal representative was also present during the questioning. In a statement to the magistrate, the individual denied all the charges against them. After the interrogation, the individual was placed in pre-trial detention on the order of the judge.

In an indictment dated later that month, the state prosecutor at the regional special security court charged the individual concerned and several other co-accused with aiding and abetting an illegal organisation. The charges were brought under Article 169 of the Criminal Code and Article 5 of Law no. 3713 (the anti-terrorist law).

During the trial, the individual denied any connection with the designated group. Furthermore the co-defendant, one of the co-accused, admitted to being a designated group member before the court, but stated that although they had stayed at the individual's residence when they had come to the city, the individual did not know that they were a designated group member. In this connection, the co-defendant rejected their police statement, alleging that it had been taken under duress. In mid-2002 the regional special security court convicted the individual as charged and sentenced them to a substantial prison term of several years. In convicting the individual, the court relied on the individual's police statement, which had been taken in the absence of legal counsel. The court further took into consideration the police statement of the co-defendant who had given evidence that the individual had been involved in the activities of the designated group. It therefore concluded that the individual's police statement, in which they had admitted to their involvement in the designated group, had been confirmed by other evidence in the case file.

In late 2003 the highest appellate court dismissed the individual's appeal.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a specific application number) against the Kingdom of Sweden lodged with the international human rights tribunal under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a national of that country, the applicant, on a specific date in the late 1990s.

The applicant was represented before the tribunal by a legal representative, a legal professional practising in a major city in the country. The national government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent of the Ministry for Foreign Affairs.

On a date in the early 2000s, the tribunal decided to communicate to the Government the complaint under Article 6 § 1 of the Convention concerning the refusal to hold an oral hearing. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born early in the previous century and lives in a major city in the country. Until his retirement in the early 1980s he worked as a teacher for a part-time schedule.

In the late summer of a particular year, the applicant applied for disability benefits (handikappersättning) under Chapter 9, section 2 of the Social Insurance Act of that period (Lagen om allmän försäkring, of that period:381 - hereinafter “the Act of that period”). He claimed that, even before his customary retirement age in the early 1980s, he had incurred extra costs due to his illness, Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease, from which he had suffered since the 1970s and which had been diagnosed in the autumn of the early 1980s. In support of his claim, he submitted:

(i) A medical certificate dated in late August of that year, produced by the applicant's general practitioner, a medical professional, at the applicant's request, supporting his application for disability benefits. It stated that the medical professional had known and treated the applicant since the early 1960s, and that the Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease had started in the early 1970s involving difficulties in walking, problems of balance, dragging of the feet and the patient tripping over and falling continuously. The muscles in the legs and feet had considerably withered. The illness had attacked even the hands and arms, with withered muscles and reduced strength in the fingers. Because of multiple inconveniences, the patient's functional capacity had been strongly reduced; he needed help for heavier household tasks, the preparation of meals, the purchase of household goods, carrying heavier objects, and for personal hygiene. The patient had incurred extra costs for medical treatment, foot rails, soft shoes, home assistance, and to some extent his food budget because of a limited ability to prepare meals; he also had to pay for the transportation service for disabled persons, and extra travel by personal car, because of his considerably reduced ability to walk.

(ii) A statement of in the spring of a later year by the medical professional, reproducing extracts from the applicant's medical records for the period between the mid-1970s and the early 1980s, with a diagnosis of the Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease in the autumn of the early 1980s; (iii) A statement dated in early spring of the early 1980s by a medical specialist, Professor and Doctor of a prominent medical institution at a university in a major European city, which concluded: “I quite agree that the diagnosis here is Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease. The clinical findings and the nerve conduction studies indicate that it can be classified as a specific type of hereditary motor and sensory neurological condition. He does show some minor neurological indicators in the legs, which may be associated. I have explained to the applicant that although his symptoms may continue slowly to deteriorate, this is unlikely ever to become a very serious incapacity so that he becomes unable to walk.” 6. In mid-summer of a subsequent year, the Social Insurance Office (the social insurance agency -hereinafter “the Office”) of a regional administrative division rejected the application, finding that the applicant's disability had not reached the level required under Chapter 9, section 2 before he turned the customary retirement age. One member reserved his position, considering that the applicant's need for assistance before reaching that age should be investigated. 7. The applicant, represented by a lawyer, appealed to the County Administrative Court (the initial administrative court) of that regional administrative division and requested that an oral hearing be held in his case because he wished to call as witnesses his personal doctor, the doctor appointed by the Office and all the members of the Office who had participated in the decision of his case. In early winter of the following year, the County Administrative Court refused the request with reference to section 9 of the Administrative Court Procedure Act of that period (of that period:291 - hereinafter “the Act of that period”). Its decision contained the following reasoning: “Written material, which includes inter alia medical certificates and extracts from [the applicant's] medical journal, a multitude of submissions and other documents sent by [the applicant] as well as the diary notes made during the processing of the case before [the Office] ..., are available in the case. There are no uncertainties as regards, at least, the basis for the medical assessment. The uncertainty regarding [the applicant's] extra costs due to his disability at that life stage can be clarified satisfactorily by [him] in writing. According to the documents, [the applicant's] requests and reasons therefore are clearly defined, as are the submissions by the respondent party. Nor has [the applicant] pointed to circumstances which would benefit from being orally presented by him. Thus, there is no reason to assume that an oral hearing could add anything meaningful. The County Administrative Court therefore considers an oral hearing to be unnecessary and rejects the request to that effect. ...” The County Administrative Court invited the applicant to mention any further circumstances he wished to invoke and to submit his final written observations in the case within a short period. In response he reiterated his request for an oral hearing, relying on Article 6 of the Convention. He argued that the medical certificates needed to be clarified and that a witness account by his personal doctor would be important in order to establish the exact level of support that he had required at that life stage. He also submitted that the members of the Office should have been asked to give evidence about their precise reasons for refusing his request. 8. By a judgment of in mid-winter of the same year, the County Administrative Court rejected the applicant's appeal on the grounds that the medical and other evidence in the case showed that, even before he had reached that life stage, he had for a considerable time been functionally impaired, but not to such a degree that, on an assessment of the overall need of assistance, he was entitled to disability benefit. The court noted that the applicant had “commented” (yttrat sig) on its rejection of his request for an oral hearing, but did not respond to his renewed request or his reasons invoked therein. In reaching this decision the County Administrative Court took note of a breakdown of additional costs allegedly caused by his disability, totalling a significant sum of money, which the applicant had initially submitted to the Social Insurance Office in mid-autumn of the application year. 9. The applicant appealed against the lower court's judgment to the Administrative Court of Appeal (the higher administrative court) requesting it to quash the judgment and refer the case back for fresh examination. In the alternative, he requested the appeals tribunal to find that he was entitled to disability benefits at a level corresponding to a specific percentage of the basic amount as of mid-summer of an earlier year. He further requested that the court hold an oral hearing, on the same grounds as those he had presented to the lower court. He submitted that, by refusing to hold an oral hearing, the latter had breached Article 6 of the Convention. 10. By a decision of in early summer of the appeal year, the Administrative Court of Appeal rejected his request for an oral hearing, finding this unnecessary for determining whether to grant him leave to appeal, and gave him a short period to complete his submissions in writing. The applicant made further submissions and maintained his request for an oral hearing. In late autumn of the same year, the Administrative Court of Appeal rejected his renewed request for an oral hearing and refused him leave to appeal.
On a date in the late 1990s, the Supreme Administrative Court (the highest administrative tribunal), observing that it did not normally hold oral hearings, rejected the applicant's request for a hearing and gave him a period of several weeks within which to submit additional written observations. On a date in the early 2000s it refused him leave to appeal. In this connection the applicant was given a copy of an analysis presented to the Supreme Administrative Court by its legal secretary, which included an opinion to the effect that the County Administrative Court's refusal to hold a hearing was not deemed incompatible with the Convention.
results.final_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a specific case number) against the respondent State lodged with the relevant international judicial body under Article 34 of a foundational human rights instrument (“the instrument”) by a national of the respondent State, the claimant, on a specific date late in the 20th century.

The claimant was represented before the judicial body by a legal representative, a legal professional practising in a major city in the country. The national authorities of the respondent State (“the national authorities”) were represented by a designated agent from the diplomatic service.

On a later date in the early 2000s, the judicial body decided to communicate to the national authorities the complaint under Article 6 § 1 of the instrument concerning the refusal to hold an oral hearing. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the instrument, it decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The claimant was born in the early part of the 20th century and lives in a major city in the country. Until an early period in the 1980s, when they ceased working, they worked in an educational capacity on a part-time basis.

On a specific date in the mid-1990s, the claimant applied for benefits for impaired capacity (handikappersättning) under Chapter 9, section 2 of the applicable social insurance legislation. They claimed that, even before reaching a predefined age threshold in the early 1980s, they had incurred extra costs due to a specific chronic neurological condition, from which they had suffered since an earlier period in the latter half of the 20th century and which had been diagnosed in the early 1980s. In support of their claim, they submitted:

(i) A medical certificate dated on a subsequent date in the mid-1990s, produced by the claimant's primary care physician, a medical professional, at the claimant's request, supporting their application for benefits for impaired capacity. It stated that the medical professional had known and treated the claimant since an early period of treatment, and that the specific chronic neurological condition had started in an earlier period in the latter half of the 20th century involving difficulties in walking, problems of balance, dragging of the feet and the patient tripping over and falling continuously. The muscles in the legs and feet had considerably atrophied. The health condition had impacted even the hands and arms, with muscle atrophy and diminished physical capability in the fingers. Because of multiple inconveniences, the patient's functional capacity had been strongly reduced; they needed help for heavier household tasks, the preparation of meals, the purchase of household goods, carrying heavier objects, and for personal hygiene. The patient had incurred extra costs for medical treatment, foot supports, soft shoes, home assistance, and to some extent their food budget because of a limited ability to prepare meals; they also had to pay for transportation services for individuals with impaired mobility, and extra travel by personal car, because of their considerably reduced ability to walk.

(ii) A statement from a later medical professional from the mid-1990s, reproducing extracts from the claimant's medical records for a multi-year span of medical documentation from an earlier period, with a diagnosis of the specific chronic neurological condition in the early 1980s; (iii) A statement dated an early date in the 1980s by a specialist medical academic from a renowned medical institution, which concluded: “I quite agree that the diagnosis here is a specific chronic neurological condition. The clinical findings and the nerve conduction studies indicate that it can be classified as a specific subtype of this condition affecting motor and sensory nerves. The claimant does show certain neurological indicators in the legs, which may be associated. I have explained to the claimant that although their symptoms may continue slowly to deteriorate, this is unlikely ever to become a very serious incapacity so that they become unable to walk.” 6. On a specific date in the mid-1990s, the responsible administrative agency (försäkringskassan -hereinafter “the agency”) of a specific administrative region rejected the application, finding that the claimant's impaired capacity had not reached the level required under Chapter 9, section 2 before they reached a specific age limit. A member of the decision-making body reserved their position, considering that the claimant's need for assistance before reaching the specific age limit should be investigated. 7. The claimant, represented by legal counsel, appealed to the regional administrative court (länsrätten) of a specific administrative region and requested that an oral hearing be held in their case because they wished to call as witnesses their personal medical professional, the medical professional appointed by the agency and all the members of the agency who had participated in the decision of their case. On a date in the late 1990s, the regional administrative court refused the request with reference to procedural rules for administrative courts. Its decision contained the following reasoning: “Written material, which includes inter alia medical certificates and extracts from the claimant's medical journal, a multitude of submissions and other documents sent by the claimant as well as the diary notes made during the processing of the case before the agency ..., are available in the case. There are no uncertainties as regards, at least, the basis for the medical assessment. The uncertainty regarding the claimant's extra costs due to their health condition at the specific age limit can be clarified satisfactorily by them in writing. According to the documents, the claimant's requests and reasons therefore are clearly defined, as are the submissions by the respondent. Nor has the claimant pointed to circumstances which would benefit from being orally presented by them. Thus, there is no reason to assume that an oral hearing could add anything meaningful. The regional administrative court therefore considers an oral hearing to be unnecessary and rejects the request to that effect. ...” The regional administrative court invited the claimant to mention any further circumstances they wished to invoke and to submit their final written observations in the case within a short period. In response they reiterated their request for an oral hearing, relying on a relevant article of the foundational legal instrument. They argued that the medical certificates needed to be clarified and that a witness account by their personal medical professional would be important in order to establish the exact level of support that they had required at the specific age limit. They also submitted that the members of the agency should have been asked to give evidence about their precise reasons for refusing their request. 8. By a judgment on a subsequent date in the late 1990s, the regional administrative court rejected the claimant's appeal on the grounds that the medical and other evidence in the case showed that, even before they had reached the specific age limit, they had for an extended period been impaired in their function, but not to such a degree that, on an assessment of the overall need for assistance, they were entitled to benefits for impaired capacity. The judicial body noted that the claimant had “commented” (yttrat sig) on its rejection of their request for an oral hearing, but did not respond to their renewed request or their reasons invoked therein. In reaching this decision the regional administrative court took note of a breakdown of additional costs allegedly caused by their health condition, totalling a specific monetary sum, which the claimant had initially submitted to the responsible administrative agency on an earlier date in the mid-1990s. 9. The claimant appealed against the lower judicial body's judgment to the regional administrative appellate court (kammarrätten) requesting it to quash the judgment and refer the case back for fresh examination. In the alternative, they requested the appellate judicial body to find that they were entitled to benefits for impaired capacity at a level corresponding to a specific percentage of the basic amount as of a date in the mid-1990s. They further requested that the judicial body hold an oral hearing, on the same grounds as those they had presented to the lower judicial body. They submitted that, by refusing to hold an oral hearing, the latter had breached a relevant article of the foundational legal instrument. 10. By a decision on a date in the late 1990s, the regional administrative appellate court rejected their request for an oral hearing, finding this unnecessary for determining whether to grant them leave to appeal, and gave them a short period to complete their submissions in writing. The claimant made further submissions and maintained their request for an oral hearing. On a later date that year, the regional administrative appellate court rejected their renewed request for an oral hearing and refused them leave to appeal. 11. On a date in late 1998, the highest administrative court (Regeringsrätten), observing that it did not normally hold oral hearings, rejected the claimant's request for a hearing and gave them a defined period within which to submit additional written observations. In early 2000, it refused them leave to appeal. In this connection the claimant was given a copy of an analysis presented to the highest administrative court by its legal official, which included an opinion to the effect that the regional administrative court's refusal to hold a hearing was not deemed incompatible with the foundational legal instrument.
