results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. X) against the respondent State lodged with the judicial body under Article 34 of the relevant human rights convention (“the international agreement”) by a national of the country, the applicant, in late 2006.

The applicant was represented by Mr. C. of a law firm, a legal representative practising in a city in the region. The respondent government (“the respondent government”) were represented by their Agent, Mr. W. of a government department.

The applicant complained, among other things, that he had been dismissed on account of his political views and membership of a political organization.

In early 2009 the presiding official of a section of the judicial body decided to communicate the complaints concerning Articles 10, 11 and 14 of the international agreement to the respondent government. It was also decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility (Article 29 § 1).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the mid-20th century and lives in a northern urban area.

The applicant was employed by Company X from late 2003 to his dismissal in late June of the following year. Company X provided transport to local authorities, including the local authority.

The applicant, who is a national of the country with a particular ethnic background, was employed formerly as a transport assistant and latterly as a vehicle operator. As such, he was responsible for transporting younger individuals and older individuals with physical and/or specific support needs within the local urban area. The majority of his passengers were of a particular ethnic origin. There had been no complaints about his work or his conduct at work and his team leader, who was of a different ethnic background, had nominated him for the award of “an exemplary worker”.

In late May of that year a local newspaper article published in the northern urban area and the surrounding areas identified the applicant as a candidate for the political organization (“the organization”) in the forthcoming local elections. On the same day, the applicant was temporarily assigned to deliver mail to local council offices.

At the relevant time the organization only extended membership to nationals of a particular racial background. According to its constitution it was: “wholly opposed to any form of integration between individuals of a particular racial background and people from non-European backgrounds. It is therefore committed to stemming and reversing the tide of those of different racial backgrounds immigration and to restoring, by legal changes, negotiation and consent, the overwhelmingly individuals of a particular racial background makeup of the majority population that existed in the country prior to an earlier decade.”

The following day Trade Union A, a public sector trade union, sent a letter to Company X stating that many of its members found the applicant’s continued employment a “significant cause for concern, bearing in mind the organization’s overt and racial/extremist agenda.” The letter advised Company X that a large majority of its customer base and over a third of its workforce were of a particular ethnic origin. Trade Union A asked that Company X take immediate action to ensure its members were not subjected to racial hatred. Another trade union, Trade Union B, and a number of employees also made representations to Company X about the applicant’s continued employment.

In mid-June of that year the applicant was elected as an elected local representative for the political organization. After taking legal advice Company X summarily dismissed him in late June of that year. Company X cited, inter alia, potential health and safety risks as the applicant’s continued employment would give rise to considerable anxiety among passengers and their carers. It also expressed concern that the applicant’s continued employment could jeopardise its reputation and possibly lead to the loss of its contract with the local authority.

Ordinarily, one year’s service is required before an employee can bring an action for unfair dismissal under the Employment Rights Act from the mid-1990s (“the mid-1990s Act”), although this qualifying period does not apply where the dismissal was on grounds of pregnancy, racial background, sex or religion. The applicant therefore lacked sufficient continuous service to bring an action for unfair dismissal. However, in mid-August of that year he lodged a statutory claim of racial background discrimination in the initial labor dispute forum pursuant to the Racial Background Relations Act from the mid-1970s (“the mid-1970s Act”).

The applicant claimed that he had been unlawfully discriminated against as his dismissal constituted less favourable treatment on racial grounds. The racial grounds relied on were those of the passengers and employees of Company X who were of a particular ethnic origin. He further argued that since the political organization was an exclusively for individuals of a specific racial background party, his dismissal also constituted indirect racial discrimination.

The initial labor dispute forum gave judgment in early 2005. It noted Company X’s concerns that the applicant’s continued employment might lead to difficulties with other employees; damage its relationship with the trade unions; lead to attacks on Company X’s minibuses which would jeopardise the health and safety of Company X’s staff, its vulnerable passengers, and the applicant himself; cause considerable anxiety amongst Company X’s passengers and those relatives/carers entrusting vulnerable passengers to its care; and damage its reputation so as potentially to place at risk existing contracts and future bids for work in government-related contracts and elsewhere.

The initial labor dispute forum dismissed the claim of direct discrimination as it was satisfied that if any discrimination existed against the claimant it was not on racial grounds but rather on health and safety grounds. The forum also dismissed the claim of indirect discrimination on the ground that the applicant’s dismissal was a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim, namely the maintenance of health and safety.

The applicant lodged an appeal with the higher labor dispute forum. In late July of that year his appeal was upheld on the ground that the forum had erred in its construction of the phrase “on racial grounds” by failing to interpret its meaning broadly and had not indicated how it had come to the conclusion that the applicant’s dismissal was a proportionate means of achieving the aim of ensuring health and safety because, inter alia, there had been no consideration of any alternatives to dismissal.

In early September of that year Company X was granted permission to appeal to the appellate court.

In late May of the subsequent year the appellate court allowed Company X’s appeal and restored the order of the initial labor dispute forum. Justice M. found the applicant’s submission that he had been subjected to direct racial background discrimination to be wrong in principle and inconsistent with the purposes of the legislation.

In rejecting the claim of direct discrimination, the appellate court noted that: “The applicant was treated less favourably not on the ground that he was an individual of a particular racial background, but on the ground of a particular non-racial characteristic shared by him with a very small fraction of the majority population, that is membership of and standing for election for a political organization like the party. Company X was not adopting a policy which discriminated on the basis of a dividing line of racial background. Company X would apply the same approach to a member of a similar political organization, which confined its membership to individuals of a different racial background. The dividing line of racial background was not made by Company X, but by the organization which defines its own composition by racial background. The applicant cannot credibly make a claim of direct racial background discrimination by Company X against him on the ground that he is an individual of a particular racial background by relying on the decision of his own chosen political organization to limit its membership to individuals of a particular racial background. The organization cannot make a non-racial criterion (party membership) a racial one by the terms of its constitution limiting membership to individuals of a particular racial background. Properly analysed the applicant’s complaint is of discrimination on political grounds, which falls outside the anti-discrimination laws.”

In rejecting the claim of indirect discrimination, the following was noted: “For indirect discrimination ... it is necessary to identify a ‘provision, criterion or practice’ which Company X has applied or would apply equally to persons not of the same racial background. ... The applicant ... failed to present the forum with a case, which satisfied the requisite elements of a claim for indirect racial background discrimination and upon which the forum could properly make a finding of indirect racial background discrimination.... The initial labor dispute forum appears to have attempted itself a version of a ‘provision, criterion or practice’ in paragraph 5.6 of its decision (see paragraph 28 above). However, it is formulated too narrowly (membership of the organization) to be meaningful. A provision of ‘membership of the organization’ could not be applied to a person who was not of the same racial background as the applicant, because only persons of the same racial background as him (individuals of a particular racial background) are eligible to be members of the organization. A more general and meaningful provision along similar lines would be one applying to membership of a political organization like the party, which existed to promote views hostile to members of a different racial background than those that belonged to the organization. If such a provision were applied, however, it would not put persons of the same racial background as the applicant ‘at a particular disadvantage’ when compared with other persons within section 1(1A) of the mid-1970s Act. All such advocates for a political cause would be at the same disadvantage, whatever racial background they were.”

Lastly, with regard to the applicant’s contention that he had been subjected to less favourable treatment arising from membership of a political organization contrary to his international agreement rights under Articles 9, 10, 11 and 14 and that this should have been taken into account in deciding whether indirect discrimination had been justified, the appellate court stated that: “The late 1990s Act does not assist the applicant in this case. He is not entitled to make a claim under it as Company X is not a public authority. Section 3 of the late 1990s Act does not assist, as there is no respect in which the relevant provisions of the mid-1970s Act are incompatible with the international agreement rights. As for justification under the mid-1970s Act I have already explained that it does not arise, as no case of indirect discrimination has been made out.”

The applicant was refused leave to appeal to the highest court of appeal at the time.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. X) against the respondent State lodged with the judicial body under Article 34 of the relevant human rights convention (“the international agreement”) by a national of the country, the applicant, in late 2006.

The applicant was represented by Mr. C. of a law firm, a legal representative practising in a city in the region. The respondent government (“the respondent government”) were represented by their Agent, Mr. W. of a government department.

The applicant complained, among other things, that he had been dismissed on account of his political views and membership of a political organization.

In early 2009 the presiding official of a section of the judicial body decided to communicate the complaints concerning Articles 10, 11 and 14 of the international agreement to the respondent government. It was also decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility (Article 29 § 1).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the mid-20th century and lives in a northern urban area.

The applicant was employed by Company X from late 2003 to his dismissal in late June of the following year. Company X provided transport to local authorities, including the local authority.

The applicant, who is a national of the country with a particular ethnic background, was employed formerly as a transport assistant and latterly as a vehicle operator. As such, he was responsible for transporting younger individuals and older individuals with physical and/or specific support needs within the local urban area. The majority of his passengers were of a particular ethnic origin. There had been no complaints about his work or his conduct at work and his team leader, who was of a different ethnic background, had nominated him for the award of “an exemplary worker”.

In late May of that year a local newspaper article published in the northern urban area and the surrounding areas identified the applicant as a candidate for the political organization (“the organization”) in the forthcoming local elections. On the same day, the applicant was temporarily assigned to deliver mail to local council offices.

At the relevant time the organization only extended membership to nationals of a particular racial background. According to its constitution it was: “wholly opposed to any form of integration between individuals of a particular racial background and people from non-European backgrounds. It is therefore committed to stemming and reversing the tide of those of different racial backgrounds immigration and to restoring, by legal changes, negotiation and consent, the overwhelmingly individuals of a particular racial background makeup of the majority population that existed in the country prior to an earlier decade.”

The following day Trade Union A, a public sector trade union, sent a letter to Company X stating that many of its members found the applicant’s continued employment a “significant cause for concern, bearing in mind the organization’s overt and racial/extremist agenda.” The letter advised Company X that a large majority of its customer base and over a third of its workforce were of a particular ethnic origin. Trade Union A asked that Company X take immediate action to ensure its members were not subjected to racial hatred. Another trade union, Trade Union B, and a number of employees also made representations to Company X about the applicant’s continued employment.

In mid-June of that year the applicant was elected as an elected local representative for the political organization. After taking legal advice Company X summarily dismissed him in late June of that year. Company X cited, inter alia, potential health and safety risks as the applicant’s continued employment would give rise to considerable anxiety among passengers and their carers. results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. XXXX/XX) against the United Kingdom lodged with the European Court of Human Rights under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a national of the respondent State, the applicant, in early March of that year.

The applicant was represented by a legal representative from a law firm in a specific locality. The national government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, from a relevant government department.

Early in the following year, the European Court decided to communicate the application to the Government. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the early 1980s and lives in a specific locality.

The applicant was arrested in mid-May of that year on suspicion of having intimidated witnesses. In an identity parade held the same afternoon, he was positively identified by one of the two witnesses. The applicant states that this witness accepted in her original statement of evidence to the police that she was an individual known to use heroin and an inherently unreliable witness. The applicant was subsequently charged with five counts of doing acts tending and intended to pervert the course of public justice.

The applicant pleaded not guilty in early August that year and the trial was adjourned to the local Crown Court in early October that year. On that date no judge was available and the trial was moved to early February the following year. On the second day of the trial the witness who had identified the applicant did not attend and the trial had to be aborted. At the re-listed trial in mid-September that year, Counsel for the Crown addressed the court in the following terms: “When this matter was listed for trial earlier that year, the witness attended on the first day. She wasn't called to give evidence and didn't come back on the second day. Certainly, that caused some concern. I think it is fair to say, at different stages of the entire case, she has blown slightly hot and cold. The witness indicated to the police when they contacted her recently and, in fact, again yesterday, that she was reluctant to give evidence. She has attended the court today and with my learned friend's permission, I have had the chance to sit down and have a chat to her. Your Honour, the reality is that she does not want to give evidence. She has personal reasons I don't propose to divulge in open court. She has formed a new relationship. She has moved on. She is trying very hard to put the events of this entire case behind her. She has satisfied the police who spoke to her yesterday and satisfied those who instruct me that no threats have been made toward her in relation to today's proceedings. She insists that there has been no contact from the applicant or others acting on his behalf. We don't feel that, due to the nature of this allegation, she ought to be compelled to give evidence in this case. Your Honour, there has been a very full discussion and the view we have taken is that without her evidence, plainly, we can't proceed. I offer no evidence. Verdicts must follow.”

The applicant was duly acquitted. On his counsel's application for a defendant's costs order, the judge refused to make an order and stated: “That order will be refused. There is clear evidence on the court papers. The Crown have taken the view that they are not going to compel this witness although there is compelling evidence in respect of those matters. It is a discretion which I am afraid I am not going to exercise in your favour.”

The applicant's attempted appeal was dismissed in mid-November that year as “to be appealable as a sentence, the order must be contingent upon conviction. As the defendant's costs order only arises when a prosecution is unsuccessful, it cannot be a sentence and cannot be appealed at the Court of Appeal, Criminal Division”.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. [CASE_NUMBER]) against the respondent State lodged with the international human rights tribunal under Article 34 of the relevant human rights convention (”the Convention”) by a national of the respondent State, [THE_APPLICANT] (”the applicant”), early in the year 2000.

The applicant was represented by a legal professional practicing in a major city in the region. The authorities of the respondent State did not designate an Agent for the purposes of the proceedings before the international human rights tribunal.

Late in the year 2006, the international human rights tribunal declared the application partly inadmissible and decided to communicate the complaint concerning the alleged unfairness of the proceedings against the applicant on account of the lack of independence and impartiality of a special security court in the eastern region to the authorities of the respondent State. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the early 1970s and lives in a city in the western part of the country.

At the material time, the applicant was associated with a local newspaper published in a city in the northern part of the country, a regional publication.

Mid-January of that year, the applicant was taken into police custody on suspicion of membership of a designated illegal organization.

A few days later, he was brought before a judge who ordered his detention on remand.

Early March of that same year, the prosecuting authority at a different regional security tribunal filed a bill of indictment against the applicant, along with several other persons. The applicant was accused of membership of the designated illegal organization (the [ORGANIZATION_ACRONYM] (the Revolutionary People's Liberation Party- Front)). According to the indictment, following his arrest, the applicant's house was searched by police officers who found illegal periodicals, newspapers, books and a bullet. The prosecuting authority also alleged that the applicant had published a newspaper containing propaganda in support of the designated illegal organization, participated in illegal demonstrations, put up posters, distributed leaflets supporting the designated illegal organization and carried out activities in a local educational institution for the recruitment of new members for the designated illegal organization.

A few days after the indictment, the applicant was transferred to a facility in the eastern region special type prison.

On different dates that year, several other criminal proceedings were brought against the applicant before various courts, with charges of disseminating separatist propaganda and incitement to hatred and hostility through the medium of his newspaper.

The cases against the applicant and his co-accused were subsequently joined before the different regional security tribunal.

Following promulgation of a legislative act, which abolished the different regional security tribunal and established the special security court in the eastern region, in the mid-1990s the special security court in the eastern region acquired jurisdiction over the case and the case-file was sent to it.

Late November a few years later, the special security court in the eastern region convicted the applicant under a relevant article of the criminal code of membership of the designated illegal organization, namely the [ORGANIZATION_ACRONYM]. The court found that two articles published in the regional publication in mid-November of the preceding year and early March of the year in question contained separatist propaganda and incitement to hatred. It further found it established that the applicant had participated in illegal demonstrations, put up posters, distributed leaflets supporting the designated illegal organization and carried out activities in a local educational institution for the recruitment of new members for the designated illegal organization. The court sentenced the applicant to over a decade's imprisonment.

Late in the year following the conviction, the highest appeals court upheld the judgment from late November a few years earlier in respect of the applicant.

Mid-2004, the applicant was conditionally released from prison.
results.final_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (with a specific application number) against the respondent State lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms ("the Convention") by a national of the respondent State, the applicant, early in the year 2000.

The applicant was represented by a legal representative practising in a major city. The Government did not designate an Agent for the purposes of the proceedings before the Court.

In late August of a later year, the Court declared the application partly inadmissible and decided to communicate the complaint concerning the alleged unfairness of the proceedings against the applicant on account of the lack of independence and impartiality of a regional security court to the Government. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the early 1970s and lives in a city in the country.

At the material time, the applicant was the owner and editor of a local newspaper published in a specific region, a publication known as "The Free Black Sea".

In mid-January of a particular year in the mid-1990s, the applicant was taken into police custody on suspicion of membership of a proscribed organization.

Later that month, he was brought before a judge who ordered his detention on remand.

In early March of the same year, the public prosecutor at an initial regional security court filed a bill of indictment against the applicant, along with several other persons. The applicant was accused of membership of a specified illegal organization (a group often referred to as DHKP-C). According to the indictment, following his arrest, the applicant's house was searched by police officers who found illegal periodicals, newspapers, books and a bullet. The public prosecutor also alleged that the applicant had published a newspaper containing propaganda in support of the specified organization, participated in illegal demonstrations, put up posters, distributed leaflets supporting the specified organization and carried out activities in a local university for the recruitment of new members for the illegal organization.

In mid-March of that year, the applicant was transferred to a regional special type prison.

On different dates later in the mid-1990s, five other criminal proceedings were brought against the applicant before various courts, with charges of disseminating separatist propaganda and incitement to hatred and hostility through the medium of his newspaper.

The cases against the applicant and his co-accused were subsequently joined before the initial regional security court.

Following promulgation of the Law no. 4210, which abolished the initial regional security court and established a subsequent regional security court, in a later year of the mid-1990s the subsequent regional security court acquired jurisdiction over the case and the case-file was sent to it.

In late November of a subsequent year, the subsequent regional security court convicted the applicant under Article 168 § 2 of the Criminal Code of membership of an illegal organization, namely the specified organization. The court found that two articles published in the local publication on a date in late 1994 and a date in early 1995 contained separatist propaganda and incitement to hatred. It further found it established that the applicant had participated in illegal demonstrations, put up posters, distributed leaflets supporting the specified organization and carried out activities in the local university for the recruitment of new members for the illegal organization. The court sentenced the applicant to a significant prison term.

In late 1999, a higher appeals court upheld the judgment from late November of the prior year in respect of the applicant.

In late May 2004 the applicant was conditionally released from prison.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a specific application number) against the Republic of Turkey lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a national, the applicant, in early 2004.

The applicant was represented by the applicant's lawyer, practising in Istanbul. The Turkish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent.

On a later date in the process, the President of the Second Section decided to give notice of the application to the Government. It was also decided to rule on the admissibility and merits of the application at the same time (Article 29 § 3).

THE FACTS

THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the late 1970s and lives in a certain city.

In early 1998, the applicant was arrested on suspicion of membership of an illegal armed organisation, namely the TIKB (the Revolutionary Communist Union of Turkey).

Shortly after the arrest, he was brought before the public prosecutor and then the investigating judge at the Istanbul State Security Court. The latter remanded him in custody on the same day.

A few days after that, the public prosecutor filed a bill of indictment with the Istanbul State Security Court, charging the applicant with membership of an illegal organisation under Article 168 § 2 of the Criminal Code.

Between early 1998 and early 2001, the court held nineteen hearings. During these hearings the Istanbul Security Court, relying on the state of the evidence, the nature of the offence, and the duration of his detention, refused to release the applicant.

In early 2001, the Istanbul State Security Court pronounced its judgment and convicted the applicant as charged and sentenced him to several years’ imprisonment under Article 168 of the Criminal Code.

Later that year, the Court of Cassation quashed this judgment. The case was accordingly remitted to the Istanbul State Security Court.

Shortly after the quashing, the Istanbul State Security Court resumed the trial. During the subsequent hearings, the court rejected the applicant’s requests for release, having regard to the state of the evidence.

At a hearing in mid-2003, the applicant’s lawyer once again requested the court to release the applicant. In her submissions, she also drew attention to the psychological state and treatment of the applicant. The court rejected this request. An appeal lodged in that connection was also dismissed by a further court later that same year.

By Law no. 5190, in mid-2004 State Security Courts were abolished. Subsequently, the Istanbul Assize Court acquired jurisdiction over the case.

In late 2004, the applicant’s lawyer requested the court to release the applicant. Shortly after that, the applicant was released pending trial.

According to the information made available to the Court by the parties to date, the case is apparently still pending before the Istanbul Assize Court.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case was referred to the tribunal on a date in early 1995 by the supervisory body (“the Commission“) and on a later date that month by the national authority (“the Government“), within a period of several months laid down by relevant provisions of the Convention. It originated in an application (a specific case number) against the national authority lodged with the supervisory body under a particular article on a date in late 1991 by a national of that country, the applicant. The supervisory body's request referred to other relevant articles and to the declaration whereby the national authority recognised the compulsory jurisdiction of the tribunal (another specific article); the national authority's application referred to a specific provision. The object of the request and of the application was to obtain a decision as to whether the facts of the case disclosed a breach by the national authority of its obligations under particular articles related to fundamental rights of the Convention.

In response to the enquiry made in accordance with a specific procedural rule, the applicant stated that he wished to take part in the proceedings and designated the legal representative who would represent him (another procedural rule).

The judicial panel to be constituted included ex officio an elected judicial officer of that nationality (a specific article of the Convention), and the Vice-President of the tribunal (a relevant procedural rule). On a date in mid-1995, in the presence of the court registrar, the President of the tribunal drew by lot the names of several other members, specifically the other members drawn by lot (other specific articles and rules).

The President of the judicial panel (a specific procedural rule), the Vice-President of the tribunal, through the court registrar, consulted the Agent of the national authority, the applicant's legal representative and the Delegate of the supervisory body on the organisation of the proceedings (relevant procedural rules). Pursuant to the order made in consequence, the court registrar received the national authority's memorial on a date in late July of that year and the applicant's memorial on a date in early August of that year.

In accordance with the President's decision, the hearing took place in public in the courthouse building in a specific European city on a date in late November of that year. The tribunal had held a preparatory meeting beforehand. There appeared before the tribunal: (a) for the national authority: a legal adviser from a government department, legal representatives, and a legal adviser from another government department; (b) for the supervisory body: the Delegate for the supervisory body; (c) for the applicant: legal representatives and an adviser. The tribunal heard addresses by the Delegate, the applicant's legal representative and a legal representative for the national authority.

Following deliberations on the following day, the judicial panel decided to relinquish jurisdiction forthwith in favour of an expanded tribunal (a particular procedural rule).

The expanded tribunal to be constituted included ex officio the President of the tribunal, the Vice-President of the tribunal, and other members and substitute judicial officers of the judicial panel which had relinquished jurisdiction (other procedural rules). On a date in early December of that year, in the presence of the court registrar, the President drew by lot the names of several additional judicial officers called on to complete the expanded tribunal, specifically the designated additional judicial officers (a further procedural rule).

Having taken note of the opinions of the Agent of the national authority, the Delegate of the supervisory body and the applicant, the expanded tribunal decided on a date in early 1996 that it was not necessary to hold a further hearing following the relinquishment of jurisdiction by the judicial panel (relevant procedural rules).

AS TO THE FACTS

I. CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

On a date in early 1990, the applicant became liable to pay a community charge of a specific sum. Since he did not pay it, on a date in late August of that year, a local magistrates' court ordered the issue of a liability order, entitling a local authority (“the charging authority“) to commence enforcement proceedings against him (see other relevant provisions).

The applicant did not pay the amount owed, and bailiffs visited his parents' house (where he was living), but were told that he had no goods of any value there or elsewhere which could be seized by them and sold in order to pay the debt.

Under a specific regulation of the Community Charge (Administration and Enforcement) Regulations of that time (“the Regulations“: see a referenced section), if a person is found to have insufficient goods on which to levy outstanding community charge the charging authority may apply to a magistrates' court for an order committing him to prison. On such an application being made, the court must inquire in the presence of the debtor as to his present means and also whether his failure to pay which led to the liability order being made was due to wilful refusal or culpable neglect. The charging authority applied for such an order, and on a date in early 1991 the applicant appeared at the local magistrates' court for the inquiry required by the Regulations. He was not assisted or represented by a legal representative, although he was eligible for “Green Form“ legal advice and assistance before the hearing (see a referenced section), and the magistrates could have made an order for Assistance by Way of Representation (“ABWOR“) if they had thought it necessary (see another referenced section).

The magistrates found that the applicant, who had several academic qualifications, had started a national employment training scheme in the autumn of a particular year, but had left it in the spring of the following year and had not worked since. He had applied for income support, but had been turned down because it is not payable to those who are voluntarily unemployed, and he had no personal assets or income. On the basis of this evidence, the magistrates concluded that his failure to pay the community charge was due to his culpable neglect, “as he clearly had the potential to earn money to discharge his obligation to pay“. Accordingly, they decided that he ought to be sent to prison for a short period unless he paid what was owing. The applicant was taken to a specific correctional facility on the same day.

Shortly after this, a legal representative went on the record as representing the applicant and lodged a notice of appeal by way of case stated (see a referenced section) and an application for bail pending appeal (see another referenced section). Legal aid was obtained for the appeal, but not for the bail application, because it is not available for such proceedings. In the event, the legal representative appeared without payment before the magistrates on the following day to apply for bail, but he was unsuccessful.

A few days later, the applicant's legal representative lodged an application for leave to apply for judicial review and for bail in a higher court. He was obliged to ask for judicial review, despite the fact that he had already lodged an appeal by way of case stated, because otherwise he could not have applied for bail in the higher court until the magistrates had stated a case (see a referenced section). Bail was granted the next day and the applicant was thus released from prison, having served a brief period.

A specific division of the higher court heard the appeal by way of case stated and the application for judicial review together on a couple of days in early October of that year. The applicant was represented and legally aided. The court noted that it had been necessary to apply for judicial review in order to get bail, but that the case stated procedure was more appropriate. Accordingly no order was made on the judicial review application.

A presiding judge in that division of the higher court held that the magistrates had been mistaken in concluding that the applicant's failure to pay the community charge had been due to culpable neglect: “In my view this finding was wrong on the evidence available to the justices. In certain circumstances a failure on the part of the debtor to work and put himself in funds to pay the community charge might constitute culpable neglect. In my judgment, however, before such a finding could be sustained, at the very least there would have to be clear evidence that gainful employment, for which he was fit, was on offer to the debtor and that he had rejected or refused that offer. There was no such evidence in this case. In my judgment, the justices' finding of culpable neglect cannot be sustained on the evidence adduced before them.“

In addition, he found that the decision to commit the applicant to prison would have been wrong even if there had been evidence of culpable neglect, because he did not have any means with which to pay the debt during the hearing before the magistrates, and because “[s]uch an order is only to be made if payment can be made and there is no other way of inducing the [debtor] to do so“. In the circumstances it was incumbent upon them to consider the alternatives to immediate detention provided for by the Regulations: they could have suspended the term of imprisonment subject to such conditions as they thought fit, or refused to issue a warrant, since the local authority could have renewed their application at an unspecified future time if the applicant's circumstances had changed (see a referenced section).

The applicant was not able to apply for compensation in respect of the period he spent in confinement, because he was unable to show bad faith on the part of the magistrates, as was required by a specific section of the Courts and Legal Services Act of that year (see a referenced section).

results.judged_text:
The case was brought before the international judicial body on a date in mid-July of a particular year by a human rights commission (“the Commission“) and early September of the same year by the government of the respondent national entity (“the government“), within a period of several months laid down by Article 32 para. 1 and Article 47 (art. 32-1, art. 47) of the Convention. The case originated in a specific application (identified by a case number) against the respondent country lodged with the Commission under Article 25 (art. 25) by the applicant, a citizen of a neighboring country of another national origin, on late November of an earlier year. The Commission’s request referred to Articles 44 and 48 (art. 44, art. 48) and to the declaration whereby the respondent country recognized the compulsory jurisdiction of the international judicial body (Article 46) (art. 46). The purpose of the request and of the application was to obtain a decision as to whether the facts of the case disclosed a breach by the respondent national entity of its obligations under Articles 9 and 14 of the Convention, Article 14 being taken together with Article 9 (art. 14+9) and with Article 1 of Protocol No. 1 (art. 14+P1-1).

In response to the enquiry made in accordance with Rule 33 para. 3 (d) of the Rules of the international judicial body, the applicant stated that he wished to take part in the proceedings and designated the legal representative who would represent him (Rule 30).

The judicial panel to be constituted included ex officio an elected judge of that country's nationality (Article 43 of the Convention) (art. 43), and the President of the international judicial body (Rule 21 para. 3 (b)). On late August of that same year the President of the international judicial body drew by lot, in the presence of the Registry official, the names of a number of other judicial figures, namely, Member A, Member B, Member C, Member D and Member E (Article 43 in fine of the Convention and Rule 21 para. 4) (art. 43).

The President of the judicial panel assumed the office of President of the judicial panel (Rule 21 para. 5) and, through the Registry official, consulted the designated representative of the government, the Delegate of the Commission and the legal representative for the applicant on the need for a written procedure (Rule 37 para. 1). He also granted a request from the applicant for legal aid (Rule 4 of the Addendum to the Rules of the international judicial body). Thereafter, in accordance with the Order of the President, the Registry official received the applicant’s and the government’s memorials on late February and early March of the subsequent year respectively; in a letter of early April of that same year a secretariat official for the Commission informed the Registry official that the Delegate would submit his observations at the hearing.

Having consulted, through the Registry official, those who would be appearing before the international judicial body, the President directed on mid-January of the subsequent year that the oral proceedings should open on late May of that year (Rule 38).

The hearing took place in public in a designated judicial building in a European city, on the appointed day. The judicial body had held a preparatory meeting beforehand. There appeared before the judicial body:

for the government the government’s lead representative, a high-ranking diplomat and a senior legal and consular official, the designated representative, a legal adviser from a government ministry, Legal Adviser, and another legal adviser from a government ministry, Legal Adviser, Advisers;

for the Commission the Commission’s Delegate, Delegate;

for the applicant the applicant’s legal representative, legal representative. The judicial body heard addresses by the government representative for the government, by the Commission’s delegate for the human rights commission and by the applicant’s legal representative for the applicant, as well as their replies to its questions. The applicant also made a short statement.

AS TO THE FACTS

I. PARTICULAR CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

A. General background

The applicant is a citizen of a neighboring country, of another national origin, born in the mid-1920s. In the late 1970s he was employed as a physician by a national railway company in a city in the respondent country. He rented a flat in the town, but spent the weekends with his family on an island in an archipelago in a northern European region at the southern end of a bay in northern Europe. From the early 1980s he worked as a physician in a governmental health service in another town in the respondent country. Since the mid-1980s he has been working in the archipelago.

During the period when the applicant was working in the respondent country his income from the above-mentioned posts was, in accordance with the convention between the respondent country and a neighboring Nordic country for the avoidance of double taxation (see paragraph 18 below), liable to tax of the respondent country. The applicant was allowed deductions for the cost of maintaining multiple residences as well as for travel expenses to and from the archipelago. As he was considered not to be permanently resident in the respondent country, he was, until a certain earlier year, taxed in a specific tax district (a local term) and he paid only a reduced municipal tax (see paragraph 19 below).

On the beginning of a certain year the law was amended, with the result that the applicant was no longer taxed in the specific tax district but in the municipality where he stayed, i.e. in the city (see paragraph 20 below). The deductions which had previously been allowed were no longer permitted and he now had to pay the full municipal tax, including a special tax to the established national church (“the religious community tax“ - see paragraphs 21-23 below). He was informed by the tax authorities that he could not claim any reduction of the religious community tax unless he was formally registered as resident in the respondent country (see paragraph 22 below).

B. The applicant’s appeal against the decision to tax him as a resident

The applicant appealed to a regional tax court (the local tax tribunal) against the decision to treat him for tax purposes as having been resident in that city in an early assessment period. He claimed that he should still be taxed in the specific tax district as he was not living in the respondent country. In a judgment of late February of a subsequent year the court dismissed the appeal.

In the meantime, on mid-February of that same year, the applicant had obtained a decision from the national tax authority (the central tax agency) to the effect that if he travelled regularly from the archipelago to his work in the respondent country he would not be regarded as a resident for the purposes of section 68 of the Municipal Tax Act of the late 1920s (kommunalskattelagen - see paragraph 20 below) and would thus be taxed in the specific tax district. However, he found regular commuting to that city was deemed unfeasible, and in a subsequent year he accordingly took on less responsible work with lower pay in the other town, to which he could just manage to commute from the island. As a result he was again taxed in the specific tax district and was not liable to the religious community tax.

An appeal by the applicant to a higher administrative court (the court of appeal) in a regional city against the regional tax court’s judgment was dismissed in a judgment of late October of that year and on mid-October of a later year the highest administrative court (the supreme administrative tribunal) refused an application by him for leave to appeal.

C. The applicant’s complaints regarding the obligation to pay full religious community tax

In addition to the above-mentioned proceedings, the applicant submitted an appeal to a local administrative court (the county court) in a specific county against the order to pay full religious community tax on his income from an early assessment period, on the ground that he was neither a member of the established national church, nor a citizen of the respondent country nor resident in the respondent country. In a judgment of mid-May of a later year the court dismissed the appeal, holding that a particular legislative act concerning dissenters’ tax liability (referred to as “the Dissenters Tax Act“ - see paragraph 22 below) did not apply to the applicant.

The applicant appealed to the higher administrative court in the regional city, which in a judgment of late October of that year confirmed the judgment of the lower court. His application for leave to appeal was refused by the highest administrative court on early October of a later year.

The applicant also lodged a complaint with the public complaints office (the national ombudsman office) concerning his obligation to pay tax for the religious activities of the established national church. In a decision of mid-April of that year the public complaints official noted that the requirement in the Dissenters Tax Act that a person be formally registered as resident in the respondent country (formally registered, as defined in a regulation concerning population records) in order to be able to apply for exemption from the religious community tax had been questioned on several occasions by various bodies, including the national legislature. Although the problem raised by the applicant was a limited one, the public complaints official concluded that it showed up an inconsistency in the tax legislation for which there was no objective justification and that it was understandable if this inconsistency caused irritation. In a letter of the same day to the government, he proposed that the registration requirement should be abolished. As a result of this proposal the Dissenters Tax Act was subsequently amended accordingly (see paragraph 23 below).

D. The religious community tax paid by the applicant

The applicant paid religious community tax in the amount of a certain sum in the national currency for the early assessment period, another sum for a subsequent assessment period and a third sum for yet another assessment period. If he had been allowed the reduction provided for in the Dissenters Tax Act, he would have had to pay smaller sums in the national currency, respectively (see paragraph 22 below).
results.final_text:
PROCEDURE

The case was brought before the Tribunal on a summer month of an unspecified early period by an international human rights body (“the human rights body“) and several weeks later in that period by the national administration of a Nordic country (“the national administration“), within a period of several months laid down by Article 32 para. 1 and Article 47 (art. 32-1, art. 47) of the Convention. The case originated in a specific application number against the Nordic country lodged with the human rights body under Article 25 (art. 25) by the applicant, a citizen of a Nordic country and of descent from a Western European nation, late in an autumn month in a preceding period. The human rights body’s request referred to Articles 44 and 48 (art. 44, art. 48) and to the declaration whereby the Nordic country recognised the compulsory jurisdiction of the Tribunal (Article 46) (art. 46). The purpose of the request and of the application was to obtain a decision as to whether the facts of the case disclosed a breach by the respondent state authority of its obligations under Articles 9 and 14 of the Convention, Article 14 being taken together with Article 9 (art. 14+9) and with Article 1 of Protocol No. 1 (art. 14+P1-1).

In response to the enquiry made in accordance with Rule 33 para. 3 (d) of the Rules of the Tribunal, the applicant stated that they wished to take part in the proceedings and designated the legal counsel who would represent them (Rule 30).

The Chamber to be constituted included ex officio a judicial officer, the elected judicial officer of that national origin (Article 43 of the Convention) (art. 43), and the presiding official of the Tribunal (Rule 21 para. 3 (b)). Late in a summer month of the initial period the presiding official of the Tribunal drew by lot, in the presence of the record keeper, the names of the other five members namely, an individual, another individual, a third individual, a fourth individual and a fifth individual (Article 43 in fine of the Convention and Rule 21 para. 4) (art. 43).

The presiding official assumed the office of Presiding Officer of the Chamber (Rule 21 para. 5) and, through the record keeper, consulted the Agent of the national administration, the representative of the human rights body and the legal counsel for the applicant on the need for a written procedure (Rule 37 para. 1). They also granted a request from the applicant for legal aid (Rule 4 of the Addendum to the Rules of the Tribunal). Thereafter, in accordance with the Presiding Officer’s Order, the record keeper received the applicant’s and the national administration’s memorials late in a winter month and early in the following spring month of the subsequent period respectively; in a communication of a few weeks later in that spring the administrative officer to the human rights body informed the record keeper that the representative would submit observations at the hearing.

Having consulted, through the record keeper, those who would be appearing before the Tribunal, the presiding official directed early in the new period that the oral proceedings should open late in spring of that same period (Rule 38).

The hearing took place in public in the Tribunal's premises in a major European city, on the appointed day. The Tribunal had held a preparatory meeting beforehand. There appeared before the Tribunal:

for the national administration its representative, an Ambassador and an Under-Secretary for Legal and Consular Affairs; a legal expert from a government department dealing with financial matters; and another legal expert from a government department dealing with foreign affairs, serving as advisers;

for the human rights body its representative;

for the applicant an advocate, serving as legal counsel. The Tribunal heard addresses by the national administration’s representative, by the human rights body’s representative and by the applicant’s advocate, as well as their replies to its questions. The applicant also made a short statement.

AS TO THE FACTS

I. PARTICULAR CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

A. General background

The applicant, an individual of national background, was born in the mid-1920s. In the late 1970s they were employed as a physician by a national transport company in a town in Sweden. They rented an accommodation in that town, but spent the weekends with their family on a remote island territory in an adjacent country within a specific sea region. From the early 1980s they worked as a physician in the public health service in another Swedish town. Since the mid-1980s they have been working in the island territory.

During the period when the applicant was working in Sweden their earnings from the above-mentioned posts were, in accordance with the convention between Sweden and Finland for the avoidance of double taxation (see paragraph 18 below), liable to Swedish tax. The applicant was allowed deductions for the cost of maintaining two homes as well as for travel expenses to and from the island territory. As they were considered not to be permanently resident in Sweden, they were, until a specific year in the late 1970s, taxed under a specific tax classification and paid only a reduced municipal tax (see paragraph 19 below).

Early in that specific year the law was amended, with the result that the applicant was no longer taxed under the specific tax classification but in the municipality where they stayed, i.e. in the initial town (see paragraph 20 below). The deductions which had previously been allowed were no longer permitted and they now had to pay the full municipal tax, including a special tax to the established church of Sweden (“the church tax“ - see paragraphs 21-23 below). They were informed by the tax authorities that they could not claim any reduction of the church tax unless they were formally registered as resident in Sweden (see paragraph 22 below).

B. The applicant’s appeal against the decision to tax them as a resident

The applicant appealed to a municipal tax tribunal against the decision to treat them for tax purposes as having been resident in the initial town late in the 1970s. They claimed that they should still be taxed under the specific tax classification as they were not living in Sweden. In a judgment late in a winter month of a particular period the tribunal dismissed the appeal.

In the meantime, mid-winter of the same period, the applicant had obtained a decision from the national tax authority to the effect that if they travelled daily from the island territory to their work in Sweden they would not be regarded as a resident for the purposes of a Municipal Tax Act from the late 1920s (kommunalskattelagen - see paragraph 20 below) and would thus be taxed under the specific tax classification. However, they found daily commuting to the initial town to be unfeasible, and in that same period they accordingly took on less responsible work with lower pay in another Swedish town, to which they could just manage to commute from the island territory. As a result they were again taxed under the specific tax classification and were not liable to the church tax.

An appeal by the applicant to the regional appellate body of a regional administrative center against the municipal tax tribunal’s judgment was dismissed in a judgment late in autumn of that period and mid-autumn of a later period the highest administrative court refused an application by them for leave to appeal.

C. The applicant’s complaints regarding the obligation to pay full church tax

In addition to the above-mentioned proceedings, the applicant submitted an appeal to a local administrative court of a specific county against the order to pay full church tax on their late 1970s earnings, on the ground that they were neither a member of the established church, nor a citizen of the said country nor resident in Sweden. In a judgment mid-spring of a specific period the court dismissed the appeal, holding that an Act from the mid-century period (lag 1951:691 om viss lindring i skattskyldigheten för den som icke tillhör svenska kyrkan, “the Dissenters Tax Act“ - see paragraph 22 below) did not apply to the applicant.

The applicant appealed to the regional appellate body of a regional administrative center, which in a judgment late in autumn of that period confirmed the judgment of the lower court. Their application for leave to appeal was refused by the highest administrative court early in autumn of a later period.

The applicant also lodged a complaint with the national ombudsman's office concerning their obligation to pay tax for the religious activities of the established church. In their decision mid-spring of that period the Ombudsman noted that the requirement in the Dissenters Tax Act that a person be formally registered as resident in Sweden (mantalsskriven, as defined in the Ordinance on the keeping of population records, folkbokföringsförordningen) in order to be able to apply for exemption from the church tax had been questioned on several occasions by various bodies, including the national legislature. Although the problem raised by the applicant was a limited one, the Ombudsman concluded that it showed up an inconsistency in the tax legislation for which there was no objective justification and that it was understandable if this inconsistency caused irritation. In a communication of the same day to the national administration, they proposed that the registration requirement should be abolished. As a result of this proposal the Dissenters Tax Act was subsequently amended accordingly (see paragraph 23 below).

D. The church tax paid by the applicant

The applicant paid church tax in the amount of a specific amount for a late 1970s tax year, a higher amount for a subsequent tax year and a similar amount for the year after that. If they had been allowed the reduction provided for in the Dissenters Tax Act, they would have had to pay a reduced sum, another reduced sum and a final reduced sum, respectively (see paragraph 22 below).

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The judicial proceeding began with an application against the respondent State, lodged with the European tribunal under Article 34 of the international human rights treaty, by a national of the respondent State, the individual, in early 1999.

The individual was represented by their legal counsel, practicing in the capital city. The respondent State did not designate an Agent for the purposes of the proceedings before the tribunal.

In early 2002, the tribunal declared the application partly inadmissible and decided to communicate the remaining complaints to the State. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the treaty, in late 2002 the tribunal decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The individual, who was born in the early 1970s, lives in the capital city.

The facts of the case, as submitted by the parties, may be summarised as follows.

In mid-November of that year, the individual was arrested and placed in the custody of law enforcement officials from a local security agency on suspicion of their involvement in the activities of an outlawed political group.

A week later, the individual gave a statement to law enforcement, allegedly under duress and confessed that they had been involved in certain illegal activities organised by the outlawed political group.

A few days later, the prosecution at the specialized national court questioned the individual in relation to their involvement in the outlawed political group. The individual denied the content of their written statement from a week prior and alleged that it had been taken under duress.

The following day, the individual was brought before a sole judicial officer of the specialized national court, who after taking their statement ordered their release.

Later that year, in late December, the prosecution at the specialized national court filed an indictment against the individual and fourteen other defendants, accusing them of membership of an illegal organisation. The prosecution requested that the individual be convicted and sentenced pursuant to Article 7 § 1 of the Prevention of Terrorism Act.

In early 1993, the specialized national court, composed of three judges including a judicial officer from the armed forces, convicted the individual as charged. The court held that there was sufficient and satisfactory evidence to disclose that they had committed the alleged offences. It accordingly sentenced them to a multi-year sentence of imprisonment. Both the individual and the prosecution appealed.

In early 1994, the highest appellate court quashed the judgment of the initial trial court on the ground that the individual should have been convicted under Article 168 § 2 of the Criminal Code on account of their membership to an illegal organisation.

A month later, the specialized national court, to which the legal matter had been remitted, held its first hearing and asked the accused persons to submit their final observations. A few months later, the individual submitted their final observations and denied the charges against them. The court held numerous additional hearings after this date, in search of one of the accused persons, who was on the run.

In late 1997, the specialized national court applied the decision of the highest appellate court and sentenced the individual to a lengthy custodial sentence of imprisonment under Article 168 § 2 of the Criminal Code

In late 1998, the highest appellate court dismissed the individual’s request for appeal.
results.final_text:
"""
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a specific case number) against the Republic of Turkey lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Turkish national, the applicant, in early 1999.

The applicant was represented by a legal representative, a lawyer practising in a major city in the country. The Turkish Government (“the Government”) did not designate an Agent for the purposes of the proceedings before the Court.

In early 2002 the Court declared the application partly inadmissible and decided to communicate the remaining complaints to the Government. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, later that year the Court decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant, who was born in the early 1970s, lives in a major city in the country.

The facts of the case, as submitted by the parties, may be summarised as follows.

On a particular date in November of a given year, the applicant was arrested and placed in police custody by police officers from the Security Directorate in that city on suspicion of his involvement in the activities of an illegal organization.

Several days later the applicant gave a statement to the police, allegedly under duress and confessed that he had been involved in certain illegal activities organized by an illegal organization.

Later that month the public prosecutor at the specialized court in that city questioned the applicant in relation to his involvement with the illegal organization. The applicant denied the content of his written statement given several days prior and alleged that it had been taken under duress.

The following day the applicant was brought before a single judge of the specialized court in that city, who after taking his statement ordered his release.

In late December of that year the public prosecutor at the specialized court in that city filed an indictment against the applicant and fourteen other defendants, accusing them of membership of an illegal organization. The prosecution requested that the applicant be convicted and sentenced pursuant to Article 7 § 1 of the Prevention of Terrorism Act.

In April of a subsequent year the specialized court in that city, composed of three judges including a military judge, convicted the applicant as charged. The court held that there was sufficient and satisfactory evidence to disclose that he had committed the alleged offences. It accordingly sentenced him to a period of imprisonment. Both the applicant and the public prosecutor appealed.

In early February of the next year the Court of Cassation quashed the judgment of the first instance court on the ground that the applicant should have been convicted under Article 168 § 2 of the Criminal Code on account of his membership to an illegal organization.

In mid-March of that year the specialized court in that city, to which the case had been remitted, held its first hearing and asked the accused persons to submit their final observations. In late May of the same year the applicant submitted his final observations and denied the charges against him. The court held numerous additional hearings after this date, in search of one of the accused persons, who was on the run.

In late November of a later year the specialized court in that city applied the decision of the Court of Cassation and sentenced the applicant to a lengthy term of imprisonment under Article 168 § 2 of the Criminal Code

In mid-September of the following year the Court of Cassation dismissed the applicant’s request for appeal.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a specific application number) against a Member State lodged with the judicial body under Article 34 of a key human rights treaty (referred to as “the Treaty”) by a national of that country, the applicant, in early May of that year.

The applicant was represented by a legal representative practising in a major city in the region. The national authorities (“the authorities”) were represented by their Agent.

In mid-February of a later year, the presiding officer of a judicial division decided to give notice of the application to the authorities. It was also decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility (Article 29 § 3).

THE FACTS

The applicant was born in the early 1960s and lives in a major city in the region.

In mid-May of a particular year, the applicant was arrested on suspicion of membership of a proscribed group. The same day, the applicant was examined by a medical professional, who reported that there was no sign of physical mistreatment on his body. The following day, he was questioned in police custody in the absence of a legal representative. In his police statement, the applicant admitted that he had had military training in a foreign country but rejected the allegation that he was a member of a proscribed group.

A few days later, the applicant was again medically examined by a medical professional, who stated that there were no traces of physical mistreatment on his body. Subsequently, on the same day, the applicant was brought before the prosecuting authority and the judicial officer handling the inquiry at a specialized court in that city, still in the absence of a legal representative. Before the state's legal representative, the applicant declared that he had been subjected to “mental duress” in police custody and refuted his police statement. He acknowledged that he had been to that foreign country but denied having had military training there. The applicant also gave a statement to the judicial officer handling the inquiry. He retracted his police statement but did not mention any allegations of physical mistreatment or duress. After the questioning was over, the judicial officer handling the inquiry remanded the applicant in custody.

In mid-July of that same year, the prosecuting authority at the specialized court filed an indictment with that court, charging the applicant with the offence of membership of a proscribed group, namely a specific proscribed group and its affiliated subgroup. A number of other persons were also included in the same indictment and accused of various offences, such as the assassination of a number of prominent public figures and intellectuals in the country. According to the prosecuting authority, the group's aim was to establish a religious regime in the country similar to that in that foreign country.

In early January of a subsequent year, the specialized court rendered its voluminous judgment. The lower court found it established, on the basis of the applicant's initial police custody statement, that he was a member of the proscribed group. It sentenced him to a significant period of imprisonment under Article 168 of the Criminal Code. In its decision the lower court also examined the applicant's allegation of mental duress in police custody. Having regard to the two medical reports drawn up at the time of the applicant's arrest and his release from police custody, which stated that there were no marks of injury on the applicant's body, the court concluded that the applicant had retracted his police custody statement in order to avoid conviction.

Towards the end of that subsequent year, the highest appellate judicial body upheld the applicant's conviction. The applicant was released from prison several years later, in the middle of that decade.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a particular case number) against the respondent State lodged with the international tribunal under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a national of that country, the individual who submitted the application, in late 2002.

The individual was represented by legal counsel practising in a particular regional city. The national government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent for the purposes of the proceedings before the international tribunal.

Early that year the international tribunal decided to give notice of the application. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The individual, a medical professional, was born in the late 1960s and lives in a particular regional city.

The individual was working at a regional public hospital when criminal proceedings were instigated against him for alleged involvement in the activities of an illegal organisation. He was accused together with other suspects of, inter alia, hanging posters and banners in various places in the capital city on behalf of that organisation. Throughout the proceedings the individual maintained his innocence. In the meantime, the regional public hospital suspended the individual pending the criminal proceedings.

In late 2001 a specialized court in the capital, on the basis of the evidence contained in the case file, convicted the individual under Article 7 § 1 of Law no. 3713 and sentenced him to a period of several years' imprisonment and to a fine. The individual appealed.

On an unspecified date a written opinion of a senior public prosecutor at the highest appeals court dated in late 2001 was submitted to that court. This opinion was not communicated to the individual.

In early 2002 the highest appeals court held a hearing and upheld the judgment of the first-instance court in respect of the individual.

The individual's request for a rectification of the highest appeals court's judgment was dismissed by a senior public prosecutor at that court.

The individual submitted that he had been released from prison in mid-2003.

By an additional judgment dated in mid-2004, a specialized court in the capital, taking into account the amendment to the definition of “terror acts” under section 7 of Law no. 3713 pursuant to Law no. 4928, acquitted the individual, who had not taken part in any violent activities, and nullified his conviction, together with all its consequences. This judgment became final shortly thereafter.

In the meantime, in early 2002, a high-level disciplinary body of the relevant government ministry decided to dismiss the individual from the civil service. The individual's objection to this decision was dismissed by the administrative tribunal who noted, inter alia, that, according to the disciplinary investigation, the individual had taken a short period of leave from mid-2000 in pursuit of his illegal activities. The tribunal also noted that the individual's conviction had become final. This decision was subsequently upheld by the highest administrative tribunal in late 2005. In his observations the individual submitted that, despite his acquittal, his request for a retrial had been rejected by the administrative tribunal. However, he did not submit any court decisions.

According to an attestation dated in late 2007 the individual is currently employed at a local medical facility in a particular regional city.
results.final_text:
PROCEEDING

The case originated in an application (a specific application number) against a signatory state lodged with the international human rights court under Article 34 of a relevant international human rights convention (“the convention”) by a national of that country, the claimant, in late 2002.

The claimant was represented by their legal representative, a legal professional practising in Samsun. The national government was represented by its representative in the proceedings before the international human rights court.

Early in a subsequent year, the international human rights court decided to give notice of the application. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the convention, it decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The claimant, a medical professional, was born in the late 1960s and lives in Samsun.

The claimant was working at a regional public hospital when criminal proceedings were instigated against him for alleged involvement in the activities of an illegal organisation. He was accused together with other suspects of, inter alia, hanging posters and banners in various places in Ankara on behalf of that organisation. Throughout the proceedings the claimant maintained his innocence. In the meantime, the regional public hospital suspended the claimant pending the criminal proceedings.

On a date in late 2001, a specialized court in Ankara, on the basis of the evidence contained in the case file, convicted the claimant under Article 7 § 1 of Law no. 3713 and sentenced him to a period of several years' imprisonment and to a fine. The claimant appealed.

On an unspecified date a written opinion of a senior prosecutor at the supreme appeals court dated a subsequent date in late 2001 was submitted to that court. This opinion was not communicated to the claimant.

Early in the following year, the supreme appeals court held a hearing and upheld the judgment of the initial trial court in respect of the claimant.

The claimant's request for a rectification of the supreme appeals court's judgment was dismissed by a senior prosecutor at that court.

The claimant submitted that he had been released from prison in mid-2003.

By an additional judgment dated in mid-2004, the specialized court in Ankara, taking into account the amendment to the definition of “terror acts” under section 7 of Law no. 3713 pursuant to Law no. 4928, acquitted the claimant, who had not taken part in any violent activities, and nullified his conviction, together with all its consequences. This judgment became final shortly thereafter.

In the meantime, in early 2002, the health ministry's disciplinary body decided to dismiss the claimant from the civil service. The claimant's objection to this decision was dismissed by a relevant administrative tribunal who noted, inter alia, that, according to the disciplinary investigation, the claimant had taken a short period of leave from around mid-2000 in pursuit of his illegal activities. The court also noted that the claimant's conviction had become final. This decision was subsequently upheld by the highest administrative court in late 2005. In his observations the claimant submitted that, despite his acquittal, his request for a retrial had been rejected by the administrative tribunal. However, he did not submit any court decisions.

According to an attestation dated a few years later, the claimant is currently employed at a local hospital in Samsun.
