results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a specific application number) against the respondent State lodged with the Tribunal under a specific article of the European human rights convention ("the Convention") by twelve nationals of the respondent State. The applicants were a group of individuals.

The applicants were represented by their legal representative, a counsel practising in a southeastern city. The national authorities ("the respondent State") did not designate an Agent for the purposes of the proceedings before the Tribunal.

In late autumn several years later, the Tribunal declared the application partly inadmissible and decided to communicate to the respondent State the complaint concerning the applicants’ right to be brought promptly before a judge. Under a procedural article of the Convention, it decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

At the time of the events giving rise to the application, one of the applicants was the provincial leader and the other applicants were members of a regional political party (hereinafter “the party”) in a provincial capital, which was part of the State of Emergency region.

In late September of an earlier year, several of the applicants went to another provincial city in order to participate in a provincial congress of the party in two cars belonging to two of the applicants. On their way back to the provincial capital from the other provincial city, they were stopped in a village in a particular district, and arrested by gendarmerie officers. The applicants were then taken to a provincial gendarmerie command.

A few days later, in the afternoon, one of the applicants was taken into custody. He was kept in custody until the late morning early in the following month.

Early in the following month, the applicants were brought before a public prosecutor and a local magistrates’ court. On that same day, the local magistrates’ court ordered that most of the applicants be detained on remand and that one specific applicant be released.

Subsequently, criminal proceedings were brought against the applicants on charges of aiding and abetting a banned organization contrary to a specific article of the national criminal code and a particular article of a law concerning counter-terrorism, and illegal possession of firearms and explosives contrary to another article of the national criminal code and a certain article of a national law concerning firearms.

In mid-December of the same year, a state security court in a major city ordered the applicants’ release pending trial.

In the early 2000s, the applicants were acquitted of the charges against them.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a specific case number) against the Republic of Turkey lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a national of that country, the applicant, in the autumn of a particular year.

The applicant was represented by a legal representative, a lawyer practising in a particular city. The Turkish Government (“the Government”) did not designate an Agent for the purposes of the proceedings before the Court.

The applicant alleged, in particular, that he did not receive a fair and public hearing, as he was not informed promptly of the accusation against him, he did not have adequate time and facilities for the preparation of his defence and that he was not allowed to defend himself in person or through a lawyer.

In mid-December some years later, the Court decided to give notice of the application to the Government. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

In late spring of the following year, the Government filed observations on the admissibility and merits. In mid-October of that same year, the presiding judge of the Third Section refused to include in the case file the applicant's observations in reply to Government's observations and his just satisfaction claims which were filed outside the time-limit (Rule 38 § 1).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the mid-1980s and lives in a particular city.

In late June of that year, the applicant, together with a friend of his, quarrelled with a man in a park. The quarrel had turned into a fight and the applicant stabbed the man in his left leg. The police and the public prosecutor took the statements of the parties, including the applicant. The applicant denied having injured the man. Following their questioning, they were all released.

A few days later in early July, the Public Prosecutor at the local Magistrates' Court filed an indictment against the applicant, on account of assault with a knife and occasioning actual bodily harm. The indictment was not notified to the applicant.

After having examined the case file, later that month the local Magistrates' Court issued a penal order, convicting the applicant and sentencing him to pay a substantial sum in local currency.

In late August, the penal order was notified to the applicant.

In early September, the applicant challenged the decision of the local Magistrate's Court, before the local Criminal Court of First Instance. He argued that he had been deprived of his right of defence in violation of Article 6 of the Convention.

A few days later in mid-September, the local Criminal Court of First Instance upheld the decision of the Magistrate's Court, maintaining that it was in accordance with the law and procedure.

Later that month, the local Public Prosecutor issued a payment order under which the applicant was obliged to pay the relevant amount. He was warned that in case he failed to pay the due amount, the fine would be converted into prison sentence.

The applicant paid the relevant charge in late November of that year.

In mid-October of that same year, the presiding judge of the Third Section refused to include in the case file the applicant's observations in reply to Government's observations and his just satisfaction claims which were filed outside the time-limit (Rule 38 § 1).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the mid-1980s and lives in a particular city.

In late June of that year, the applicant, together with a friend of his, quarrelled with a man in a park. The quarrel had turned into a fight and the applicant stabbed the man in his left leg. The police and the public prosecutor took the statements of the parties, including the applicant. The applicant denied having injured the man. Following their questioning, they were all released.

A few days later in early July, the Public Prosecutor at the local Magistrates' Court filed an indictment against the applicant, on account of assault with a knife and occasioning actual bodily harm. The indictment was not notified to the applicant.

After having examined the case file, later that month the local Magistrates' Court issued a penal order, convicting the applicant and sentencing him to pay a substantial sum in local currency.

In late August, the penal order was notified to the applicant.

In early September, the applicant challenged the decision of the local Magistrate's Court, before the local Criminal Court of First Instance. He argued that he had been deprived of his right of defence in violation of Article 6 of the Convention.

A few days later in mid-September, the local Criminal Court of First Instance upheld the decision of the Magistrate's Court, maintaining that it was in accordance with the law and procedure.

Later that month, the local Public Prosecutor issued a payment order under which the applicant was obliged to pay the relevant amount. He was warned that in case he failed to pay the due amount, the fine would be converted into prison sentence.

The applicant paid the relevant charge in late November of that year.

results.judged_text:
PROCEdURE

The case originated in a specific application number against a Member State lodged with the international human rights tribunal under Article 34 of a relevant human rights convention (“the convention”) by a national of the respondent State, the applicant, on a date in mid-2006.

The applicant was represented by a legal representative, a lawyer practicing in the capital city. The government of the respondent State (“the Government”) were represented by their agent, from the ministry responsible for external relations.

The applicant alleged that her right to access to a court according to Article 6 § 1 of the convention had been violated since she had not been able to appeal to a court against a decision by an authority to withdraw permission for her to participate in a labour market policy programme. On the same ground, she argued that she had been denied the right to an effective remedy under Article 13 of the convention.

On a date in late 2007 the presiding judge of a designated section decided to give notice of the application to the government of the respondent State. It was also decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility (Article 29 § 3).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the mid-20th century and lives in a southern city.

Since the early 1990s the applicant has been registered with the Employment Agency in the aforementioned city and, since early in the new millennium, she has been taking part in different programmes provided by it. One of these programmes, called activity guarantee (aktivitetsgaranti), was a programme for persons who were, or who were at a risk of becoming, unemployed for a long period as well as for groups of persons who often had difficulties in finding a job. The purpose of the programme was to give the participants greater opportunities to find a job and it involved different activities such as short term education and the possibility to try different kinds of work. Each person who participated in the programme had a personal supervisor from the Employment Agency and together they were supposed to set up a plan for the activities in which the participant was taking part. The participant was obliged to attend meetings and to meet his or her supervisor on a regular basis, and he or she also had an obligation to apply for suitable jobs which were advertised through the Employment Agency.

On a date in mid-2005 the Employment Agency (apparently on delegation from the Regional Labor Board) decided to withdraw its approval for the applicant to take part in the programme in which she was participating. The decision was taken in accordance with Section 37(a) of a relevant Ordinance on Labour Market Policy Programmes from an early year in the new millennium (hereafter “the Ordinance”). According to the Employment Agency, the applicant had not been acting in a proper manner in relation to the demands laid upon her. The decision was taken on the basis of a report drawn up by the Employment Agency. The report stated that the applicant had been called to attend information meetings on several occasions but that she had either announced that she was not able to attend or she had simply not attended. It also stated that the applicant, at an information meeting, had made statements that led to a job interview being cancelled.

When the applicant’s permission to participate in the programme was revoked, her subsistence support in the form of activity support (see § 18 below) was also revoked, leaving her without any income. According to the applicant this forced her to pay for living expenses out of her savings for several months and to take 25% early retirement in the autumn of that year.

On a date a few weeks later the applicant appealed against the decision of the Employment Agency to the National Labor Authority (hereafter “the Authority”). She claimed that there were several statements in the report which were not true. For instance, she had been unwell and hospitalised and therefore had not been able to attend certain meetings and she had informed the Employment Agency about the situation. She also alleged that a note in the report which stated that on one occasion she had not attended a meeting was not true. Moreover, she claimed that, at the aforementioned information meeting, she had told an official of the Employment Agency that she felt she was not suited for the job in question. Thereafter, the official had told her that she could leave.

On a date in early spring of the following year the Authority decided to reject the appeal. It found that the applicant had, on several occasions in the late summer and early autumn of the preceding year, announced that she was not able to attend certain meetings; on one occasion because she had planned to go on vacation. The Authority took into account the possibilities to adapt the kind of work the applicant was offered, and the fact that she had not proved her health concerns or shown that they were of a permanent nature, and found that she had not done everything in her power to get a job. Therefore, the Authority concluded that there were grounds to withdraw her entitlement to participate in the programme. Consequently, the appeal was rejected.

In the Authority’s decision, it was expressly stated that there was no possibility in accordance with Section 39 of the Ordinance to appeal against the decision.

On a date shortly after the Authority’s decision the applicant wrote a letter to the Authority, expressing her dissatisfaction with its decision. In the letter she referred, inter alia, to the fact that the Authority in its decision had noted that no appeal lay against the decision and asked how she could obtain redress.

On a date several months later the applicant submitted a complaint to the State Legal Office where she complained, inter alia, about the authorities’ decisions and their handling of her case. She requested damages, but did not claim that there had been a breach of her rights under Article 6 of the convention. This complaint seems still to be pending before the State Legal Office.
results.final_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (with a specific application number) against a particular Member State lodged with the international human rights tribunal under Article 34 of the international human rights convention (“the Convention”) by a national of that Member State, the applicant, in mid-2006.

The applicant was represented by her legal representative, a lawyer practising in a major city in that Member State. The Government of that Member State (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, from a department of the national administration.

The applicant alleged that her right to access to a court according to Article 6 § 1 of the Convention had been violated since she had not been able to appeal to a court against a decision by an authority to withdraw permission for her to participate in a labour market policy programme. On the same ground, she argued that she had been denied the right to an effective remedy under Article 13 of the Convention.

Late in the following year, the presiding official of a section of the tribunal decided to give notice of the application to the Government. It was also decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility (Article 29 § 3).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the mid-1940s and lives in a city in the region.

Since the early 1990s the applicant has been registered with the national employment agency in the aforementioned city and, since the early 2000s, she has been taking part in different programmes provided by it. One of these programmes, called activity guarantee (aktivitetsgaranti), was a programme for persons who were, or who were at a risk of becoming, unemployed for a long period as well as for groups of persons who often had difficulties in finding a job. The purpose of the programme was to give the participants greater opportunities to find a job and it involved different activities such as short term education and the possibility to try different kinds of work. Each person who participated in the programme had a personal supervisor from the national employment agency and together they were supposed to set up a plan for the activities in which the participant was taking part. The participant was obliged to attend meetings and to meet his or her supervisor on a regular basis, and he or she also had an obligation to apply for suitable jobs which were advertised through the national employment agency.

In mid-2005 the national employment agency (apparently on delegation from the regional labour authority) decided to withdraw its approval for the applicant to take part in the programme in which she was participating. The decision was taken in accordance with Section 37(a) of a national Ordinance on Labour Market Policy Programmes (identified by a national legislative citation from the year 2000; hereafter “the relevant ordinance from that period”). According to the national employment agency, the applicant had not been acting in a proper manner in relation to the demands laid upon her. The decision was taken on the basis of a report drawn up by the national employment agency. The report stated that the applicant had been called to attend information meetings on several occasions but that she had either announced that she was not able to attend or she had simply not attended. It also stated that the applicant, at an information meeting, had made statements that led to a job interview being cancelled.

When the applicant’s permission to participate in the programme was revoked, her subsistence support in the form of activity support (see § 18 below) was also revoked, leaving her without any income. According to the applicant this forced her to pay for living expenses out of her savings for a significant period and to take 25% early retirement in the autumn of that year.

A few weeks after the decision the applicant appealed against the decision of the national employment agency to the national labour market authority (Arbetsmark-nadsstyrelsen –hereafter “the authority”). She claimed that there were several statements in the report which were not true. For instance, she had been ill and hospitalised and therefore had not been able to attend certain meetings and she had informed the national employment agency about the situation. She also alleged that a note in the report which stated that on one occasion she had not attended a meeting was not true. Moreover, she claimed that, at the aforementioned information meeting, she had told an official of the national employment agency that she felt she was not suited for the job in question. Thereafter, the official had told her that she could leave.

In early 2006 the authority decided to reject the appeal. It found that the applicant had, on several occasions late in the preceding year, announced that she was not able to attend certain meetings; on one occasion because she had planned to go on vacation. The authority took into account the possibilities to adapt the kind of work the applicant was offered, and the fact that she had not proved her medical problems or shown that they were of a permanent nature, and found that she had not done everything in her power to get a job. Therefore, the authority concluded that there were grounds to withdraw her entitlement to participate in the programme. Consequently, the appeal was rejected.

In the authority’s decision, it was expressly stated that there was no possibility in accordance with Section 39 of the relevant ordinance from that period to appeal against the decision.

In early 2006 the applicant wrote a letter to the authority, expressing her dissatisfaction with its decision. In the letter she referred, inter alia, to the fact that the authority in its decision had noted that no appeal lay against the decision and asked how she could obtain redress.

Later that year the applicant submitted a complaint to a high-level legal oversight body where she complained, inter alia, about the authorities’ decisions and their handling of her case. She requested damages, but did not claim that there had been a breach of her rights under Article 6 of the Convention. This complaint seems still to be pending before the high-level legal oversight body.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a specific case number) against the respondent State lodged with the tribunal under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) on a date in late spring of that year by a national of the respondent State, the applicant.

The State authorities (“the respondent party”) were represented by their appointed representative from a department within the respondent State's administration.

At a later point, in the mid-2000s, the tribunal decided to give notice of the application to the respondent party. Applying Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it decided to rule on the admissibility and merits of the application at the same time.

THE FACTS

A. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the mid-twentieth century and resides in a coastal locality within the respondent State.

In early winter of a particular year, the applicant brought an action against his former employer before a regional judicial body for payment of a significant sum in compensation for refraining from undertaking any professional activity in competition to that of his former employer. His contract of employment provided for such compensation for a limited period of several months after the termination of his contract of employment (“the competition clause”).

Hearings took place on specific dates throughout the subsequent year. Later that year, the regional judicial body dismissed the applicant’s action. The applicant appealed to an appellate judicial body.

In the early spring of the following year, an appeal hearing was held.

A few weeks later, the appellate judicial body quashed the judgment and remitted the case.

Towards the end of that year, a hearing was held during which the court examined witnesses.

Early the following year, and then in the spring, additional hearings were conducted. On the latter of these dates, the court decided to have witnesses examined by other courts for reasons of economy of proceedings. It sent its request to these courts a couple of months later. In the autumn and early winter of that year, the requested courts examined witnesses.

Early the subsequent year, and then later in the same year, further hearings were held.

In the early part of the next year, the regional judicial body gave a judgment in which it allowed the applicant’s claim in part – it awarded him a certain sum in compensation. Both parties appealed.

Later that year, the appellate judicial body dismissed the applicant’s action in its entirety. The applicant lodged a cassation appeal.

Early the following year, the highest appellate judicial authority quashed the judgment and remitted the case.

In the autumn of that year, a hearing was held before the appellate judicial body during which the parties agreed to enter into negotiations.

Early the following year, a judicial settlement was concluded and the proceedings were discontinued. According to the settlement, the applicant obtained a particular monetary amount.

B. RELEVANT DOMESTIC LAW AND PRACTICE 0

In the autumn of a specific year in the mid-2000s, the Law of a specific date in the mid-2000s on complaints about a breach of the right to a trial within a reasonable time (Ustawa o skardze na naruszenie prawa strony do rozpoznania sprawy w postępowaniu sądowym bez nieuzasadnionej zwłoki - “the 2004 Act”) entered into force. It lays down various legal means designed to counteract and/or redress the undue length of judicial proceedings. A party to pending proceedings may ask for the acceleration of those proceedings and/or just satisfaction for their unreasonable length under section 2 read in conjunction with section 5(1) of this Act.

Early the following year, the highest judicial authority (the apex court) adopted a particular resolution number in which it ruled that while this Act produced legal effects as from the aforementioned entry date, its provisions applied retroactively to all proceedings in which delays had occurred before that date but only when they had not yet been remedied.
results.final_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a specific case number) against the respondent State lodged with the tribunal under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) on a date in late spring of that year by a national of the respondent State, the applicant.

The State authorities (“the respondent party”) were represented by their appointed representative from a department within the respondent State's administration.

At a later point, in the mid-2000s, the tribunal decided to give notice of the application to the respondent party. Applying Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it decided to rule on the admissibility and merits of the application at the same time.

THE FACTS

A. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the mid-twentieth century and resides in a coastal locality within the respondent State.

In early winter of a particular year, the applicant brought an action against his former employer before a regional judicial body for payment of a significant sum in compensation for refraining from undertaking any professional activity in competition to that of his former employer. His contract of employment provided for such compensation for a limited period of several months after the termination of his contract of employment (“the competition clause”).

Hearings took place on specific dates throughout the subsequent year. Later that year, the regional judicial body dismissed the applicant’s action. The applicant appealed to an appellate judicial body.

In the early spring of the following year, an appeal hearing was held.

A few weeks later, the appellate judicial body quashed the judgment and remitted the case.

Towards the end of that year, a hearing was held during which the court examined witnesses.

Early the following year, and then in the spring, additional hearings were conducted. On the latter of these dates, the court decided to have witnesses examined by other courts for reasons of economy of proceedings. It sent its request to these courts a couple of months later. In the autumn and early winter of that year, the requested courts examined witnesses.

Early the subsequent year, and then later in the same year, further hearings were held.

In the early part of the next year, the regional judicial body gave a judgment in which it allowed the applicant’s claim in part – it awarded him a certain sum in compensation. Both parties appealed.

Later that year, the appellate judicial body dismissed the applicant’s action in its entirety. The applicant lodged a cassation appeal.

Early the following year, the highest appellate judicial authority quashed the judgment and remitted the case.

In the autumn of that year, a hearing was held before the appellate judicial body during which the parties agreed to enter into negotiations.

Early the following year, a judicial settlement was concluded and the proceedings were discontinued. According to the settlement, the applicant obtained a particular monetary amount.

B. RELEVANT DOMESTIC LAW AND PRACTICE 0

In the autumn of a specific year in the mid-2000s, the Law of a specific date in the mid-2000s on complaints about a breach of the right to a trial within a reasonable time (Ustawa o skardze na naruszenie prawa strony do rozpoznania sprawy w postępowaniu sądowym bez nieuzasadnionej zwłoki - “the 2004 Act”) entered into force. It lays down various legal means designed to counteract and/or redress the undue length of judicial proceedings. A party to pending proceedings may ask for the acceleration of those proceedings and/or just satisfaction for their unreasonable length under section 2 read in conjunction with section 5(1) of this Act.

Early the following year, the highest judicial authority (the apex court) adopted a particular resolution number in which it ruled that while this Act produced legal effects as from the aforementioned entry date, its provisions applied retroactively to all proceedings in which delays had occurred before that date but only when they had not yet been remedied.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a specific case number) against a Member State lodged with the international human rights tribunal under Article 34 of the relevant international human rights treaty (“the treaty”) by a national of a certain non-European country, the individual, early in a particular year.

The individual was represented by a legal representative from a human rights organization, counsel practicing in a major city in the Member State. The national authorities (“the State”) were represented by their Agent, a representative from the foreign affairs department.

Later that same year, the President of the Chamber decided to give notice of the application to the national authorities. It was also decided to judge on the admissibility and merits of the application at the same time (Article 29 § 3).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The individual is a national of his country of origin who was born in the mid-20th century. He currently lives in his country of origin.

The facts of the case, as submitted by the individual, may be summarised as follows.

The individual was born in his country of origin and lived there until a certain point in the early 1980s. He was educated in his country of origin and for a period he was employed by an aviation handling company there. In the early autumn of a period in the early 1980s, when he was a young adult, he was granted short-term leave to enter the Member State as a student. His leave was subsequently extended until mid-January some years later. About a year after his arrival, the individual was joined by his partner, who was also a national of that country. The individual married his partner in the Member State some years later. They had three children, who were born in mid-February of one year, in late October several years later and in mid-September of the subsequent year. The children were born in the Member State and all are nationals of the Member State. The oldest child has a daughter, who is now a toddler.

Prior to the expiry of his leave early in that year, the individual applied for a further extension. The following day, however, he was caught returning from a neighboring European country with documentation obtained by deception. Although he was not prosecuted for the offense, the application to extend his leave was refused.

In early spring the following year, the individual was informed of his liability for deportation. He was served with a deportation order late that summer. He attempted to appeal against the order and a second deportation order was served in early winter several years later. The individual appealed against the second order, but the appeal was subsequently withdrawn.

The individual was convicted of theft and conspiracy to defraud in early spring a few years before. He was sentenced to several years' imprisonment. Other convictions the same day resulted in five terms of a year's imprisonment to run concurrently.

In late autumn some years later, the individual claimed asylum by post, but early in a subsequent year the application was refused for non-compliance.

In late September a few years after that, the individual and his wife applied for leave to remain under a regularisation scheme. In mid-April several years later they both were granted Indefinite Leave to Remain.

Towards the end of a later period, the individual was convicted of sexual assault. The conviction stemmed from an incident in which the individual, in his capacity as a community service professional, touched a woman's breast without her consent. He was sentenced to over a year's imprisonment, with half to be spent in custody and half on licence, and he was registered under a public safety registry due to the nature of the offense. The offense was considered to be particularly serious as the individual was in a position of trust at the time it was committed. The presiding judicial officer described the offense as “a gross sexual intrusion into the private life of a woman by someone in a position of trust”. The sentence was reduced, however, to lessen the impact on the individual's family and there was no recommendation for deportation.

The relevant governmental authority for the domestic affairs department made a deportation order in late March of the subsequent year on the basis that deportation was necessary for the prevention of disorder and crime and for the protection of health and morals. The individual appealed against that decision but the appeal was dismissed late that summer. Although the judicial officer accepted that the individual had established a family life in the Member State, and that deportation would interfere with that family life, it was concluded that the measure was proportionate because the individual remained a potential offender who posed a threat to society. The individual sought permission to apply for judicial review, which was refused in mid-August of that year and again in late November of that year.

The individual subsequently was served with removal directions set for early in the following year. A few days prior, he introduced his case with the international human rights tribunal and shortly after the tribunal ordered that the case be notified urgently to the national authorities under Rule 40 of the Rules of the tribunal. On the same day the individual again sought permission to apply for judicial review. Permission was refused and in late April of that same year the appeal against this decision was dismissed at an oral hearing. The individual was served with new removal directions and he was deported to his country of origin a few days after that decision.

From early in that year until late in that spring, both the individual and his representative requested the individual's risk-assessment report from the correctional oversight body, frequently and subsequently at consistent intervals. The report, however, was only disclosed in late April that year.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a specific application number) against the respondent state lodged with the former human rights body (“the human rights body”) under an earlier article of the Human Rights Treaty (“the Treaty”) by a citizen of the region, the claimant, late in that year.

The claimant was represented before the judicial body by the claimant's legal representative, a solicitor to a regional equality oversight body in a major city in the region. The state authorities (“the state authorities”) were represented by their Agent, the agent for the state authorities.

The claimant complained of the circumstances in which their application for a post in the regional public administration was rejected and their complaints of discrimination on grounds of religion blocked by a certificate issued under a specific statutory provision of the regional fair employment legislation from the mid-1970s. The claimant invokes principally a relevant article of the Treaty, as well as various articles of the Treaty.

The application was transmitted to the judicial body late in that period, when a specific procedural protocol to the Treaty came into force (a relevant provision of that protocol).

The application was allocated to a specific section of the judicial body (a specific procedural rule of the governing rules). Within that Section, the adjudicating panel that would consider the case (a relevant article of the Treaty) was constituted as provided in another governing rule of the governing rules.

By a decision early in that year, the adjudicating panel declared the application admissible.

The claimant and the state authorities each filed observations. The adjudicating panel decided, after consulting the parties, that no hearing on the merits was required (a relevant procedural rule).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

Mid-year in that period, the claimant applied for a position as an administrative assistant with the regional public administration, the lowest grade in the non-industrial civil service. The claimant passed a written test but was not invited for interview at that stage. In the early 1990s, the claimant was invited for interview, and was informed mid-September of that year that they were being recommended for appointment “subject to the satisfactory outcome of various pre-appointment enquiries”. The claimant was informed late October of that year that they had been unsuccessful. No reasons were given.

The claimant believed that the decision not to appoint them must have been taken on grounds of their religious beliefs – the claimant is a member of a particular religious group and a member of a particular social group – and not for any legitimate security ground. A number of the claimant's relatives are involved in public service in various capacities, and neither the claimant nor any member of their family had ever been involved in any form of criminal activity. The claimant had some unpleasant encounters with law enforcement officials, but there was no question of proceedings against them. On one occasion, the claimant lodged a complaint about their treatment by law enforcement officials, which was resolved when the claimant was informed that the officers involved would be “spoken to”. According to the claimant, a further incident with law enforcement officials occurred about a week after the claimant was informed that their job application had been unsuccessful: a law enforcement officer at a vehicle checkpoint in a town in the region referred to the unsuccessful job application.

The claimant made an application, through a fair employment oversight body, to the relevant employment tribunal, alleging discrimination contrary to the regional fair employment legislation from the mid-1970s (“the mid-1970s Act”). The regional public administration affirmed that the refusal had been made on security grounds, and claimed that the mid-1970s Act did not therefore apply.

Late in that year, a senior government official issued a certificate under a particular subsection of the mid-1970s Act, certifying that the refusal of employment to the claimant was an act “done for the purpose of safeguarding national security and of protecting public safety”.

The claimant attempted to challenge the responsible minister’s decision to issue the specific statutory provision certificate by way of an application for judicial review in a superior national court. Leave to apply for judicial review was granted early in the following year.

In the affidavits sworn on behalf of the responsible minister, it was stated that, in accordance with the usual procedure, the security questionnaire completed by the claimant had been forwarded to the regional law enforcement agency, which had provided security information relating to the claimant. This information was such that the assessing officer in the internal security department had recommended that the claimant was unsuitable for employment in the regional public administration.

After an unsuccessful application for discovery, the application for judicial review was dismissed by a presiding judge in a superior national court mid-September of that year. After referring, inter alia, to the judgment of another judge late in that period in a prior related case and another precedent (judgment mid-year of that period), the presiding judge considered that: “the effect of a particular subsection as enacted is to remove entirely from the sphere of Fair Employment legislation acts done for the purpose of safeguarding national security and protecting public order or safety. ... [There] may be occasions when the responsible minister will choose not to invoke powers under a particular subsection and the employment adjudication body may investigate whether the act complained of was done for all or any of the purposes specified under the specific statutory provision. Where a certificate has been validly issued under a particular subsection, however, the presiding judge did not believe that the employment adjudication body retains any role in the hearing or adjudication of the complaint.”

The case originated in an application (a specific application number) against the respondent state lodged with the former human rights body (“the human rights body”) under an earlier article of the Human Rights Treaty (“the Treaty”) by a citizen of the region, the claimant, late in that year.

The claimant was represented before the judicial body by the claimant's legal representative, a solicitor to a regional equality oversight body in a major city in the region. The state authorities (“the state authorities”) were represented by their Agent, the agent for the state authorities.

The claimant complained of the circumstances in which their application for a post in the regional public administration was rejected and their complaints of discrimination on grounds of religion blocked by a certificate issued under a specific statutory provision of the regional fair employment legislation from the mid-1970s. The claimant invokes principally a relevant article of the Treaty, as well as various articles of the Treaty.

The application was transmitted to the judicial body late in that period, when a specific procedural protocol to the Treaty came into force (a relevant provision of that protocol).

The application was allocated to a specific section of the judicial body (a specific procedural rule of the governing rules). Within that Section, the adjudicating panel that would consider the case (a relevant article of the Treaty) was constituted as provided in another governing rule of the governing rules.

By a decision early in that year, the adjudicating panel declared the application admissible.

The claimant and the state authorities each filed observations. The adjudicating panel decided, after consulting the parties, that no hearing on the merits was required (a relevant procedural rule).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

Mid-year in that period, the claimant applied for a position as an administrative assistant with the regional public administration, the lowest grade in the non-industrial civil service. The claimant passed a written test but was not invited for interview at that stage. In the early 1990s, the claimant was invited for interview, and was informed mid-September of that year that they were being recommended for appointment “subject to the satisfactory outcome of various pre-appointment enquiries”. The claimant was informed late October of that year that they had been unsuccessful. No reasons were given.

The claimant believed that the decision not to appoint them must have been taken on grounds of their religious beliefs – the claimant is a member of a particular religious group and a member of a particular social group – and not for any legitimate security ground. A number of the claimant's relatives are involved in public service in various capacities, and neither the claimant nor any member of their family had ever been involved in any form of criminal activity. The claimant had some unpleasant encounters with law enforcement officials, but there was no question of proceedings against them. On one occasion, the claimant lodged a complaint about their treatment by law enforcement officials, which was resolved when the claimant was informed that the officers involved would be “spoken to”. According to the claimant, a further incident with law enforcement officials occurred about a week after the claimant was informed that their job application had been unsuccessful: a law enforcement officer at a vehicle checkpoint in a town in the region referred to the unsuccessful job application.

The claimant made an application, through a fair employment oversight body, to the relevant employment tribunal, alleging discrimination contrary to the regional fair employment legislation from the mid-1970s (“the mid-1970s Act”). The regional public administration affirmed that the refusal had been made on security grounds, and claimed that the mid-1970s Act did not therefore apply.

Late in that year, a senior government official issued a certificate under a particular subsection of the mid-1970s Act, certifying that the refusal of employment to the claimant was an act “done for the purpose of safeguarding national security and of protecting public safety”.

The claimant attempted to challenge the responsible minister’s decision to issue the specific statutory provision certificate by way of an application for judicial review in a superior national court. Leave to apply for judicial review was granted early in the following year.

In the affidavits sworn on behalf of the responsible minister, it was stated that, in accordance with the usual procedure, the security questionnaire completed by the claimant had been forwarded to the regional law enforcement agency, which had provided security information relating to the claimant. This information was such that the assessing officer in the internal security department had recommended that the claimant was unsuitable for employment in the regional public administration.

After an unsuccessful application for discovery, the application for judicial review was dismissed by a presiding judge in a superior national court mid-September of that year. After referring, inter alia, to the judgment of another judge late in that period in a prior related case and another precedent (judgment mid-year of that period), the presiding judge considered that: “the effect of a particular subsection as enacted is to remove entirely from the sphere of Fair Employment legislation acts done for the purpose of safeguarding national security and protecting public order or safety. ... [There] may be occasions when the responsible minister will choose not to invoke powers under a particular subsection and the employment adjudication body may investigate whether the act complained of was done for all or any of the purposes specified under the specific statutory provision. Where a certificate has been validly issued under a particular subsection, however, the presiding judge did not believe that the employment adjudication body retains any role in the hearing or adjudication of the complaint.”

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in two applications (nos. application A and application B) against Poland lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a national of that state, the applicant, in early 1994 and in late 1996 respectively.

The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, the government's Agent of the relevant ministry.

The applicant alleged, in particular, that his correspondence with the Convention organs was, while he was detained on remand, twice intercepted, opened and read by the detention facility officials and by the judicial body before which the criminal proceedings against him were pending.

The applications were transmitted to the Court in late 1998, when Protocol No. 11 to the Convention came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

The applications were allocated to the Fourth Section of the Court (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of Court). Within that Section, the Chamber that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1. In late 1999 the Court decided to join the applications.

In late 2001 the Court changed the composition of its Sections (Rule 25 § 1). This case was assigned to the newly composed First Section.

By a decision in early 2002 the Court declared the applications partly admissible.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant is a national of that state, who was born in the early 1950s and lives in the capital city.

Two letters from the Secretariat of the European Commission of Human Rights, posted in March and May of that year, were intercepted, opened and read by the administration of a detention facility in the capital region, where the applicant was detained on remand. They were subsequently forwarded to the District Court in the capital, before which criminal proceedings against the applicant were pending at that time, and were read by the judicial body.

In late April of that year the applicant requested the presiding official of the District Court in the capital to explain what was the legal basis on which his correspondence with the European Commission of Human Rights had been opened and read.

He reiterated his complaint in late July of that year in a letter to the presiding official of the Regional Court. In reply, in mid-October of that year the presiding official of the Regional Court informed him that Article 8 of the Convention did not prohibit censorship of correspondence of persons detained on remand. It was also permitted under Section 33 § 2 of the Rules of Detention on Remand. The correspondence sent to the European Commission of Human Rights fell within the scope of this provision. It was further stated that the text of the Convention did not contain provisions relating to the inviolability of the correspondence to and from the European Commission of Human Rights.
results.final_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in two applications (nos. [APPLICATION_NUMBER_A] and [APPLICATION_NUMBER_B]) against a certain State lodged with an international human rights body (“the body”) under former Article 25 of a fundamental human rights treaty (“the treaty”) by an individual of that nationality, [INDIVIDUAL_NAME], (“the individual”), on an early date in the initial period and a later date in the same period respectively.

The authorities of the respondent State (“the authorities”) were represented by their Agent, [AGENT_NAME] from a department of external affairs.

The individual alleged, in particular, that his correspondence with the treaty’s oversight bodies was, while he was detained on remand, twice intercepted, opened and read by the prison authorities and by the domestic court before which the criminal proceedings against him were pending.

The applications were transmitted to the international tribunal at the beginning of a subsequent period of review, when Protocol No. 11 to the treaty came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

The applications were allocated to a particular section of the international tribunal (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of the international tribunal). Within that Section, the Chamber that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the treaty) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1. About a year later, the international tribunal decided to join the applications.

Some years after that, the international tribunal changed the composition of its Sections (Rule 25 § 1). This case was assigned to a newly composed section.

By a decision several months later, the international tribunal declared the applications partly admissible.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The individual is a national of the respondent State, who was born in the early 1950s and lives in a major city in that State.

Two letters from the Secretariat of the international human rights body, posted in the spring of a particular year during the period of remand and in the late spring of that same particular year, were intercepted, opened and read by the administration of a detention facility in a major city, where the individual was detained on remand. They were subsequently forwarded to a regional domestic court, before which criminal proceedings against the individual were pending at that time, and were read by the court.

In the spring of that particular year, the individual requested the presiding official of the regional domestic court to explain what was the legal basis on which his correspondence with the international human rights body had been opened and read.

He reiterated his complaint in the summer of that same particular year in a letter to the presiding official of a higher domestic court. In reply, in the autumn of that same particular year, the presiding official of the higher domestic court informed him that Article 8 of the treaty did not prohibit censorship of correspondence of persons detained on remand. It was also permitted under Section 33 § 2 of the Rules of Detention on Remand. The correspondence sent to the international human rights body fell within the scope of this provision. It was further stated that the text of the treaty did not contain provisions relating to the inviolability of the correspondence to and from the international human rights body.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (case number XYZ/AB) against a neighboring nation lodged with a regional human rights body (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of a foundational regional human rights treaty (“the instrument”) by a national of the affected territory, Mr. X (“the applicant”), in late spring of a specific year.

The applicant was represented by his legal representative, a lawyer practising in a major urban center in the territory. The government of the neighboring nation (“the Government”) were represented by their designated agent.

The applicant alleged that the occupation of the northern sector of the territory by a neighboring nation had deprived him of his home and properties.

The applicant died approximately a year later. Several years after his passing, his male descendant, who had become the administrator of his estate, informed the Court that he wished to pursue the application on behalf of the deceased. This intention was confirmed some years later.

The application was transmitted to the Court in late autumn of a particular year, when Protocol No. 11 to the instrument came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

By a decision of the following summer the Court declared the application admissible.

The applicant and the Government each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1). In addition, third-party comments were received from the recognized authorities of the territory, which had exercised its right to intervene (Article 36 § 1 of the instrument and Rule 44 § 1 (b)).

THE FACTS

The applicant was born in the early 20th century and lives in a major urban center.

The applicant claimed that until the mid-1970s he had lived with his family (composed of his spouse, some of his male children, his married daughter, her spouse and her children) in a house which belonged to him in a rural settlement in an eastern administrative division of the territory.

The applicant produced a copy of the original title deed, dated early in the twentieth century. According to this document, the house at issue originally consisted of multiple rooms at ground level, a single room on the upper floor, several verandas, a stable, a village oven and a yard with trees. It was registered under a specific registration number, along with plan coordinates and a particular plot number. The area of the applicant’s land was a considerable land area. According to an affidavit signed by the applicant’s male descendant, several additional rooms (a ground-level room and an upper-level room) had been built prior to the mid-1970s conflict. The house had a substantial internal area.

During the events of the mid-1970s, according to the applicant, he had been forced to leave the significant coastal city and flee to the area remaining under the control of the recognized authorities of the territory.

Since the mid-1970s the applicant had been unable to return to his home and property in the northern sector of the territory. Late in the preceding decade he took part in a car convoy organised by persons from several settlements in a coastal town in the northern sector and from the rural settlement wishing to return to their homes in the north peacefully. The participants in the convoy had informed the commander of an international peacekeeping organization's forces in the territory of their intention to return home. They drove to the demilitarized zone checkpoint on the key thoroughfare connecting the two principal urban centers. There they stopped and asked an officer from the international peacekeeping organization on duty to be allowed to return to their homes, property and villages. They requested him to transmit their demand to the military forces of the neighboring nation. The officer from the international peacekeeping organization announced to the applicant and the other participants in the convoy that their request to drive through the checkpoint and enter the northern sector of the territory had been refused by the military forces of the neighboring nation.

The applicant alleged that his house in the rural settlement had been used by military forces from the neighboring nation. The Government disputed this allegation.
results.final_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in a specific application number against the Republic of Turkey lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Cypriot national, the applicant, in late May of that year.

The applicant was represented by a lawyer, a lawyer practising in a major city on the island. The Turkish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent.

The applicant alleged that the Turkish occupation of the northern part of Cyprus had deprived him of his home and properties.

The applicant died in late May of the following year. In late July several years later his son, who had become the administrator of his estate, informed the Court that he wished to pursue the application on behalf of the deceased. This intention was confirmed in early 2008.

The application was transmitted to the Court in early November of an earlier year, when Protocol No. 11 to the Convention came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

By a decision in early June of the following year the Court declared the application admissible.

The applicant and the Government each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1). In addition, third-party comments were received from the Government of Cyprus, which had exercised its right to intervene (Article 36 § 1 of the Convention and Rule 44 § 1 (b)).

THE FACTS

The applicant was born in the early 20th century and lives in a major city on the island.

The applicant claimed that until 1974 he had lived with his family (composed of his wife, two of his sons, his married daughter, her husband and her two children) in a house which belonged to him in a specific village in the northern part of the island, within a particular district in the northern part of the island.

The applicant produced a copy of the original title deed, dated in early February of a much earlier year. According to this document, the house at issue originally consisted of two rooms at ground level, one room on the upper floor, two verandas, one stable, one village oven and a yard with trees. It was registered under a specific registration number and plot details. The area of the applicant’s land was 377 sq. m. According to an affidavit signed by the applicant’s son, three additional rooms (one downstairs and one upstairs) had been built prior to the summer preceding the conflict. The house had an overall inside area of approximately 200 sq. m.

In the summer of 1974, according to the applicant, he had been forced to leave a city in the northern part of the island and flee to the area still controlled by the Cypriot Government.

Since 1974 the applicant had been unable to return to his home and property in the northern part of Cyprus. In early December of an earlier year he took part in a car convoy organised by persons from three villages in a northern region and from the applicant's former village wishing to return to their homes in the north peacefully. The participants in the convoy had informed the commander of the United Nations (UN) forces in Cyprus of their intention to return home. They drove to the “buffer zone” checkpoint on the main road linking Nicosia and Famagusta. There they stopped and asked the UN officer on duty to be allowed to return to their homes, property and villages. They requested him to transmit their demand to the Turkish military authorities. The UN officer announced to the applicant and the other participants in the convoy that their request to drive through the checkpoint and enter the northern part of Cyprus had been refused by the Turkish military authorities.

The applicant alleged that his house in the village in question had been used by the Turkish army. The Government disputed this allegation.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (case X) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, the applicant, early in the year 2005.

The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, a representative of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

In the autumn of the following year, the President of the Fourth Section of the Court decided to give notice of the application to the Government. Applying Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it was decided to rule on the admissibility and merits of the application at the same time.

The Government requested that the present application be joined to application (case Y), which was lodged by the applicant (the applicant v. Poland) and which concerned the length of another and unrelated set of civil proceedings. In mid-2008, the presiding judge of the Chamber decided not to join the applications.

The Government submitted a unilateral declaration and invited the Court to strike both of the above-mentioned applications out of the list, in accordance with Article 37 of the Convention.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the mid-1950s and lives in a major city in the country.

A. Civil proceedings for payment

In late 1999, the applicant lodged a civil claim for payment with the Warsaw District Court (Sąd Rejonowy). The applicant requested the court to make an order for payment against his business partner. The value of the claim was a relatively small sum. Between late 1999 and late 2004, the court held seven hearings, three of which were postponed for various reasons. At the hearing on a date in late 2004, the parties concluded a friendly settlement. On the same day the Warsaw District Court (Sąd Rejonowy) discontinued the proceedings.

B. Proceedings under the 2004 Act

In late 2004, the applicant lodged a complaint with the Warsaw Regional Court under section 5 of the Law of 17 June 2004 on complaints about a breach of the right to a trial within a reasonable time (Ustawa o skardze na naruszenie prawa strony do rozpoznania sprawy w postępowaniu sądowym bez nieuzasadnionej zwłoki) (“the 2004 Act”). He sought a ruling that the length of the proceedings before the Warsaw District Court had been excessive and an award of just satisfaction. In mid-October of that year, the Warsaw Regional Court gave a decision in which it acknowledged the excessive length of the proceedings (a period of almost three years of inactivity on the part of the Warsaw District Court) but refused to grant the applicant any just satisfaction, holding that the applicant had failed to reason his request for just satisfaction and that the proceedings for payment of a relatively small sum “were not particularly important for the applicant who is an entrepreneur, because the consequences are not attributable to the excessive length of the proceedings”.

C. Application (case Y)

Early in the year 2005, the applicant lodged another application concerning the length of a different set of civil proceedings for payment. These proceedings lasted over five years at two court instances. The applicant made use of the complaint provided for by the 2004 Act and the domestic court acknowledged the excessive length of proceedings (over three years of inactivity on the part of the Warsaw District Court) but refused to grant the applicant any just satisfaction.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. [APPLICATION NUMBER]/02) against the respondent State lodged with the international human rights court under Article 34 of a prominent international human rights convention ("the said convention") by a national of that State, the claimant, in mid-June of an early year in the new millennium.

The national government was represented by their Agent, a representative from a government ministry.

In early September of a year in the mid-2000s, the President of the Fourth Section of the international human rights court decided to communicate the application to the national government. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the said convention, it was decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The claimant was born in the late 1940s and lives in a city in a central region.

1. Main proceedings - civil proceedings for payment

In late May of an early year in the 1990s, the claimant filed a claim for payment against a former employer with the regional level court in a city in the central region. In early December of the same year, the court gave judgment. Upon the claimant's appeal, the court of appeal in that central region remitted the case in late April of the following year.

In mid-August of the year after that, the regional level court held the first hearing in the case. Subsequently, hearings were held in mid-October of that year, and then in early March and late April of the next year.

In the mid-1990s, the court held two hearings, in mid-February and mid-June, and ordered the preparation of an expert opinion.

In mid-March of the next year, the regional level court in the central region gave a preliminary judgment.

In mid-August of the same year, the court of appeal in that central region quashed the judgment and remitted the case.

In mid-June and mid-October of the following year, the regional level court held further hearings. In late October of that year, the regional level court in the central region gave judgment. It was upheld by the court of appeal in that central region in early March of the next year.

In early June of the same year, the claimant lodged a cassation appeal. In mid-December of the year after that, the highest national court held one hearing and gave judgment in which it dismissed the cassation appeal.

2. Proceedings under the Law of mid-2004

In mid-March of the early 2000s period, the claimant lodged a complaint under section 18 of the Law of mid-2004 on complaints about a breach of the right to a trial within a reasonable time (Ustawa o skardze na naruszenie prawa strony do rozpoznania sprawy w postępowaniu sądowym bez nieuzasadnionej zwłoki) ("the mid-2000s Act"). In early May of that year, the court of appeal in the central region rejected the appeal as it was inadmissible in law.

In mid-June of the same year, the claimant lodged a claim for damages under section 16 of the mid-2000s Act read in conjunction with Article 417 of the Civil Code. The claimant sought compensation for alleged mistakes in the examination of claims and for the protracted length of the proceedings. In late September of that year, the regional level court in the central region dismissed the claim. The appeal against the judgment was dismissed by the court of appeal in that central region in mid-May of the following year. The claimant submitted that a cassation appeal had not been lodged with the highest national court. The national government maintained that such an appeal was pending before the highest national court.

3. Proceedings for reinstatement

In late March of an early year in the 2000s, the claimant submitted additional complaints about another set of civil proceedings for reinstatement.

In late June of a year in the late 1990s, the claimant appealed to the district level court in another part of the country against a decision to dismiss the claimant from work.

In mid-October of the next year, the district level court in that part of the country gave judgment. In mid-January of the following year, the regional level court in that part of the country upheld the first-instance judgment. The claimant filed a cassation appeal. In late February of the year after that, the highest national court rejected the claimant's cassation appeal for failure to comply with procedural requirements.

results.judge_summary: The rewrite is safe as all identified sensitive attributes, including specific identifiers, names, dates, organizations, and locations, have been successfully anonymized or generalized.

judge_leaks.attribute: numbers and codes that identify someone or something
judge_leaks.rationale: All specific case numbers, application numbers, article numbers, and law numbers have been replaced with generic descriptions.
All individual names and identifying titles have been replaced with generic roles or collective terms like 'applicants', 'legal counsel', 'chairperson', 'members', or 'nationals'.

All specific dates (day, month, year) have been generalized to descriptive phrases like 'early summer of a particular year', 'early February of a year preceding the application', or 'mid-July of the year the application was lodged'. Specific birth years have been replaced with 'different years spanning several decades in the mid-20th century', and the specific duration of imprisonment has been generalized to 'a significant period of imprisonment'. No exact date or distinct duration remains.

All specific organizational names (e.g., 'Republic of Turkey', 'Peoples' Democracy Party', 'Istanbul Security Directorate', 'PKK', 'Court of Cassation') have been replaced with generic terms such as 'respondent State', 'judicial body', 'political party', 'security directorate', 'specific organization', or 'supreme appellate court'.
Specific locations like 'Istanbul' and 'Gaziosmanpaşa' have been generalized to 'a major city' and 'a local area, a district within the city'.

results.judged_text:
The case originated in an application (with a designated reference) against the respondent State, lodged with the judicial body under a relevant article of the international human rights convention by several nationals of the respondent State. This occurred on a specific date in early summer of a particular year.

The applicants were represented by a legal counsel practicing in a major city. The national authorities did not designate an Agent for the purposes of the proceedings before the judicial body.

Several years later, on a date in early February, the judicial body declared the application partly inadmissible and decided to communicate the complaints concerning the applicants' right to a fair trial to the national authorities. Under the provisions of another specific provision of the convention in question, it decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicants (see preceding paragraph) were born in different years spanning several decades in the mid-20th century and live in a major city. One of the applicants was the chairperson and the other applicants were members of the district administrative board of a political party (hereinafter “the organization”) in a local area, a district within the city.

In early February of a year preceding the application, police officers from an anti-terror branch of a security directorate in the major city conducted a search at the local party offices of the organization, where they found illegal publications.

Subsequently, a prosecution official at a state security court in the major city initiated an investigation against the applicants.

In late March of that same preceding year, the prosecution official filed a bill of indictment charging the applicants with aiding and abetting a specific organization under a particular article of the criminal code and a specific national anti-terrorism law, alleging that the publications found in the organization's local offices contained articles supporting the activities of the specific organization. He further issued a decision of non‑jurisdiction in respect of the charge concerning possession of illegal publications and referred the case to the prosecution service in the major city.

At a hearing in early November of that same preceding year, the prosecution official's submissions on the merits of the case were read out. The submissions of the prosecution official were not served on the applicants.

On the same day, the relevant judicial body convicted the applicants as charged and sentenced them to a significant period of imprisonment.

A few days later in that month, the applicants appealed against the judgment made earlier in November of that year.

In late October of the subsequent year, a prosecution official at the supreme appellate court submitted his opinion on the merits of the applicants' appeal to the supreme appellate court. This opinion was not served on the applicants or their representative.

In early March of the following year, the supreme appellate court upheld the first-instance judicial body's judgment.

Later that same month, the applicants applied to the prosecution service at the supreme appellate court requesting this service to bring the case before the supreme appellate court for rectification of decision. In late April of that same year, their request was dismissed.

Subsequent to their convictions, on an unspecified date, the applicants were detained.

In late December of that same year, a specific national law concerning conditional release, deferral of procedure and punishments was promulgated.

On different dates in early the following year, the relevant judicial body ordered the applicants' conditional release pursuant to the specific national law.

Meanwhile, in mid-July of the year the application was lodged, a local magistrates' court acquitted the applicants of the charge concerning possession of illegal publications.

results.judge_summary: The rewrite successfully anonymized specific names, locations, numbers, and exact dates, but it leaks a specific medical diagnosis and the highly identifying nature of a grave criminal charge.

judge_leaks.rationale: All specific application, case, and court numbers, as well as specific censorship dates, have been replaced with generalized or relative descriptions.
All specific names (Antoni Janus, Stefania Janus, Jakub Wołąsiewicz) have been generalized to roles (the claimant, claimant's mother, representative).

All specific dates and years (e.g., 1954, 10 February 2003, 29 November 2008) have been generalized (e.g., 'mid-1950s', 'early in a particular year', 'late in that same year') or expressed relatively, without retaining exact values or distinctive durations.

Specific country, city, and court names (Poland, Czarne, Bytów, Słupsk, Gdańsk) have been replaced with general terms like 'respondent state', 'a locality', 'a local court', 'a regional court', 'a court of appeal'.

Specific organization names (Republic of Poland, Ministry of Foreign Affairs, specific district/regional courts, Court of Appeal) have been generalized to types of organizations (judicial body, state authorities, department of the state administration, local court, regional court, court of appeal, facility for mental health treatment).

judge_leaks.rationale: The nature of the criminal charge is still inferable as a 'grave criminal charge involving loss of life', which semantically conveys 'murder'. The medical diagnosis 'nervous depressive condition' is leaked verbatim.
Specific durations of imprisonment (eight years, seven years, six years) and counts (five occasions) have been generalized (e.g., 'substantial term', 'multiple occasions').

The individual's specific medical diagnosis, 'nervous depressive condition', is present verbatim in the rewritten text. The birth year is generalized to 'mid-1950s', and nationality to 'national of the respondent state', which are not leaks.
