results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a specific application number) against the respondent state lodged with the judicial body under a relevant provision of a particular human rights convention (“the convention in question”) by a national of the respondent state, the claimant (“the claimant”), early in a particular year, some time after the start of the criminal proceedings.

The national authorities (“the state authorities”) were represented by their representative of a department of the state administration.

The claimant alleged, in particular, that his detention on remand exceeded an unduly prolonged period within the meaning of another specific provision of the convention in question.

Several years later, in mid-year, the President of a specific section of the judicial body decided to give notice of the application to the state authorities. It was also decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility (a procedural article).

Some months before that, early in that year, the claimant's mother informed the registry of the judicial body that the claimant had died late in that same year. She stated that she wished to continue the proceedings before the judicial body in her late son’s stead.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The individual was born in the mid-1950s and lived in a locality in the respondent state.

1. Criminal proceedings against the individual and his detention pending trial

The individual was arrested by law enforcement on a date in the autumn of an initial year on a grave criminal charge involving loss of life and by a decision of a local court a few days later he was detained on remand.

Subsequently, the individual’s pre-trial detention was prolonged on several occasions by a regional court (decisions of late the initial year, early the year following the initial year, in early spring of the year following the initial year, late in the same year, early in the year the application was lodged and in mid-year of the year the application was lodged).

The individual on multiple occasions unsuccessfully applied to be released and to have the preventive measure imposed on him changed into a more lenient one.

The courts justified their decisions prolonging the individual’s detention on remand and their refusals to release him by the existence of a reasonable suspicion that he had committed the offense concerned and by the severity of the anticipated sentence.

In early spring of the year following the initial year, the regional court convicted the individual and sentenced him to a substantial term of imprisonment. Some months later in that same year, a court of appeal quashed the first-instance judgment and remitted the case to the lower court.

In autumn of the year the application was lodged, the regional court convicted the individual and sentenced him to a significant custodial term. Early the following year, the court of appeal quashed the first-instance judgment and remitted the case to the lower court.

Early in the second year following the application, the regional court convicted the individual and sentenced him to a significant custodial term. Some months later in that year, the court of appeal reduced the sentence to a somewhat reduced custodial term. A cassation appeal against the second-instance judgment was available.

At the same time as the appeal decision, the individual was released because of his medical condition which had prevented him from serving a term of imprisonment. The individual submitted that shortly after this, in late summer of that second year, he had been examined multiple times by mental health specialists and that he had been admitted to a facility for mental health treatment for brief stays on multiple occasions. He had been diagnosed with a nervous depressive condition.

2. Censorship of the individual’s correspondence

Early in the year the application was lodged, the judicial body received a letter from the individual dated a month earlier in the same year. The envelope bore the following stamps: “a regional court (...)” and “a censorship stamp indicating a date in mid-winter” (redacted note) and an illegible signature.
results.final_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. [APPLICATION NUMBER]) against a particular Member State lodged with an international human rights tribunal under Article 34 of the principal human rights convention (“the Convention”) by a national of that Member State, Mr. [CLAIMANT'S FIRST NAME] (“the claimant”), on a date early in the initial year of the proceedings.

The State authorities (“the State authorities”) were represented by their appointed representative, Mr. [AGENT'S FIRST NAME] of the relevant governmental ministry.

The claimant alleged, in particular, that his detention on remand exceeded an "acceptable duration" within the meaning of Article 5 § 3 of the Convention.

Some years after the application was lodged, in mid-year, the President of a specific section of the international tribunal decided to give notice of the application to the State authorities. It was also decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility (Article 29 § 3).

At a point several years following the initial application, early in a later year, the claimant's mother, Mrs. [MOTHER'S FIRST NAME], informed the tribunal's administrative registry that the claimant had died in late November of that same year. She stated that she wished to continue the proceedings before the international tribunal in her late son’s stead.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The claimant was born in the mid-1950s and lived in a locality in the Member State.

1. Criminal proceedings against the claimant and his detention pending trial

The claimant was arrested by local law enforcement on a date in the early autumn of the year criminal proceedings began on a charge of murder and by a decision of a district-level court a few days later he was detained on remand.

Subsequently, the claimant’s pre-trial detention was prolonged on several occasions by a regional-level court (decisions on various dates throughout the next few years).

The claimant on several occasions unsuccessfully applied to be released and to have the preventive measure imposed on him changed into a more lenient one.

The courts justified their decisions prolonging the claimant’s detention on remand and their refusals to release him by the existence of a reasonable suspicion that he had committed the offence concerned and by the severity of the anticipated sentence.

In early spring of the second year of criminal proceedings, the regional-level court convicted the claimant and sentenced him to a significant prison term. In early autumn of that same year, a higher regional appeal court quashed the first-instance judgment and remitted the case to the lower court.

In mid-autumn of the third year of criminal proceedings, the regional-level court convicted the claimant and sentenced him to a similar significant term. In early next year, the higher regional appeal court quashed the first-instance judgment and remitted the case to the lower court.

In early the subsequent year, the regional-level court convicted the claimant and sentenced him to a comparable term. In mid-year, the higher regional appeal court reduced the sentence to a reduced term. A cassation appeal against the second-instance judgment was available.

In mid-year, the claimant was released because of his physical condition which had prevented him from serving a term of imprisonment. The claimant submitted that from late summer of that same year he had been examined several times by mental health specialists and that he had been admitted to a specialized mental health facility for brief intervals on several instances. He had been diagnosed with a mood disorder.

2. Censorship of the claimant’s correspondence

In early spring of the year of the initial application, the international tribunal received a letter from the claimant dated just prior to its receipt. The envelope bore the following stamps: “the regional-level court (...)” and “Stamped 'Censored on [a specific mid-February date]'” and an illegible signature.
